topics.title: Basic Components of Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Components of Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired output behaviour. The **basic components** of a control system are the fundamental building blocks that together form a complete control loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCECT503 Module 1):**
> *"A control system consists of interconnected components that manage, command, direct, or regulate the behaviour of other devices or systems to achieve a desired objective. The basic components include: Reference Input, Comparator, Controller, Actuator, Plant/Process, Sensor/Feedback Element, and Controlled Output."*

### 1.2 The Seven Basic Components

| # | Component | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Reference Input (r)** | The desired value of the output that the system should achieve. |
| 2 | **Comparator / Error Detector** | Subtracts the feedback signal from the reference to produce the error signal. |
| 3 | **Controller (C)** | Processes the error signal and generates a control action. |
| 4 | **Actuator (A)** | Converts the control signal into a physical action (energy input to the plant). |
| 5 | **Plant / Process (P)** | The system or process being controlled (e.g., motor, furnace, robot arm). |
| 6 | **Sensor / Feedback Element (H)** | Measures the actual output and converts it to a comparable signal. |
| 7 | **Disturbance (d)** | An unwanted input signal that adversely affects the output. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Thermostat

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: The Shower-Mixer Analogy**
> Imagine you are taking a shower. You set the knob to "warm" (this is your **Reference Input**). Your skin (acting as a **Sensor**) detects the actual water temperature. If the water is too hot or too cold, your brain (the **Controller**) sends signals to your hand to turn the **Actuator** (the shower knob). The **Plant** is the water-heating system, and the **Disturbance** is when someone else flushes the toilet, suddenly changing the temperature. Your hand keeps adjusting — this is a **Closed-Loop Feedback System** in daily life!

### 1.4 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop (Foundation Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A control system is classified as **Open-Loop** if the control action is independent of the output. It is **Closed-Loop (Feedback)** if the control action depends on the output through a feedback path.

**Key contrast:**

- **Open-Loop Example:** A toaster — it heats for a fixed time regardless of the actual toast colour.
- **Closed-Loop Example:** A room air-conditioner with a thermostat — it shuts off once the room reaches the set temperature.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Gain (K):** A dimensionless ratio — *Output / Input* — measuring amplification.
- **Transfer Function (G(s)):** Laplace-domain ratio — Output / Input, assuming zero initial conditions.
- **Time (t):** Measured in **seconds (s)** in SI units.
- **Frequency (ω):** Measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Walkthrough of a Closed-Loop System

The closed-loop control sequence proceeds in **seven structured steps**:

1. **Reference Input (r(t))** — the user defines the desired output, e.g., a temperature of 25 °C.
2. **Error Computation** — the comparator subtracts feedback from reference: $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
3. **Controller Action (C)** — applies a control law, e.g., PID: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$.
4. **Actuation (A)** — the actuator, e.g., a motor driver, amplifies the controller signal to drive the plant.
5. **Plant Response (P)** — the plant produces an output $y(t)$.
6. **Disturbance Injection (d)** — external noise affects the plant.
7. **Feedback Sensing (H)** — the sensor measures $y(t)$ and sends $b(t) = H \cdot y(t)$ back to the comparator.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why"** behind feedback: Feedback reduces sensitivity to parameter variations, rejects disturbances, and improves steady-state accuracy. The **"How"**: By continuously comparing the desired and actual output, the system self-corrects.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Domain |
|--------------------|----------------|--------|
| $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ | Error signal | Time |
| $b(t) = H(s) \cdot Y(s)$ | Feedback signal | Laplace |
| $U(s) = C(s) \cdot E(s)$ | Controller output | Laplace |
| $Y(s) = P(s) \cdot [U(s) + D(s)]$ | Plant output with disturbance | Laplace |
| $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Transfer Function of a system | Laplace |
| $T(s) = \dfrac{C(s) P(s)}{1 + C(s) P(s) H(s)}$ | Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Unity feedback, $H=1$) | Laplace |
| $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + C(s) P(s) H(s)}$ | Error Transfer Function | Laplace |
| $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{P(s)}{1 + C(s) P(s) H(s)}$ | Disturbance-to-Output Transfer Function | Laplace |
| $K_p, K_i, K_d$ | Proportional, Integral, Derivative gains | — |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural frequency | rad/s |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$, students often miss the **negative feedback sign** — when the comparator subtracts, the denominator becomes $1 + G(s)H(s)$ for a positive feedback comparator. For the standard negative feedback configuration, the denominator is $1 + G(s)H(s)$. Always draw the comparator sign first.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Components Used |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Automotive** | Cruise control | Speed sensor + ECU + Throttle actuator |
| **Aerospace** | Autopilot | Gyro sensor + Flight computer + Servo actuator |
| **Robotics** | Robotic arm | Encoders + Microcontroller + Motor driver |
| **Process Industry** | Distillation column | Temperature sensor + PID controller + Valve |
| **Power Systems** | Automatic Voltage Regulator | Voltage sensor + AVR + Exciter |
| **Biomedical** | Insulin pump | Glucose sensor + Microprocessor + Pump actuator |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Consider a standard **negative-feedback** closed-loop system with the following blocks:

- Forward path transfer function: $G(s) = C(s) P(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function: $H(s)$
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $Y(s)$
- Feedback signal: $B(s) = H(s) Y(s)$
- Error signal: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$

**Step 1:** Write the error equation.

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $B(s) = H(s) Y(s)$ into the error equation.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$$

**Step 3:** Write the forward-path equation. The controller output drives the plant.

$$Y(s) = G(s) E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $E(s)$ from Step 2 into Step 3.

$$Y(s) = G(s) [R(s) - H(s) Y(s)]$$

**Step 5:** Distribute $G(s)$.

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$$

**Step 6:** Collect all $Y(s)$ terms on the left side.

$$Y(s) + G(s) H(s) Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 7:** Factor $Y(s)$ on the left.

$$Y(s) [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the ratio $Y(s)/R(s)$, which is the **Closed-Loop Transfer Function** $T(s)$.

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The "+" sign in the denominator $1 + G(s) H(s)$ arises because we assumed **negative feedback** at the comparator. The term $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **characteristic polynomial**, and its roots dictate the stability and dynamic behaviour of the system.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Error Transfer Function

**Step 1:** Start with the closed-loop transfer function.

$$Y(s) = \frac{G(s) R(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 2:** Express the error $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot \frac{G(s) R(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 3:** Take $R(s)$ common.

$$E(s) = R(s) \left[ 1 - \frac{G(s) H(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Bring to a common denominator.

$$E(s) = R(s) \left[ \frac{1 + G(s) H(s) - G(s) H(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the numerator.

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

**Step 6:** Thus, the error transfer function is:

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

### 3.3 Effect of Disturbance on Output

**Step 1:** Modify the plant equation to include disturbance $D(s)$ entering at the plant input.

$$Y(s) = G(s) U(s) + D(s)$$

where $G(s) = P(s)$ is taken as the plant transfer function (controller assumed unity here for simplicity).

**Step 2:** Substitute $U(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$.

$$Y(s) = G(s) [R(s) - H(s) Y(s)] + D(s)$$

**Step 3:** Collect $Y(s)$ terms.

$$Y(s) [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s) + D(s)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $Y(s)$.

$$Y(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} R(s) + \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)} D(s)$$

**Step 5:** The transfer function from disturbance to output is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $G(s) H(s)$ grows large, both the error and the disturbance effect on the output shrink — this is the **fundamental benefit of high loop gain** in feedback systems, enabling disturbance rejection.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix of Component Roles

| Component | Input Signal | Output Signal | Purpose |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| Comparator | $r(t), b(t)$ | $e(t)$ | Detects deviation from desired value |
| Controller $C(s)$ | $e(t)$ | $u(t)$ | Implements control law (P, PI, PD, PID) |
| Actuator $A(s)$ | $u(t)$ | $a(t)$ | Provides power amplification |
| Plant $P(s)$ | $a(t) + d(t)$ | $y(t)$ | Produces the controlled output |
| Sensor $H(s)$ | $y(t)$ | $b(t)$ | Converts output to feedback signal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Closed-Loop Control System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete interaction between all basic components of a closed-loop control system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Reference Input R] --> C1{Comparator}
    F[Feedback Signal B] --> C1
    C1 --> E[Error Signal E]
    E --> CON[Controller C]
    CON --> ACT[Actuator A]
    ACT --> PL[Plant P]
    DIS[Disturbance D] --> PL
    PL --> OUT[Output Y]
    OUT --> SEN[Sensor H]
    SEN --> F
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For a deeper, layered view of how signals propagate through the system, the architecture is mapped below.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[LAYER 1: Reference and Error] --> L2[LAYER 2: Decision and Control]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER 3: Power and Actuation]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER 4: Plant and Output]
    L4 --> L5[LAYER 5: Sensing and Feedback]

    L1A[Reference Input R] --> L1B[Comparator]
    L5A[Sensor Output B] --> L1B
    L1B --> L1C[Error E]

    L1C --> L2A[Controller C]
    L2A --> L2B[Control Signal U]

    L2B --> L3A[Actuator A]
    L3A --> L3B[Power Signal M]

    L3B --> L4A[Plant P]
    L4D[Disturbance D] --> L4A
    L4A --> L4B[Output Y]

    L4B --> L5A
    L5A --> L5B[Feedback B]
    L5B --> L1B
```

### 4.3 Reduced Block Diagram (Unified Forward Path)

When the controller, actuator, and plant are combined into a single forward block $G(s) = C(s) A(s) P(s)$, the diagram simplifies.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REF[Reference R] --> SUM{Comparator}
    FBK[Feedback B] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error E]
    ERR --> FW[Forward Path G of s]
    DIS[Disturbance D] --> FW
    FW --> OUT[Output Y]
    OUT --> SENS[Sensor H]
    SENS --> FBK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The comparator (drawn as a circle with a "+/−" sign) is where the negative-feedback convention originates. The disturbance $D(s)$ typically enters between the controller and the plant, but in the reduced diagram, it is shown as entering the forward path.

### 4.4 Open-Loop Block Diagram (Comparison)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R2[Reference R] --> C2[Controller C]
    C2 --> A2[Actuator A]
    A2 --> P2[Plant P]
    D2[Disturbance D] --> P2
    P2 --> Y2[Output Y]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Visual Difference:** In the open-loop diagram, there is **no feedback path** from the output back to the input. The output is not measured, and the system cannot self-correct. This is the fundamental structural difference between open-loop and closed-loop control systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List any four basic components of a control system and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (4 components):**

1. **Comparator / Error Detector:** Computes the difference between the reference input and the feedback signal to produce the error signal.
2. **Controller:** Processes the error signal using a control law (P, PI, PD, or PID) and generates the actuating signal.
3. **Actuator:** Converts the low-power control signal into a high-power signal capable of driving the plant.
4. **Sensor / Feedback Element:** Measures the actual output and feeds it back to the comparator.

> [!NOTE]
> For full 3 marks, students must state **component name + function**. Listing only the names without functions may lead to a 1-mark deduction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low | High |
| Sensitivity to disturbances | High | Low |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Stability | Generally stable | Requires design effort |
| Example | Washing machine (timer-based) | Room air-conditioner with thermostat |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic components of a closed-loop control system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a negative-feedback system having forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation with Block Diagram:**

The basic components of a closed-loop control system are:

1. **Reference Input R(s):** The desired value of the controlled variable.
2. **Comparator:** A summing junction where the reference input is compared with the feedback signal. The output is the error signal $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.
3. **Controller C(s):** Generates a control signal $U(s)$ based on the error. Common types are P, PI, PD, and PID controllers.
4. **Actuator A(s):** Amplifies the controller output to a level sufficient to drive the plant.
5. **Plant P(s):** The physical system or process being controlled. It produces the output $Y(s)$.
6. **Sensor / Feedback Element H(s):** Measures the output and converts it to a signal $B(s) = H(s) Y(s)$ compatible with the reference.
7. **Disturbance D(s):** An unwanted signal that affects the plant adversely.

The block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Part (b) — Derivation:**

Given: forward path $G(s)$, feedback path $H(s)$, reference $R(s)$, output $Y(s)$.

**Step 1:** Error signal: $E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) Y(s)$.

**Step 2:** Output of forward path: $Y(s) = G(s) E(s)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute: $Y(s) = G(s) [R(s) - H(s) Y(s)]$.

**Step 4:** Expand: $Y(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Y(s)$.

**Step 5:** Rearrange: $Y(s) [1 + G(s) H(s)] = G(s) R(s)$.

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** for a negative-feedback system.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain, with examples, the difference between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Mention any two advantages and two disadvantages of closed-loop systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a unity-feedback system with forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, find the closed-loop transfer function. Also, find the steady-state error for a unit-step input. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Explanation:**

- **Open-Loop System:** The control action is independent of the output. Example: A traffic light with a fixed timer.
- **Closed-Loop System:** The control action depends on the output via feedback. Example: A traffic light with a vehicle-presence sensor.

**Advantages of Closed-Loop:**

1. Improved accuracy due to feedback correction.
2. Reduced sensitivity to parameter variations and disturbances.
3. Better transient and steady-state response.

**Disadvantages of Closed-Loop:**

1. More complex and expensive due to additional components (sensors, controllers).
2. Possible instability if not designed carefully (the system may oscillate).
3. Requires more design effort and tuning.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Step 1:** For unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 2:** Closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)} = \frac{\frac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Step 4:** Steady-state error for a unit-step input using the Final Value Theorem:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:

$$E(s) = \frac{s(s+2)}{s[s(s+2) + 10]} = \frac{s+2}{s^2 + 2s + 10}$$

**Step 6:** Apply Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+2)}{s^2 + 2s + 10} = \frac{0 \cdot 2}{10} = 0$$

**Step 7:** The steady-state error is **zero** because the system is **Type 1** (one pole at the origin), which means it can track a step input with zero error.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Feedback:** Mixing up $1 + GH$ vs. $1 - GH$. The sign depends on whether the comparator uses **negative** or **positive** feedback. Always draw the comparator sign first.
> 2. **Skipping Block Diagram:** A 7-mark question on basic components **demands a neat block diagram**. Text-only answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Definition of Transfer Function:** Transfer functions are defined only for **linear time-invariant (LTI)** systems with **zero initial conditions**. Students often forget to mention these conditions.
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Problems:** Always mention units where applicable (e.g., rad/s for frequency).
> 5. **Confusing Plant and Process:** The plant is the *physical object* being controlled; the process is the *operation* being performed. In KTU answers, the terms are often used interchangeably, but be precise.
> 6. **Not Labelling Disturbance Path:** Many students omit the disturbance entry point in the block diagram, losing 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A control system has **7 basic components**: Reference, Comparator, Controller, Actuator, Plant, Sensor, and Disturbance.
- [x] The **error signal** is defined as $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ for negative feedback.
- [x] The **closed-loop transfer function** for a negative-feedback system is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

- [x] The **error transfer function** is:

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

- [x] High loop gain $G(s) H(s)$ → small error and good disturbance rejection.
- [x] **Open-loop** systems have **no feedback**; they are simple but inaccurate.
- [x] **Closed-loop** systems have **feedback**; they are accurate, robust, and can reject disturbances.
- [x] The **characteristic polynomial** $1 + G(s) H(s)$ governs **stability** (roots must lie in the left half-plane).
- [x] A **Type-1 system** (one pole at origin) tracks a step input with **zero steady-state error**.
- [x] The **Final Value Theorem** applies only when all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the left half-plane.
- [x] Always draw a **neat, labelled block diagram** with clear signal directions and the comparator sign.
- [x] Distinguish between **plant** (physical system) and **process** (operation).
- [x] The **sensor** in the feedback path converts the physical output into an electrical/measurable signal.
- [x] The **actuator** is the **power-amplifying** element that drives the plant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control Systems with Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control Systems with Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Control System?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Nagrath & Gopal / Ogata Standard):**
> A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components interconnected in such a way that it regulates (controls) itself or some other system. The output of the system is governed by a reference input command, and the goal is to make the output behave in a desired manner.

In simpler words — a control system **commands a process (plant)** so that its output follows a desired value (set-point) despite disturbances. A refrigerator keeping milk at 4 °C, a drone hovering at 1 m altitude, and the cruise control in a car are all control systems.

**Core Terminology (KTU Mandatory Glossary):**
- **Plant (Process):** The physical object to be controlled (e.g., a motor, a furnace, a robotic arm).
- **Input / Reference Signal (r):** The desired value we want the output to reach.
- **Output / Controlled Variable (c or y):** The actual variable being measured and regulated.
- **Actuator:** The device that supplies the physical power/energy to drive the plant (e.g., motor, hydraulic piston, heater coil).
- **Sensor / Transducer:** The device that measures the output and converts it to an equivalent electrical signal for comparison.
- **Disturbance (d):** Any unwanted signal that adversely affects the output (e.g., wind gust, load change, voltage drop).
- **Error Signal (e):** The difference between the desired input and the measured output; it is the *driving force* of the controller.

---

### 1.2 Open-Loop Control System (Non-Feedback System)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Open-Loop Control System** is one in which the control action is **independent of the output**. There is **no feedback** from the output back to the input. The output has no influence on the input signal that drives the actuator.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Common Toaster 🍞:**
Think of an ordinary pop-up toaster. You set a timer (say, 2 minutes). The toaster will heat the bread for *exactly* 2 minutes *regardless* of whether the bread is lightly browned, perfectly golden, or burnt to a crisp. The toaster **never measures** the actual color of the bread — it simply executes the command. The output (toast darkness) does not influence the input (timer). That is an open-loop system.

**Other Real-World Open-Loop Examples:**
- **Washing Machine:** Set a wash cycle of 30 minutes — it runs 30 minutes whether the clothes are clean or not.
- **Electric Hand Dryer / Room Heater:** Continuously blows hot air at a fixed setting — does not measure room temperature.
- **Traffic Lights:** Cycle red → green → yellow based on a fixed timer, ignoring actual traffic density.
- **DC Motor Driving a Conveyor at a Fixed Speed:** Voltage applied is constant; the speed is *assumed* to be constant.
- **Bread Maker, Coffee Vending Machine, Automatic Irrigation Timers.**

**Key Characteristics (KTU Standard):**
1. **No feedback path** — block diagram has a single forward path only.
2. **Simple construction**, **low cost**, and **easy to maintain**.
3. **Accuracy depends entirely on calibration** — any change in environment causes errors.
4. **Disturbances are not corrected** — output drifts from the desired value.
5. **Generally stable** (no tendency to oscillate) because there is no feedback loop to create instability.

---

### 1.3 Closed-Loop Control System (Feedback System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Closed-Loop Control System** is one in which the control action is **dependent on the output**. A portion of the output is **fed back** to the input side and compared with the reference input. The resulting **error signal** drives the controller so that the output continuously tracks the desired value.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Air Conditioner with a Thermostat ❄️:**
You set your AC to 24 °C. A sensor measures the actual room temperature. The thermostat continuously computes *(24 °C − actual temp)* and tells the compressor to work harder if the room is too warm, and ease off if it is too cool. **The output (room temperature) directly influences the input (compressor effort).** That is a closed-loop (feedback) system.

**Other Real-World Closed-Loop Examples:**
- **Human Body Thermoregulation:** Body temperature deviates from 37 °C → sensors in skin/hypothalamus send signals → shivering/sweating restores 37 °C. The body is the *world's finest feedback controller*.
- **Cruise Control in a Car:** Set 80 km/h → speedometer measures actual speed → throttle adjusts to maintain 80 km/h *even when going uphill*.
- **Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR):** Output voltage is sensed, compared to a reference, and the field current of the generator is adjusted.
- **Missile Guidance System:** The missile constantly measures its position relative to the target and corrects its course.
- **Servo Motor Position Control, Robotic Arm, Autopilot, Inverted Pendulum, Heart-Rate Pacemaker.**

**Key Characteristics (KTU Standard):**
1. **Negative feedback path** exists from output back to input.
2. **More accurate** — automatically corrects for errors and disturbances.
3. **Reduced sensitivity** to parameter variations, noise, and nonlinearities.
4. **Increased bandwidth** and improved transient response.
5. **Risk of instability** — feedback can cause oscillations if not designed carefully (a core focus of Module 2 onward).
6. **More complex, expensive**, and **requires more components** (sensors, comparators).

---

### 1.4 The Concept of Feedback — Heart of Control Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Positive Feedback:** The fed-back signal *adds* to the reference input. Used in oscillators (e.g., Schmitt trigger, sine-wave generator) — generally **leads to instability** in control systems.
>
> **Negative Feedback:** The fed-back signal is *subtracted* from the reference input. This is the **backbone of all industrial control** — reduces error, improves accuracy, and stabilizes the system.

**The Fundamental Equation (used everywhere in this course):**

$$
e(t) = r(t) - b(t)
$$

where $b(t) = H(s)\,c(t)$ is the feedback signal. The error drives the **controller** $G_c(s)$, which drives the **plant** $G_p(s)$. This single equation is the soul of KTU Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Closed-Loop System (in logical order)

1. **Reference Input Generator** → produces the set-point.
2. **Error Detector / Comparator** → produces $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
3. **Controller** → processes the error (P, PI, PD, PID — covered in Module 4).
4. **Actuator / Power Amplifier** → provides the muscle (energy).
5. **Plant / Process** → the actual physical system being controlled.
6. **Sensor / Transducer** → measures the output.
7. **Feedback Element** → shapes the measured signal to be in the same units as the input.

### 2.2 Block-Diagram Algebra (the language of Control Systems)

Every block represents a **Transfer Function** $G(s) = \dfrac{\mathcal{L}\{\text{output}\}}{\mathcal{L}\{\text{input}\}} \bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Standard Block Diagram Reduction Rules:**
> - **Cascade (Series):** $G_{\text{eq}}(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s)$
> - **Parallel (with same sign):** $G_{\text{eq}}(s) = G_1(s) + G_2(s)$
> - **Parallel (with opposite sign):** $G_{\text{eq}}(s) = G_1(s) - G_2(s)$
> - **Feedback Loop:** $G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ for **negative** feedback
> - $G_{\text{cl}}(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 - G(s)H(s)}$ for **positive** feedback

### 2.3 Transfer Function of Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop

**Open-Loop Transfer Function** (single forward path):

$$
G_{\text{OL}}(s) = G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,G_a(s)
$$

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function** (unity negative feedback, $H(s) = 1$):

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

**Closed-Loop Transfer Function** (general negative feedback):

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

**Transfer Function with respect to disturbance** (very important for KTU):

$$
\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}
$$

Notice the $1 + GH$ in the denominator — this is what makes feedback **reduce the effect of disturbance by a factor of $(1 + GH)$**.

### 2.4 Effect of Feedback on Sensitivity (High-Yield KTU Concept)

The **sensitivity** of the closed-loop system to a small change in the forward path $G$ is:

$$
S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}
$$

- If $GH \gg 1$, sensitivity $\to 0$. This is why feedback **desensitizes** the system.
- This is one of the *most-tested* KTU 2-mark and 3-mark concepts.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for this topic. Memorize it for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$ | Driving force of the controller |
| 2 | Open-loop TF | $C(s)/R(s) = G(s)$ | No feedback considered |
| 3 | Closed-loop TF (negative FB) | $C(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+GH}$ | Standard feedback equation |
| 4 | Closed-loop TF (positive FB) | $C(s)/R(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1-GH}$ | Used in oscillators |
| 5 | Disturbance Rejection | $C(s)/D(s) = \dfrac{G_2}{1+G_1G_2H}$ | Output due to disturbance |
| 6 | Sensitivity | $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | Lower is better (robust) |
| 7 | Steady-state error (Unity FB) | $e_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | Step input, position const. |
| 8 | Tracking accuracy | $\dfrac{C}{R} \to 1$ as $GH \to \infty$ | High loop gain = ideal tracking |
| 9 | Effect of disturbance | Reduced by factor $\dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ | Disturbance rejection |
| 10 | Bandwidth | $BW \approx (1+GH) \cdot BW_{\text{open}}$ | Feedback increases bandwidth |

### 2.6 Why Feedback Is a "Two-Edged Sword" (Real-World Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> In industry, **every serious control loop** uses feedback. The Boeing 777 fly-by-wire system, the Apollo guidance computer, the Tesla autopilot — all rely on continuous feedback. The reason is the four-fold benefit:
>
> 1. **Accuracy** — error correction automatically.
> 2. **Disturbance rejection** — wind, load, friction are cancelled.
> 3. **Reduced sensitivity** — sensor calibration drift matters less.
> 4. **Improved transient response** — bandwidth and damping can be tuned.
>
> The *price* you pay: **possible instability** and **higher cost/complexity**. Balancing these trade-offs is the central theme of your entire control-systems course.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Standard Negative Feedback)

Consider a basic negative feedback loop with:
- Forward path transfer function $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function $H(s)$
- Input $R(s)$, output $C(s)$, error $E(s)$, feedback signal $B(s)$

**Step 1 — Error at the comparator:**

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Output from the forward path:**

$$
C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Feedback signal from the feedback block:**

$$
B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:**

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute this $E(s)$ into Step 2:**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\bigl[R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)\bigr]
$$

**Step 6 — Expand:**

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,R(s) - G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s)
$$

**Step 7 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left:**

$$
C(s) + G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $C(s)$:**

$$
C(s)\bigl[1 + G(s)\,H(s)\bigr] = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for the closed-loop transfer function:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation in Module 1**. It is worth **at least one 7-mark sub-part** in every KTU End-Semester Examination. Practice writing it from memory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Transfer Function with Disturbance

Now assume a disturbance $D(s)$ enters the plant as shown in the standard two-block forward path:

**Step 1 — Output has two contributions** (one from $R(s)$ through $G_1 G_2$, and one from $D(s)$ through $G_2$):

$$
C(s) = G_1(s)\,G_2(s)\,E(s) + G_2(s)\,D(s)
$$

**Step 2 — Error with feedback:**

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:**

$$
C(s) = G_1 G_2\bigl[R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)\bigr] + G_2 D(s)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand:**

$$
C(s) = G_1 G_2 R(s) - G_1 G_2 H(s) C(s) + G_2 D(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Collect $C(s)$ terms:**

$$
C(s)\bigl[1 + G_1 G_2 H(s)\bigr] = G_1 G_2 R(s) + G_2 D(s)
$$

**Step 6 — Divide throughout by the bracket:**

$$
C(s) = \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}\,R(s) \;+\; \frac{G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}\,D(s)
$$

**Step 7 — Two transfer functions emerge:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{D=0} = \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{R=0} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** If $G_1 G_2 H \gg 1$, then $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} \to 0$ — **disturbance is rejected**. This is the mathematical proof of why feedback works.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Sensitivity Function

**Step 1 — Closed-loop TF (unity feedback, $H=1$):**

$$
T = \frac{G}{1 + G}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity definition (KTU standard):**

$$
S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{G}{T}\cdot\frac{\partial T}{\partial G}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\partial T / \partial G$:**

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1+G)\cdot 1 - G\cdot 1}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+G)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
S_G^T = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{G}{\dfrac{G}{1+G}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+G)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
S_G^T = \frac{(1+G)}{(1+G)^2} = \frac{1}{1+G}
$$

**Step 6 — For general $H$:**

$$
S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Large loop gain $GH$ ⇒ small sensitivity. The closed-loop system becomes **insensitive to variations in $G$**. This is the engineering reason feedback is used in manufacturing where component tolerances are unavoidable.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-part)

**Problem:** A unity-feedback system has forward path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Find:
1. Closed-loop transfer function $T(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.
2. Output $c(t)$ for a unit step input $r(t) = 1$.
3. Steady-state output $c_{\text{ss}}$.

**Step 1 — Closed-loop TF using $T = G/(1+G)$:**

$$
T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}} = \frac{10}{s(s+2) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 10}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$:**

$$
C(s) = T(s)\cdot R(s) = \frac{10}{s(s^2+2s+10)}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state value using Final Value Theorem:**

$$
c_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s \to 0}\,s\,C(s) = \lim_{s \to 0}\,\frac{10}{s^2+2s+10} = \frac{10}{10} = 1
$$

**Interpretation:** The output ultimately reaches 1 (matching the input) — the system **tracks the reference** at steady state. This is a hallmark of *Type-1* systems, which you will study in Module 3.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Closed Loop

| S.No. | Property | Open-Loop System | Closed-Loop System |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback path | Absent | Present (negative FB) |
| 2 | Accuracy | Poor (depends on calibration) | High (auto-corrects) |
| 3 | Sensitivity to parameter variation | High | Reduced by $(1+GH)$ |
| 4 | Effect of disturbance | No correction | Reduced by $(1+GH)$ |
| 5 | Bandwidth | Lower | Higher (improved) |
| 6 | Stability | Always stable (usually) | Can become unstable |
| 7 | Cost & complexity | Low, simple | High, complex |
| 8 | Examples | Toaster, washing machine | AC with thermostat, cruise control |
| 9 | Steady-state error | Large | Small (often zero) |
| 10 | Transient response | Not tunable | Tunable via controller |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Open-Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input R] --> B[Controller Gc]
    B --> C[Actuator Ga]
    C --> D[Plant Gp]
    D --> E[Output C]
    D -.-> F[Disturbance D]
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6961,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows strictly **left → right**. There is **no arrow going from Output back to the input**. The dashed red line represents the disturbance $D(s)$ acting on the plant — notice that the open-loop system has no mechanism to *react* to this disturbance.

---

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Control System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Input R] --> Sum[Comparator]
    Sum --> E[Error E]
    E --> Gc[Controller Gc]
    Gc --> Ga[Actuator Ga]
    Ga --> Gp[Plant Gp]
    Gp --> C[Output C]
    C --> H[Feedback Sensor H]
    H --> B[Feedback Signal B]
    B --> Sum
    Gp -.-> D[Disturbance D]
    style R fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Gc fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Ga fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Gp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Sum fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6961,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**How to read the summing junction (`Sum`):** Two arrows enter — $R(s)$ with a $+$ sign and $B(s)$ with a $-$ sign. The output is $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$.

---

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Graph Representation (Advanced)

A signal-flow graph (SFG) is an alternative to block diagrams. Nodes represent signals; directed branches represent transfer functions.

**Open-Loop SFG (3 nodes):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR((R)) --G1--> nodeX((X))
    nodeX((X)) --G2--> nodeC((C))
    style nodeR fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeX fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Closed-Loop SFG (3 nodes, 1 loop):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR((R)) --G1--> nodeE((E))
    nodeE((E)) --G2--> nodeC((C))
    nodeC((C)) --H1--> nodeE((E))
    style nodeR fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mason's Gain Formula (preview of Module 1 SFG topic):** For the closed-loop SFG above, the forward path is $P_1 = G_1 G_2$ and the only loop is $L_1 = -G_2 H_1$. Therefore
>
> $$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{P_1}{1 - L_1} = \frac{G_1 G_2}{1 + G_1 G_2 H_1}$$
>
> This **matches** the block-diagram result from Section 3.1 — confirming both methods are equivalent.

---

### 4.4 Physical-World Schematic — Air Conditioner with Thermostat (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[User sets 24 deg C] --> Therm[Thermostat Reference]
    Therm --> Comp[Comparator]
    RoomSensor[Room Temperature Sensor] --> Comp
    Comp --> MCU[Controller MCU]
    MCU --> Compressor[Compressor Actuator]
    Compressor --> ColdAir[Cool Air Output]
    ColdAir --> Room[Room Environment]
    Room --> RoomSensor
    style User fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Therm fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Comp fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RoomSensor fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MCU fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Compressor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ColdAir fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Room fill:#C8F7C5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop `Room → RoomSensor → Comparator → MCU → Compressor → ColdAir → Room` is the **feedback loop**. If the room gets too hot, the comparator outputs a positive error; the MCU increases compressor effort. If the room gets too cold, the comparator outputs a negative error; the MCU reduces compressor effort. Equilibrium at 24 °C is maintained automatically — this is the *magic* of negative feedback.
