# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Instructions:** Each question carries **3 marks**. Answer in 2–3 sentences with necessary equations and diagrams.

---

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define the transfer function of a control system. List any four components of a block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The **transfer function** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) control system is defined as the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming **zero initial conditions**:

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$$

**Four components of a block diagram are:**

1. **Block** — represents a transfer function.
2. **Summing Junction** — performs algebraic addition/subtraction of signals.
3. **Take-off (Branch) Point** — distributes a signal to multiple paths.
4. **Signal Flow Line (Arrow)** — indicates the direction of signal flow.

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State the formula for the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback system. What is meant by the characteristic equation?**

**Model Answer:**

For a negative feedback system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback path $H(s)$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

The **characteristic equation** is obtained by setting the denominator of the closed-loop transfer function to zero:

$$1 + G(s) H(s) = 0$$

The roots of this equation are the **closed-loop poles** of the system, and they determine the system's stability, natural frequency, and damping.

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Instructions:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ of a negative feedback control system having forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Reduce the block diagram shown below and find the overall transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$:

- Forward path contains $G_1$, $G_2$ in cascade.
- Inner feedback loop with $H_1$ around $G_2$.
- Outer feedback loop with $H_2$ around the entire forward path.
- Given: $G_1 = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$, $G_2 = \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}$, $H_1 = 1$, $H_2 = \dfrac{1}{s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** The error signal is formed at the summing junction:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2:** Output of the forward block:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Feedback signal:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting (Step 3) into (Step 1):

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into (Step 2):

$$C(s) = G(s) \bigl[R(s) - H(s) C(s)\bigr]$$

**Step 6:** Expanding:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 7:** Collecting $C(s)$ terms on one side:

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 8:** Factoring:

$$C(s) \bigl[1 + G(s) H(s)\bigr] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 9:** Final transfer function:

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

**Characteristic Equation:** $1 + G(s) H(s) = 0$

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Reduce the inner feedback loop around $G_2$ with feedback $H_1 = 1$:**

$$G_{inner} = \frac{G_2}{1 + G_2 H_1} = \frac{\dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{4}{s(s+2)}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$1 + \frac{4}{s(s+2)} = \frac{s(s+2) + 4}{s(s+2)} = \frac{s^2 + 2s + 4}{s(s+2)}$$

Therefore:

$$G_{inner} = \frac{4}{s(s+2)} \cdot \frac{s(s+2)}{s^2 + 2s + 4} = \frac{4}{s^2 + 2s + 4}$$

**Step 2 — Cascade $G_1$ with $G_{inner}$:**

$$G_{fwd} = G_1 \cdot G_{inner} = \frac{1}{s+1} \cdot \frac{4}{s^2 + 2s + 4} = \frac{4}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the outer feedback loop (Rule 3, $H_2 = \dfrac{1}{s}$, negative feedback):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{fwd}}{1 + G_{fwd} H_2} = \frac{\dfrac{4}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)}}{1 + \dfrac{4}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s}}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$1 + \frac{4}{s(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)} = \frac{s(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4) + 4}{s(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)}$$

Expand $s(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)$:

- First, $(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4) = s^3 + 2s^2 + 4s + s^2 + 2s + 4 = s^3 + 3s^2 + 6s + 4$
- Then multiply by $s$: $s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s$

Add 4: $s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 4$

Therefore:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{4}{(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)} \cdot \frac{s(s+1)(s^2 + 2s + 4)}{s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 4}$$

Cancel common factors:

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{4s}{s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 4}}$$

**Characteristic Equation:** $s^4 + 3s^3 + 6s^2 + 4s + 4 = 0$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the rules for block diagram reduction. State at least six rules. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Reduce the following block diagram to obtain the overall transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$:

- Forward path: $G_1 = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$ and $G_2 = \dfrac{1}{s}$ in cascade.
- A feed-forward block $G_3 = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$ connected from input $R(s)$ to the output of $G_2$ (parallel with the cascade).
- Unity negative feedback ($H = 1$) around the entire system.

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

(Already covered in Section 2.2 of these notes — the six rules are: Cascade, Parallel, Feedback Loop, Move Summing Junction Ahead of Block, Move Summing Junction Beyond Block, and Move Take-off Point.)

The student should reproduce the diagrams and formulas for all six rules in a tabulated form as shown in Section 2.3 of these notes.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parallel combination.**  
$G_1 \cdot G_2$ (cascade) is in parallel with $G_3$ (feed-forward).

$$G_1 G_2 = \frac{2}{s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{2}{s(s+1)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 2 (parallel combination):**

$$G_{mid} = G_1 G_2 + G_3 = \frac{2}{s(s+1)} + \frac{1}{s+2}$$

Common denominator $s(s+1)(s+2)$:

$$G_{mid} = \frac{2(s+2) + s(s+1)}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{2s + 4 + s^2 + s}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 4}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Rule 3 with $H = 1$ (unity negative feedback):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_{mid}}{1 + G_{mid}} = \frac{\dfrac{s^2 + 3s + 4}{s(s+1)(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{s^2 + 3s + 4}{s(s+1)(s+2)}}$$

Combine:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 4}{s(s+1)(s+2) + s^2 + 3s + 4}$$

Expand the denominator:

- $s(s+1)(s+2) = s(s^2 + 3s + 2) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s$
- Add $s^2 + 3s + 4$: $s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 4$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 4}{s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 4}}$$

**Characteristic Equation:** $s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 4 = 0$

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign error at the summing junction:** If the problem states "positive feedback" or shows a "+" sign at the feedback input, the formula becomes $G/(1 - GH)$, **not** $G/(1 + GH)$. Always re-check the polarity of the summing junction.
>
> 2. **Order of reduction:** Students often reduce the **outer** loop first instead of the **innermost** loop. This leads to messy algebra and often an incorrect answer. **Rule:** Always work from the *innermost* loop outward.
>
> 3. **Confusing take-off movement with summing-junction movement:** These are **two distinct rules** with different sign/multiplier conventions. A summing-junction moved *before* a block requires **dividing the second input by G**. A take-off point moved *before* a block requires **inserting a 1/G block on the branch line** — the operation is on the *branch*, not the main path.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to cancel common factors:** In the final step (Step 4 of Worked Example 2), the factor $(s+1)(s^2+2s+4)$ cancels cleanly. Students who miss this leave an unnecessarily complex answer and lose method marks.
>
> 5. **Not stating the characteristic equation explicitly:** KTU examiners often award 1–2 marks specifically for writing the characteristic equation. Always end the solution with the equation: *"The characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, which expands to ..."*
>
> 6. **Skipping intermediate diagrams:** Show each reduction step as a small diagram or at least as an equation. Do not jump from the original tangled diagram to the final answer.
>
> 7. **Misidentifying cascade vs. parallel:** Two blocks in cascade have the *output of the first connected to the input of the second*. Two blocks in parallel have the *same input* and their outputs are *summed at a junction*. Confusing these changes the entire structure of the solution.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet List — KTU Module 1, Block Diagram Reduction:**
>
> ✅ The **transfer function** of an LTI system is $G(s) = C(s)/R(s)$ with **zero initial conditions**.
>
> ✅ A block diagram has **four elements:** Block, Summing Junction, Take-off Point, Signal Flow Line.
>
> ✅ **Cascade rule:** $G_{eq} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3 \cdots$
>
> ✅ **Parallel rule:** $G_{eq} = G_1 + G_2 + G_3 \cdots$
>
> ✅ **Negative feedback rule:** $G_{eq} = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$
>
> ✅ **Positive feedback rule:** $G_{eq} = \dfrac{G}{1 - GH}$
>
> ✅ **Summing junction moved before a block $\Rightarrow$** divide the second input by $G$.
>
> ✅ **Summing junction moved after a block $\Rightarrow$** multiply the second input by $G$.
>
> ✅ **Take-off point moved before a block $\Rightarrow$** insert a $1/G$ block on the *branch line*.
>
> ✅ **Take-off point moved after a block $\Rightarrow$** insert a $G$ block on the *branch line*.
>
> ✅ **Adjacent summing junctions or take-off points can be swapped** with no modification.
>
> ✅ **Reduction order: Innermost loop first, then cascade/parallel as needed, then outer loop.**
>
> ✅ **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ — write this explicitly at the end.
>
> ✅ **Open Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)** = product of blocks in the loop = $G(s)H(s)$.
>
> ✅ **Closed Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)** = $\dfrac{\text{Forward Path TF}}{1 \mp \text{Loop Gain}}$.
>
> ✅ Always **state the polarity** (negative or positive feedback) before applying the feedback formula.
>
> ✅ Always **cancel common factors** in the final transfer function.
>
> ✅ Block diagram reduction preserves the **input–output behavior** of the system — verify that the reduced TF is dimensionally consistent and matches the original at limiting cases (e.g., $s \to 0$, $s \to \infty$).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Flow Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signal Flow Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Flow Graph (SFG):** A *Signal Flow Graph* is a topological representation of a system of simultaneous linear algebraic equations that graphically depicts the flow of signals from one node to another, where each node represents a system variable and each directed branch represents the gain (transmittance) between two nodes. It was introduced by **Samuel Jefferson Mason** (1953) as a streamlined alternative to block diagram representation.

In KTU's 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the SFG is treated as the **canonical graphical method** for computing the transfer function of a linear time-invariant (LTI) single-input, single-output (SISO) system, complementing the **Block Diagram Reduction** technique taught in the same module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Block Diagrams:**
> - *Block Diagram* → uses functional blocks (rectangles), summing junctions (circles), and take-off points.
> - *Signal Flow Graph* → uses ONLY *nodes* (small circles representing variables) and *directed branches* (line segments with an associated gain).
> - An SFG is mathematically equivalent to a set of linear algebraic equations of the form $x_k = \sum_{j} a_{jk}\,x_j$.

## 1.2 Essential Terminology (KTU Board-Frequently Asked)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | A point in the graph representing a system variable (signal). |
| **Branch** | A directed line segment connecting two nodes with an associated gain (transmittance). |
| **Input Node (Source Node)** | A node that has only outgoing branches; its value is independent. |
| **Output Node (Sink Node)** | A node that has only incoming branches; its value depends on the input. |
| **Path** | A continuous succession of branches traversed in the direction of arrowheads. |
| **Forward Path** | A path from input node to output node that **does not cross any node more than once**. |
| **Loop (Feedback Loop)** | A closed path that starts and ends at the same node, crossing no other node more than once. |
| **Self-Loop** | A loop containing only one node (branch from node back to itself). |
| **Path Gain ($P_k$)** | The product of all branch gains along the $k^{th}$ forward path. |
| **Loop Gain ($L_i$)** | The product of all branch gains around the $i^{th}$ loop. |
| **Touching** | Two elements (paths/loops) are said to *touch* if they share at least one common node. |
| **Non-Touching Loops** | Two or more loops that share no common node. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Water Pipeline Network"**
>
> Imagine a city water distribution network. Each **junction** (node) is a point where water collects or splits. Each **pipe segment** (branch) carries water from one junction to another with a certain **flow restriction factor** (gain). The *source* is the main reservoir (input), and the *final tap* in your house is the sink (output).
>
> - A **forward path** = a continuous pipe route from the reservoir to your tap, without revisiting any junction.
> - A **loop** = a circular pipe arrangement that recirculates water (like a feedback pipe).
> - **Non-touching loops** = two separate circular pipe systems in different parts of the city that don't share any junction.
>
> The "net flow" reaching your tap is what Mason's Gain Formula computes — accounting for all forward routes and subtracting the recirculation (feedback) effects.

## 1.4 Why SFG is Preferred in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> - For **multi-loop, multi-path** systems, block diagram reduction is tedious and error-prone.
> - Mason's Gain Formula (a single, unified equation) handles *any* number of forward paths and loops **in one shot**.
> - SFG directly maps to a system of linear equations, enabling computational/programmatic analysis.
> - KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 explicitly lists SFG as a *high-weightage* topic with **Mason's Gain Formula application** as a mandatory 14-mark question pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of SFG from Block Diagram — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board often tests the conversion between block diagrams and SFGs. Follow this exact procedure:

**Step 1 — Identify Nodes:**
- Every summing junction becomes a node.
- Every take-off point (branching point) becomes a node.
- The input variable $R(s)$ and output variable $C(s)$ are nodes.
- Every variable between consecutive blocks becomes a node.

**Step 2 — Identify Branches:**
- Each block (with its transfer function $G_i$) becomes a directed branch from its input node to its output node, labeled with gain $G_i$.
- Each feedback path (with gain $H_j$) becomes a directed branch from the take-off point back to the summing junction.
- A unity-gain branch (gain = 1) is used when no block exists but signal flow must be preserved.

**Step 3 — Preserve Directionality:**
- All arrows must point from cause (input) to effect (output).

**Step 4 — Simplify:**
- Series nodes connected by unity gains can be merged if they form no loops.

## 2.2 Mason's Gain Formula — Operational Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mason's Gain Formula (MGF):** For a linear signal flow graph, the overall transfer function $T = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ between a designated input node and output node is given by:
>
> $$\boxed{\;T = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\,P_k\,\Delta_k\;}$$

Where:
- $P_k$ = gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path from input to output.
- $\Delta$ = **determinant of the graph**.
- $\Delta_k$ = **cofactor** of the $k^{th}$ forward path.
- $N$ = total number of forward paths.

## 2.3 The Graph Determinant $\Delta$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta = 1 - \sum_i L_i + \sum_{i,j} L_iL_j - \sum_{i,j,k} L_iL_jL_k + \cdots\;}$$

Where:
- $\sum_i L_i$ = sum of gains of **all individual loops**.
- $\sum_{i,j} L_iL_j$ = sum of products of gains of **all possible pairs of non-touching loops**.
- $\sum_{i,j,k} L_iL_jL_k$ = sum of products of gains of **all possible triplets of non-touching loops**.
- The series continues until no higher-order non-touching combinations remain.
- **Sign alternation:** single loops subtract, pairs add, triplets subtract, etc.

## 2.4 The Cofactor $\Delta_k$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta_k = 1 - \sum L_i^{(k)} + \sum L_i^{(k)}L_j^{(k)} - \cdots\;}$$

Where $\sum L_i^{(k)}$ is the sum of gains of all individual loops that do **NOT** touch the $k^{th}$ forward path. In other words, $\Delta_k$ is the value of $\Delta$ for the **portion of the SFG that is left after removing all loops touching the $k^{th}$ forward path** $P_k$.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Always commit this table to memory — every 14-mark SFG question expects fluent use of these.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta}\sum P_k \Delta_k$ | Mason's Gain Formula | $T$ = transfer function |
| 2 | $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_iL_j - \sum L_iL_jL_k + \cdots$ | Graph determinant | Alternating signs |
| 3 | $P_k = \prod_{m} G_{m}^{(k)}$ | Path gain = product of branch gains along path $k$ | Sequential multiplication |
| 4 | $L_i = \prod_{n} G_{n}^{(i)}$ | Loop gain = product of branch gains around loop $i$ | Sequential multiplication |
| 5 | $\Delta_k = \Delta \vert_{\text{all loops touching } P_k = 0}$ | Cofactor evaluated with touching loops removed | "Erase" method |
| 6 | Two loops are non-touching iff they share **zero common nodes** | Geometric node-disjoint condition | — |
| 7 | Input node has only outgoing branches; output node has only incoming | Source / Sink rule | — |
| 8 | Maximum non-touching loop order | If no three loops are mutually non-touching, terms of order 3 and higher vanish | Truncation rule |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Analog/RF Circuit Analysis:** SFG topology is mathematically identical to the **S-parameter flow graphs** used in microwave engineering for cascaded two-port networks.
> - **Digital Filter Design:** Signal flow graphs underpin the *transposed direct form II* and *lattice* filter structures.
> - **Control System Tuning:** Engineers apply Mason's formula to compute closed-loop transfer functions of multi-loop feedback controllers (cascade, multi-input-multi-output, and feed-forward controllers) without manual block reduction.
> - **State-Space Conversion:** Mason's formula provides a transparent pathway to convert a transfer function into its corresponding state-space representation.

## 2.7 Systematic Procedure to Apply Mason's Formula

1. **Identify the input node** (typically $R(s)$) and **output node** (typically $C(s)$).
2. **List all forward paths** $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_N$ from input to output (no node revisited).
3. **List all individual loops** $L_1, L_2, \dots, L_M$ in the entire SFG.
4. **Identify non-touching loop pairs** $L_iL_j$, then triplets, etc.
5. **Compute $\Delta$** using the alternating sum.
6. **For each $P_k$**, identify which loops touch it; compute $\Delta_k$ by ignoring (erasing) those touching loops.
7. **Substitute into MGF** and simplify.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mason's Gain Formula (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Foundation:** Every node in an SFG satisfies the linear algebraic equation:
>
> $$x_k = \sum_{j}\,a_{jk}\,x_j + b_k\,u$$
>
> where $a_{jk}$ is the gain of the branch from node $j$ to node $k$, and $b_k$ is the input injection gain. Writing the system in matrix form:
>
> $$\mathbf{x} = A\mathbf{x} + B\,u \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (I - A)\mathbf{x} = B\,u$$
>
> The output $y = x_n$ (output node) is solved using **Cramer's Rule** on the linear system. The denominator of the solution is $\det(I - A) = \Delta$ (graph determinant), and the numerator is the determinant of the matrix $(I - A)$ with the output-node column replaced by $B\,u$. Each term in the numerator's cofactor expansion corresponds to a forward path $P_k$ multiplied by a cofactor $\Delta_k$, yielding:
>
> $$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\,P_k\,\Delta_k$$
>
> The expansion of $\det(I-A)$ produces alternating sums of loop gains (single, pairs of non-touching, triplets of non-touching), which is exactly the formula for $\Delta$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Canonical Two-Loop SFG (Basic Level)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** For the SFG shown in §4.1 (see SECTION_4 diagram), find the transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$.

**Given:**
- Forward Path: $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$ (from $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to C$)
- Loop 1: $L_1 = G_2 H_1$ (around $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_1$)
- Loop 2: $L_2 = G_3 H_2$ (around $X_2 \to C \to X_2$)
- Loop 1 and Loop 2 share node $X_2$ → **they touch**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Determinant $\Delta$

List all individual loops: $L_1 = G_2 H_1$, $L_2 = G_3 H_2$.

List non-touching pairs: None (because $L_1$ and $L_2$ share node $X_2$).

List non-touching triplets: None.

Therefore:

$$\Delta = 1 - \sum_i L_i + \sum_{i,j} L_iL_j - \cdots$$

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta = 1 - G_2H_1 - G_3H_2\;}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Cofactor $\Delta_1$

Identify loops touching $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$:

- $L_1$ shares nodes $X_1$ and $X_2$ with $P_1$ → **touches**.
- $L_2$ shares nodes $X_2$ and $C$ with $P_1$ → **touches**.

Both loops touch $P_1$, so we remove **all** loops when computing $\Delta_1$:

$$\Delta_1 = 1 - 0 + 0 = 1$$

### Step 3 — Apply Mason's Gain Formula

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{P_1\,\Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 \cdot 1}{1 - G_2H_1 - G_3H_2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3}{1 - G_2H_1 - G_3H_2}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Path with Non-Touching Loops (Board Exam Level)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** Determine the transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ for the SFG with the following structural data:
> - Nodes: $R(s),\ X_1,\ X_2,\ X_3,\ C(s)$
> - Forward path 1: $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$ with gain $G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5$
> - Forward path 2: $R \to X_1 \to C$ with gain $G_1 G_4$
> - Loop 1: $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_1$ with gain $L_1 = G_2 H_1$
> - Loop 2: $X_2 \to X_3 \to X_2$ with gain $L_2 = G_3 H_2$

### Step 1 — Topology Summary Table

| Element | Nodes Involved | Gain |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | $R, X_1, X_2, X_3, C$ | $G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5$ |
| $P_2$ | $R, X_1, C$ | $G_1 G_4$ |
| $L_1$ | $X_1, X_2$ | $G_2 H_1$ |
| $L_2$ | $X_2, X_3$ | $G_3 H_2$ |

### Step 2 — Touch Analysis

| Comparison | Result |
|---|---|
| $L_1$ vs $L_2$ | Share node $X_2$ → **touching** |
| $P_1$ vs $L_1$ | Share $X_1, X_2$ → **touching** |
| $P_1$ vs $L_2$ | Share $X_2, X_3$ → **touching** |
| $P_2$ vs $L_1$ | Share $X_1$ → **touching** |
| $P_2$ vs $L_2$ | $P_2$ nodes are $\{R, X_1, C\}$; $L_2$ nodes are $\{X_2, X_3\}$ → **NO common node** → **non-touching** |

### Step 3 — Compute the Determinant $\Delta$

**Individual loops:** $L_1, L_2$ → sum $= L_1 + L_2 = G_2 H_1 + G_3 H_2$.

**Non-touching pairs of loops:** None (since $L_1$ and $L_2$ touch).

**Higher-order terms:** None.

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2)$$

$$\Delta = 1 - G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta = 1 - G_2H_1 - G_3H_2\;}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Cofactor $\Delta_1$ (for forward path $P_1$)

Loops touching $P_1$: both $L_1$ and $L_2$.

Therefore, when computing $\Delta_1$, all loops are removed:

$$\Delta_1 = 1 - 0 = 1$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta_1 = 1\;}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Cofactor $\Delta_2$ (for forward path $P_2$)

Loops touching $P_2$: $L_1$ (shares $X_1$); $L_2$ **does not touch** $P_2$.

Therefore, when computing $\Delta_2$, only $L_1$ is removed; $L_2$ remains.

$$\Delta_2 = 1 - (\text{sum of non-touching loops to } P_2)$$

$$\Delta_2 = 1 - L_2$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta_2 = 1 - G_3 H_2\;}$$

### Step 6 — Assemble Mason's Gain Formula

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta}$$

Substitute all terms:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{(G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5)(1) + (G_1 G_4)(1 - G_3 H_2)}{1 - G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2}$$

Expand the numerator:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5 + G_1 G_4 - G_1 G_3 G_4 H_2}{1 - G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2}$$

Factor $G_1$ from the numerator:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 \left(G_2 G_3 G_5 + G_4 - G_3 G_4 H_2\right)}{1 - G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 \left(G_4 + G_3(G_2 G_5 - G_4 H_2)\right)}{1 - G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2}\;}$$

### Step 7 — Verification (Sanity Check)

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check using Block Diagram Reduction (alternative method):**
>
> - Inner loop 2 (around $G_3$ with feedback $H_2$) reduces to $\dfrac{G_3}{1 - G_3 H_2}$.
> - Inner loop 1 (around $G_2$ with feedback $H_1$) reduces to $\dfrac{G_2}{1 - G_2 H_1}$.
> - Parallel combination: $G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5$ in parallel with $G_1 G_4$.
> - Final result must match our Mason's computation. (Students are advised to perform this cross-check during exam preparation.)

---

## 3.4 Common Algorithmic Trap — Sign Conventions

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:**
>
> In many textbooks and the KTU reference style, feedback gains are drawn as **negative** (with the minus sign already absorbed into the branch label $H$). If the block diagram shows a *positive* summing junction, then the feedback gain in the SFG must be entered as **positive**. Always trace the actual sign from the original block diagram — do not blindly insert a minus sign.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Decision Table

| If the SFG has... | Then in Mason's formula... |
|---|---|
| **1 forward path, 0 loops** | $T = P_1$ (direct feedforward) |
| **1 forward path, 1 loop touching it** | $T = \dfrac{P_1}{1 - L_1}$ |
| **1 forward path, 2 non-touching loops** | $T = \dfrac{P_1}{1 - L_1 - L_2 + L_1 L_2}$ |
| **2 forward paths, both touched by all loops** | $T = \dfrac{P_1 + P_2}{1 - \sum L_i}$ |
| **2 forward paths, only one loop touches $P_2$** | $T = \dfrac{P_1 + P_2(1 - L_{\text{non-touching to }P_2})}{\Delta}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference SFG Topology (for Worked Example 1)

The diagram below depicts the canonical two-loop SFG used in Worked Example 1 ($P_1 = G_1G_2G_3$, $L_1 = G_2H_1$, $L_2 = G_3H_2$).

```mermaid
graph LR
    RNODE((R)):::source -->|G1| X1NODE((X1))
    X1NODE -->|G2| X2NODE((X2))
    X2NODE -->|G3| CNODE((C)):::sink
    X2NODE -->|H1| X1NODE
    CNODE -->|H2| X2NODE
    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#b35900,color:#000
```

**Visual reading guide:**
- $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to C$ — the **forward path** $P_1$ (gain $G_1 G_2 G_3$).
- $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_1$ — **Loop 1** $L_1 = G_2 H_1$ (between $X_1$ and $X_2$).
- $X_2 \to C \to X_2$ — **Loop 2** $L_2 = G_3 H_2$ (between $X_2$ and $C$).
- Loop 1 and Loop 2 share node $X_2$ → they are **touching** → no $L_1 L_2$ term in $\Delta$.

---

## 4.2 Multi-Path SFG with Non-Touching Loop (Worked Example 2)

```mermaid
graph LR
    RNODE((R)):::source -->|G1| X1NODE((X1))
    X1NODE -->|G2| X2NODE((X2))
    X2NODE -->|G3| X3NODE((X3))
    X3NODE -->|G5| CNODE((C)):::sink
    X1NODE -->|G4| CNODE
    X2NODE -->|H1| X1NODE
    X3NODE -->|H2| X2NODE
    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#b35900,color:#000
```

**Visual reading guide:**
- **Forward Path 1:** $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$, gain $= G_1 G_2 G_3 G_5$.
- **Forward Path 2:** $R \to X_1 \to C$, gain $= G_1 G_4$.
- **Loop 1:** $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_1$, gain $= G_2 H_1$.
- **Loop 2:** $X_2 \to X_3 \to X_2$, gain $= G_3 H_2$.
- **Non-touching observation:** Loop 2 does **not** share any node with Forward Path 2.

---

## 4.3 Procedural Workflow — Solving an SFG using Mason's Formula

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START: Receive SFG] --> B[Mark input and output nodes]
    B --> C[Enumerate all forward paths]
    C --> D[Compute path gains Pk]
    B --> E[Enumerate all individual loops]
    E --> F[Compute loop gains Li]
    F --> G[Identify non-touching pairs]
    G --> H[Identify non-touching triplets]
    H --> I[Compute graph determinant Delta]
    C --> J[For each Pk: identify touching loops]
    J --> K[Compute cofactor Delta_k for each Pk]
    K --> L[Substitute into Mason formula]
    L --> M[Simplify algebraic expression]
    M --> N[FINAL: Transfer function T]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mason's Gain Formula Engine

For students interested in *how* an SFG solver might be implemented (conceptual, **not** a coding exercise — purely descriptive block diagram):

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[SFG Topology Input] --> B0[Adjacency Matrix Builder]
    B0 --> C0[Path and Loop Enumerator]
    C0 --> D0[Touch Matrix Analyzer]
    D0 --> E0[Delta Calculator]
    C0 --> F0[Forward Path Computer]
    D0 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Cofactor Delta_k Per Path]
    E0 --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Mason Numerator Sum]
    H0 --> I0[Transfer Function Assembly]
    E0 --> I0
    I0 --> J0[Final Transfer Function Output]
```

This schematic illustrates the conceptual *information flow* of a computational SFG solver: topology → adjacency → path/loop enumeration → touch analysis → determinants and cofactors → Mason assembly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Define a Signal Flow Graph. List any four properties of an SFG.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Signal Flow Graph (SFG) is a graphical representation of a system of simultaneous linear algebraic equations in which nodes represent system variables and directed branches represent the gain (transmittance) relating one variable to another. It is a topological alternative to the conventional block diagram.

**Four key properties:**

1. **Node property:** The value of any node equals the algebraic sum of all signals entering that node from incoming branches.
2. **Branch property:** A branch multiplies the signal at its tail node by the branch gain and delivers it to the head node.
3. **Path property:** A path is a continuous sequence of branches traversed in the direction of the arrows; no node is traversed more than once in a path.
4. **Loop property:** A loop is a closed path that starts and ends at the same node, crossing no other node more than once.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 for each property = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`
**State Mason's Gain Formula. Explain the terms $\Delta$ and $\Delta_k$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mason's Gain Formula:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

**Explanation of terms:**

- **$\Delta$ (Graph Determinant):** It is the determinant of the signal flow graph and is computed as
  $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$
  where $L_i$ are individual loop gains, and products $L_i L_j$ etc. are taken only over mutually non-touching loops.

- **$\Delta_k$ (Cofactor of $k^{th}$ forward path):** It is the value of $\Delta$ for the part of the SFG that does not touch the $k^{th}$ forward path $P_k$. All loops that touch $P_k$ are erased, and $\Delta_k$ is computed from the remaining (non-touching) loops only.

*(Award 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for $\Delta$ + 1 mark for $\Delta_k$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Draw the Signal Flow Graph for the block diagram shown below and identify all forward paths and feedback loops.

> Given Block Diagram (textual):
> - $R(s) \xrightarrow{\;G_1\;} X_1 \xrightarrow{\;G_2\;} X_2 \xrightarrow{\;G_3\;} X_3 \xrightarrow{\;G_4\;} C(s)$
> - Feedback $H_1$ from $X_2$ to $X_1$ (negative feedback).
> - Feedback $H_2$ from $X_3$ to $X_1$ (negative feedback).

**(b)** Apply Mason's Gain Formula to compute the transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — SFG Construction [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Nodes:**
$R,\ X_1,\ X_2,\ X_3,\ C$ (input, three intermediate, output).

**Step 2 — Branches:**

| Branch | From → To | Gain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R \to X_1$ | $G_1$ |
| 2 | $X_1 \to X_2$ | $G_2$ |
| 3 | $X_2 \to X_3$ | $G_3$ |
| 4 | $X_3 \to C$ | $G_4$ |
| 5 | $X_2 \to X_1$ | $-H_1$ (feedback) |
| 6 | $X_3 \to X_1$ | $-H_2$ (feedback) |

**Step 3 — SFG Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    R((R)) -->|G1| X1((X1))
    X1 -->|G2| X2((X2))
    X2 -->|G3| X3((X3))
    X3 -->|G4| C((C))
    X2 -->|H1| X1
    X3 -->|H2| X1
```

**Step 4 — Identify Forward Paths:**

Only **one** forward path exists: $R \to X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to C$ with gain

$$P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4$$

**Step 5 — Identify Loops:**

- **Loop $L_1$:** $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_1$, gain $L_1 = -G_2 H_1$.
- **Loop $L_2$:** $X_1 \to X_2 \to X_3 \to X_1$, gain $L_2 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$.

(Note: These two loops share nodes $X_1$ and $X_2$ → **touching**.)

---

**Part (b) — Mason's Gain Formula [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta$:**

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) = 1 - (-G_2 H_1) - (-G_2 G_3 H_2)$$

$$\Delta = 1 + G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 H_2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta_1$:**

Both $L_1$ and $L_2$ touch $P_1$ → erase all loops:

$$\Delta_1 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply MGF:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 H_2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 H_2}\;}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** For the SFG shown below, identify all forward paths, individual loops, and any non-touching loop pairs. Compute the determinant $\Delta$. **[7 marks]**

> **Given SFG:**
> - Forward Path 1: $R \to A \to B \to C$ with gain $G_1 G_2 G_3$.
> - Forward Path 2: $R \to A \to C$ with gain $G_1 G_4$.
> - Loop $L_1$: $A \to B \to A$ with gain $-G_2 H_1$.
> - Loop $L_2$: $C \to C$ self-loop with gain $-H_2$.

**(b)** Find the transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ using Mason's Gain Formula. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Element Enumeration [7 marks]**

**Forward Paths:**

$$P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3 \quad \text{(nodes: } R, A, B, C\text{)}$$

$$P_2 = G_1 G_4 \quad \text{(nodes: } R, A, C\text{)}$$

**Individual Loops:**

$$L_1 = -G_2 H_1 \quad \text{(nodes: } A, B\text{)}$$

$$L_2 = -H_2 \quad \text{(node: } C\text{)}$$

**Touch Analysis:**

| Pair | Common Nodes | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $L_1$ vs $L_2$ | None ($L_1$ uses $A,B$; $L_2$ uses only $C$) | **Non-touching** ✓ |
| $P_1$ vs $L_1$ | $A, B$ | Touching |
| $P_1$ vs $L_2$ | $C$ | Touching |
| $P_2$ vs $L_1$ | $A$ | Touching |
| $P_2$ vs $L_2$ | $C$ | Touching |

**Compute $\Delta$:**

- Sum of individual loops: $L_1 + L_2 = -G_2 H_1 - H_2$.
- Non-touching pair: $L_1 L_2 = (-G_2 H_1)(-H_2) = G_2 H_1 H_2$.

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + L_1 L_2$$

$$\Delta = 1 - (-G_2 H_1 - H_2) + G_2 H_1 H_2$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta = 1 + G_2 H_1 + H_2 + G_2 H_1 H_2\;}$$

---

**Part (b) — Mason's Gain Formula [7 marks]**

**Cofactor $\Delta_1$ (for $P_1$):**

Both $L_1$ and $L_2$ touch $P_1$ → all loops removed:

$$\Delta_1 = 1$$

**Cofactor $\Delta_2$ (for $P_2$):**

$L_1$ touches $P_2$ (at $A$); $L_2$ touches $P_2$ (at $C$) → all loops removed:

$$\Delta_2 = 1$$

**Apply MGF:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta}$$

$$= \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 (1) + G_1 G_4 (1)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + H_2 + G_2 H_1 H_2}$$

$$= \frac{G_1 (G_2 G_3 + G_4)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + H_2 + G_2 H_1 H_2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1 (G_2 G_3 + G_4)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + H_2 + G_2 H_1 H_2}\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting non-touching loop pairs** ($+ L_i L_j$ term): If two loops share no common node, students often miss the $+L_iL_j$ term, leading to a wrong $\Delta$ and incorrect transfer function.
> 2. **Incorrect touch analysis for $\Delta_k$**: When computing a cofactor $\Delta_k$, students frequently *include* loops that touch $P_k$, forgetting the "remove touching loops" rule.
> 3. **Missing forward paths**: Multi-path systems where a forward path bypasses intermediate nodes (like $P_2 = G_1 G_4$ in Question B) are often overlooked.
> 4. **Sign convention errors**: Drawing feedback as $H$ instead of $-H$ when the block diagram has a negative summing junction. Always trace the original block diagram's summing junction.
> 5. **Forgetting unity-gain branches**: When a block has implicit gain = 1 (e.g., direct connection between two take-off points), students occasionally omit this branch and break the topology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Signal Flow Graph (Module 1)**

- ✅ **SFG** = nodes (variables) + directed branches (gains). It is the *topological dual* of a block diagram.
- ✅ **Forward Path**: input → output without revisiting any node. Compute its gain $P_k$ as the product of branch gains along the path.
- ✅ **Loop**: closed path from a node back to itself, no node visited twice. Loop gain $L_i$ = product of branch gains.
- ✅ **Non-touching loops**: loops sharing **zero** common nodes. They produce $L_i L_j$ terms in $\Delta$.
- ✅ **Mason's Gain Formula**: $\;T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k\;$.
- ✅ **Graph Determinant**: $\;\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots\;$ — alternating signs.
- ✅ **Cofactor $\Delta_k$**: erase all loops that touch $P_k$, then re-evaluate $\Delta$ from the remaining loops.
- ✅ **Truncation rule**: If no $r+1$ mutually non-touching loops exist, all terms of order $\geq r+1$ vanish.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: Branch gains carry the sign of the physical system; do not blindly insert minus signs for feedback.
- ✅ **Verification trick**: Substitute $G_i = 1$, $H_j = 0$ to verify forward path; substitute forward paths to 0 and check $\Delta$.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip**: Always present the analysis in a tabular form (Forward Paths | Loops | Touch Matrix). Examiners reward structured presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mason's Gain Formula
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mason's Gain Formula

# Mason's Gain Formula (MGF) — Module 1 Premium Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mason's Gain Formula** is a systematic, topological (graph-based) analytical method developed by **Samuel Jefferson Mason (1953)** to determine the **overall transfer function** (or gain) between an **input node** and an **output node** of a **Signal Flow Graph (SFG)** without requiring the laborious reduction of the graph through block-diagram reduction rules.

Mathematically, the formula is expressed as:

$$T = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

Where:
- $T$ = Overall transfer function (gain) from input node $R(s)$ to output node $C(s)$
- $N$ = Total number of **forward paths** between input and output
- $P_k$ = **Transmittance** (gain) of the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path
- $\Delta$ = **Determinant** of the signal flow graph
- $\Delta_k$ = **Cofactor** corresponding to the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Mason's Formula as a "GPS Route Finder" for Signals:**
>
> Imagine you are at Point A (input) and want to reach Point Z (output) in a busy city with many interconnected roads. Some roads have tolls (gains), some roads form small circular routes (loops), and certain loops don't share any road with your chosen main route. Mason's formula tells you: *"Sum up all possible direct routes from A to Z, give extra credit (cofactor) when small city loops don't touch your route, and divide by the global 'traffic congestion factor' (the determinant)."*

### 1.3 Preliminary Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

> [!NOTE]
> Before applying MGF, the following SFG definitions are **mandatory per KTU syllabus**:
>
> - **Node**: Represents a system variable (signal). Drawn as a small circle labeled with the variable name.
> - **Branch**: A directed line segment connecting two nodes, representing a functional dependency.
> - **Transmittance (Gain)**: The gain (transfer function) associated with a branch, mathematically equivalent to $x_j = T_{ij} \cdot x_i$.
> - **Input Node (Source)**: A node with only outgoing branches (no incoming branches).
> - **Output Node (Sink)**: A node with only incoming branches (no outgoing branches).
> - **Forward Path**: A path from input to output that does **not cross any node more than once**.
> - **Feedback Loop**: A closed path that originates and terminates at the same node, crossing no other node twice.
> - **Non-Touching Loops**: Two (or more) loops that share **no common node** between them.

| Metric / Parameter | Standard Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Path Gain | $P_k$ | Product of all branch transmittances in the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path |
| Individual Loop Gain | $L_i$ | Product of all branch transmittances in the $i^{\text{th}}$ loop |
| Graph Determinant | $\Delta$ | Topological invariant of the entire SFG |
| Path Cofactor | $\Delta_k$ | Determinant of the subgraph that **does not touch** forward path $k$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Structure of Mason's Gain Formula

The complete expanded expression is:

$$T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

with the **graph determinant** $\Delta$ defined as:

$$\Delta = 1 - \sum_{i} L_i + \sum_{i,j} L_i L_j - \sum_{i,j,k} L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$$

### 2.2 Logical Steps to Apply MGF (Operational Algorithm)

The KTU examiner expects these steps **in order**:

1. **Construct** the Signal Flow Graph (SFG) from the given block diagram.
2. **Identify** all forward paths from input $R(s)$ to output $C(s)$. Compute the transmittance $P_k$ of each by multiplying branch gains along the path.
3. **Identify** all individual feedback loops. Compute the loop gain $L_i$ for each.
4. **Identify** all **pairs of non-touching loops**. Compute the product $L_i L_j$ for each such pair.
5. **Identify** all **triples of non-touching loops** (if any). Compute the product $L_i L_j L_k$ for each.
6. **Compute** the graph determinant $\Delta$ using the signed sum rule.
7. **Compute** the cofactor $\Delta_k$ for each forward path by evaluating $\Delta$ on the *reduced* SFG that excludes all nodes touching the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path.
8. **Substitute** into $T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum P_k \Delta_k$ to obtain the transfer function.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mason's Gain Formula | $T = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$ | Core equation — **must be memorized verbatim** |
| 2 | Forward Path Transmittance | $P_k = \prod_{j} a_j$ | Product of branch gains along path $k$ |
| 3 | Loop Gain | $L_i = \prod_{j} b_j$ | Product of branch gains around loop $i$ |
| 4 | Graph Determinant | $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$ | Alternating signs; "+" for even number of non-touching loops, "$-$" for odd |
| 5 | Cofactor of Path | $\Delta_k = 1 - \sum L_i^{(k)} + \sum L_i L_j^{(k)} - \cdots$ | Same expansion as $\Delta$, but loops touching path $k$ are excluded |
| 6 | Non-Touching Condition | $L_i \cap L_j = \emptyset$ | Loops must share **zero** common nodes |
| 7 | Total Number of Nodes | $n$ (no fixed relation) | Topology metric — appears in exam theory |
| 8 | Order of Determinant | Alternating series stops when all non-touching combinations are exhausted | Truncation is finite for any practical SFG |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance of Mason's Formula:**
>
> - Used in **VLSI circuit analysis** to evaluate complex multi-loop feedback amplifier gains.
> - Applied in **state-space realization** for converting state diagrams to transfer functions.
> - Essential in **digital filter design** (Direct Form II structures, lattice filters).
> - Forms the computational basis of **MATLAB/Simulink's `tf` and `connect` operations** internally.
> - Critical in **control system CAD tools** (e.g., MapleSim, Scilab/Xcos) for automated graph reduction.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Formula — Heuristic Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $\Delta$ exist?**
>
> In any feedback system, loops *feed back* signal onto themselves. When you "push" the input node, these loops keep circulating energy, modifying the effective input every instant. The determinant $\Delta$ mathematically captures this **closed-loop circulation effect**. When all loop gains tend to zero ($\sum L_i \to 0$), $\Delta \to 1$, and the system behaves like a pure feed-forward (open-loop) cascade — exactly as intuition dictates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Standard 2-Loop System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Find the transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ for the SFG shown with the following parameters:
> - Forward path: $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$
> - Loop 1: $L_1 = -G_2 H_1$ (inner feedback)
> - Loop 2: $L_2 = -G_3 H_2$ (outer feedback)
> - Loops 1 and 2 **touch** (share node $x_2$)
> - **No** non-touching loop pairs exist.

### Solution — Exhaustive Algebraic Walk-through

**Step 1:** Identify forward paths.

$$P_1 = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3 = G_1 G_2 G_3$$

Number of forward paths $N = 1$.

**Step 2:** Identify individual loop gains.

$$L_1 = -G_2 H_1 \quad ; \quad L_2 = -G_3 H_2$$

**Step 3:** Identify non-touching loop pairs.

Since $L_1$ and $L_2$ share the node $x_2$, they are **touching loops**.

$$\sum L_i L_j = 0$$

**Step 4:** No triples or higher-order non-touching combinations exist.

$$\sum L_i L_j L_k = 0$$

**Step 5:** Compute the graph determinant $\Delta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots \\
&= 1 - \left(L_1 + L_2\right) + 0 - 0 \\
&= 1 - \left(-G_2 H_1 - G_3 H_2\right) \\
\Delta &= 1 + G_2 H_1 + G_3 H_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the cofactor $\Delta_1$ for the only forward path.

Since the forward path $P_1$ traverses **all nodes** of the graph ($R, x_1, x_2, C$), removing those nodes eliminates **both loops**. Therefore:

$$\Delta_1 = 1$$

**Step 7:** Apply Mason's Gain Formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{1} P_k \Delta_k \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_3 H_2} \cdot \left(G_1 G_2 G_3\right) \cdot (1) \\
\therefore \quad T &= \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_3 H_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Non-Touching Loop Case (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** For an SFG with the following parameters, find $T = C(s)/R(s)$:
> - Forward path: $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4$
> - Loop 1: $L_1 = G_2 H_1$ (uses nodes $x_1, x_2$)
> - Loop 2: $L_2 = G_4 H_2$ (uses nodes $x_3, C$)
> - Loops 1 and 2 are **non-touching** (no common node).

### Solution — Exhaustive Walk-through

**Step 1:** Forward path transmittance.

$$P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4$$

**Step 2:** Individual loop gains.

$$L_1 = G_2 H_1 \quad ; \quad L_2 = G_4 H_2$$

**Step 3:** Non-touching loop pair.

$L_1$ and $L_2$ share no common node → they are non-touching.

$$L_1 L_2 = (G_2 H_1)(G_4 H_2) = G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2$$

**Step 4:** No triple combinations.

$$\sum L_i L_j L_k = 0$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\Delta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + (L_1 L_2) - 0 \\
&= 1 - (G_2 H_1 + G_4 H_2) + (G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2) \\
\Delta &= 1 - G_2 H_1 - G_4 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute cofactor $\Delta_1$.

The forward path uses **all** nodes of the graph. Removing them eliminates both loops.

$$\Delta_1 = 1$$

**Step 7:** Apply Mason's Gain Formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} = \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3 G_4}{1 - G_2 H_1 - G_4 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Multi-Forward-Path Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** SFG with two forward paths and a single loop:
> - Forward path 1: $P_1 = G_1 G_2$ (uses nodes $R, a, C$)
> - Forward path 2: $P_2 = G_3$ (uses nodes $R, C$ directly, skipping $a$)
> - Loop 1: $L_1 = G_1 H$ (uses nodes $R, a$)

### Solution

**Step 1:** List forward paths.

$$P_1 = G_1 G_2 \quad ; \quad P_2 = G_3$$

**Step 2:** Loop gain.

$$L_1 = G_1 H$$

**Step 3:** No non-touching pairs.

**Step 4:** Graph determinant.

$$\Delta = 1 - L_1 = 1 - G_1 H$$

**Step 5:** Cofactors.

For $P_1$: The forward path uses nodes $R, a, C$. The loop $L_1$ uses nodes $R, a$, which **touches** $P_1$. Thus $\Delta_1 = 1$.

For $P_2$: The forward path uses nodes $R, C$. The loop $L_1$ uses nodes $R, a$, which **touches** $P_2$ at node $R$. Thus $\Delta_2 = 1$.

**Step 6:** Apply MGF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{\Delta} \left( P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - G_1 H} \left( G_1 G_2 + G_3 \right) \\
\therefore \quad T &= \frac{G_1 G_2 + G_3}{1 - G_1 H}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Signal Flow Graph (Generic 2-Loop Topology)

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** of a typical multi-loop SFG used in KTU problems:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Rnode((R)) -->|G1| X1((X1))
    X1 -->|G2| X2((X2))
    X2 -->|G3| Cnode((C))
    Cnode -->|H2| X2
    X1 -->|H1| Rnode
    Rnode -->|G3 parallel| Cnode
```

### Topology Interpretation:

| Node ID | Role | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `Rnode` | Input (Source) | Reference signal $R(s)$ — only outgoing branches |
| `X1` | Intermediate node | Junction after first forward gain $G_1$ |
| `X2` | Intermediate node | Junction after second forward gain $G_2$ |
| `Cnode` | Output (Sink) | Controlled output $C(s)$ — only incoming branches |

### Branch Transmittance Legend:

| Branch | Transmittance | Path Type |
|---|---|---|
| `Rnode → X1` | $G_1$ | Forward |
| `X1 → X2` | $G_2$ | Forward |
| `X2 → Cnode` | $G_3$ | Forward |
| `X1 → Rnode` | $-H_1$ | Feedback (Loop $L_1$) |
| `Cnode → X2` | $-H_2$ | Feedback (Loop $L_2$) |
| `Rnode → Cnode` | $G_3$ | Parallel forward path |

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of MGF Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Given SFG] --> A1[Identify all nodes and branches]
    A1 --> A2[List all forward paths]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Pk for each path]
    A3 --> A4[List all individual loops]
    A4 --> A5[Compute each Li]
    A5 --> A6[Identify non-touching loop pairs]
    A6 --> A7[Identify non-touching loop triples]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Delta = 1 - sum Li + sum Li Lj - ...]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Delta_k for each forward path]
    A9 --> A10[Apply T = sum Pk Delta_k divided by Delta]
    A10 --> A11[Output Transfer Function T]
```

### Decision Logic for Cofactor Computation:

| Condition | Action |
|---|---|
| Forward path $P_k$ touches **all** loops | Set $\Delta_k = 1$ |
| Forward path $P_k$ touches **some** loops | Set those $L_i = 0$ in $\Delta$ expression |
| Forward path $P_k$ touches **no** loops | Use the full $\Delta$ expression as $\Delta_k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Trick:** Many students lose marks by miscounting forward paths. A reliable method is to **list all possible sequences from $R$ to $C$ that do not revisit any node**. For a graph with $n$ nodes, the maximum possible forward paths is bounded but can grow combinatorially with the graph's connectivity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State Mason's Gain Formula and explain the terms: forward path, loop, and non-touching loops.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

Mason's Gain Formula gives the overall transfer function of a signal flow graph as:

$$T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$

**Definitions:**

- **Forward Path:** A continuous path from the input node to the output node that does not cross any node more than once. Its transmittance $P_k$ is the product of all branch gains along the path.
- **Loop:** A closed path that begins and ends at the same node, crossing no other node more than once. Its gain $L_i$ is the product of all branch transmittances around it.
- **Non-Touching Loops:** Two or more loops that do not share any common node between them. They enable higher-order terms ($L_i L_j$, $L_i L_j L_k$) in the determinant expansion.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**What is a cofactor in Mason's Gain Formula? How is it computed?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The **cofactor $\Delta_k$** of the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path is the value of the graph determinant $\Delta$ computed on the subgraph obtained by **removing all nodes and branches that lie on (or touch) the $k^{\text{th}}$ forward path**.

Mathematically:

$$\Delta_k = 1 - \sum L_i^{(k)} + \sum L_i L_j^{(k)} - \cdots$$

where $L_i^{(k)}$ denotes loop gains of loops that **do not touch** forward path $k$. If every loop in the graph touches the forward path, then $\Delta_k = 1$.

---

## **PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice**

---

### **Question B1 (Option A — 14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** For the signal flow graph of a control system shown below, identify:
- Number of forward paths and their transmittances
- All individual loops and their gains
- All pairs of non-touching loops

**Given parameters:**
- Forward path: $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$
- Loop 1: $L_1 = -G_1 G_2 H_1$ (uses nodes $R, x_1, x_2$)
- Loop 2: $L_2 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$ (uses nodes $x_1, x_2, C$)
- Loop 3: $L_3 = -G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$ (uses nodes $R, x_1, x_2, C$)

**(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a):

**Forward Paths:**
- $N = 1$, $P_1 = G_1 G_2 G_3$ — **1 mark**

**Individual Loops:**
- $L_1 = -G_1 G_2 H_1$ — **1 mark**
- $L_2 = -G_2 G_3 H_2$ — **1 mark**
- $L_3 = -G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$ — **1 mark**

**Non-touching pairs:** Since $L_1, L_2, L_3$ all share nodes $x_1$ and/or $x_2$, **no non-touching pairs exist**.

$$\sum L_i L_j = 0 \quad \text{— 3 marks for correct identification}$$

**(b)** Using Mason's Gain Formula, compute the overall transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ for the same SFG.

**(7 marks)**

#### Solution (b):

**Step 1:** Graph determinant (no non-touching loops, so higher-order terms vanish):

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) = 1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 H_2 + G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3$$

**— 2 marks**

**Step 2:** Cofactor $\Delta_1$ for the only forward path $P_1$:

Since $P_1$ traverses all nodes, every loop touches it.

$$\Delta_1 = 1 \quad \text{— 1 mark}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Mason's Gain Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1}{\Delta} \\
&= \frac{G_1 G_2 G_3}{1 + G_1 G_2 H_1 + G_2 G_3 H_2 + G_1 G_2 G_3 H_3} \quad \text{— 4 marks}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B1 (Option B — 14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** An SFG has the following structure:
- Forward path 1: $P_1 = G_1 G_2$ (touches loop $L_1$ only)
- Forward path 2: $P_2 = G_3 G_4$ (touches loop $L_2$ only)
- Loop 1: $L_1 = -G_2 H_1$ (uses nodes $a, b$)
- Loop 2: $L_2 = -G_4 H_2$ (uses nodes $c, d$)
- Loops $L_1$ and $L_2$ are **non-touching** (no common node).

Compute the graph determinant $\Delta$ and the two cofactors $\Delta_1, \Delta_2$.

**(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a):

**Graph Determinant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + (L_1 L_2) \\
&= 1 - (-G_2 H_1 - G_4 H_2) + (-G_2 H_1)(-G_4 H_2) \\
&= 1 + G_2 H_1 + G_4 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2 \quad \text{— 4 marks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cofactor $\Delta_1$ (for $P_1$):**
- $P_1$ touches $L_1$ at node $b$ (output of $G_2$). So $L_1$ is eliminated.
- $P_1$ does **not** touch $L_2$ (uses different nodes $c, d$).
- Therefore: $\Delta_1 = 1 - L_2 = 1 + G_4 H_2$ **— 1.5 marks**

**Cofactor $\Delta_2$ (for $P_2$):**
- $P_2$ touches $L_2$ at node $d$ (output of $G_4$). So $L_2$ is eliminated.
- $P_2$ does **not** touch $L_1$.
- Therefore: $\Delta_2 = 1 - L_1 = 1 + G_2 H_1$ **— 1.5 marks**

**(b)** Apply Mason's Gain Formula to obtain $T = C(s)/R(s)$.

**(7 marks)**

#### Solution (b):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P_1 \Delta_1 + P_2 \Delta_2}{\Delta} \\
&= \frac{G_1 G_2 (1 + G_4 H_2) + G_3 G_4 (1 + G_2 H_1)}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_4 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2} \quad \text{— 7 marks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$T = \frac{G_1 G_2 + G_1 G_2 G_4 H_2 + G_3 G_4 + G_2 G_3 G_4 H_1}{1 + G_2 H_1 + G_4 H_2 + G_2 G_4 H_1 H_2}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 Marks Here):**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Loop Gains:** Many students forget that feedback loops with a *negative* summing junction give **negative** transmittance ($-H$ not $+H$). Double-check the block diagram sign convention before constructing the SFG.
> 2. **Misidentifying Non-Touching Loops:** Two loops are non-touching **only if they share zero nodes**. A single common node disqualifies them — even one shared branch makes them *touching*. Always list every node of each loop explicitly.
> 3. **Stopping $\Delta$ Expansion Too Early:** If three non-touching loops exist, the $\sum L_i L_j L_k$ term is **non-zero** and **must** be subtracted. Don't truncate at the second-order term unless proven to be zero.
> 4. **Forgetting $\Delta_k \neq 1$ When Path is Partial:** Many students blindly set $\Delta_k = 1$. Correct only when the forward path touches *every* loop. Otherwise, recompute $\Delta$ on the reduced graph.
> 5. **Missing Forward Paths:** Use a systematic node-by-node traversal. Each path must originate at $R$ and terminate at $C$ without revisiting any node.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Mason's Gain Formula (KTU Module 1):**
>
> - ✅ Mason's Formula: $T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$ — **must be memorized word-for-word**.
> - ✅ $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \cdots$ — alternating signs, **non-touching combinations only**.
> - ✅ Forward path: from input to output, no node repeated.
> - ✅ Loop: closed path, no node repeated (except start = end).
> - ✅ Non-touching loops: **zero** common nodes.
> - ✅ $\Delta_k$: determinant evaluated on the subgraph *not touching* forward path $k$.
> - ✅ If forward path $k$ touches **all** loops, then $\Delta_k = 1$.
> - ✅ Always verify the **sign convention** from the original block diagram before transferring gains to the SFG.
> - ✅ Number of forward paths $N$ can be greater than 1 — sum over **all** $P_k \Delta_k$.
> - ✅ Mason's formula gives the **complete transfer function in a single step**, bypassing all block-diagram reduction rules.
> - ✅ Use $\Delta$ formula systematically: identify $\sum L_i$ first, then $\sum L_i L_j$, then $\sum L_i L_j L_k$ — don't skip terms.
> - ✅ A path that revisits a node is **not** a valid forward path — exclude it.
> - ✅ The cofactor $\Delta_k$ is **path-specific** — recompute for each forward path independently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Test Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Test Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Signals** are a set of mathematically well-defined, deterministic, and reproducible input functions used as standardized inputs to evaluate, characterize, and compare the **time-domain** and **frequency-domain** behavior of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) control systems. These signals form the universal language through which the transient response, steady-state behavior, and overall performance of any control system can be predicted and benchmarked.

The five canonical standard test signals prescribed by the KTU 2024 Control Systems syllabus are:

1. **Unit Impulse Signal** — denoted as $\delta(t)$
2. **Unit Step Signal** — denoted as $u(t)$
3. **Unit Ramp Signal** — denoted as $r(t)$
4. **Unit Parabolic (Acceleration) Signal** — denoted as $p(t)$
5. **Unit Sinusoidal Signal** — denoted as $\sin(\omega t)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The unit is prefixed with "**Unit**" in all cases, meaning the **amplitude scaling factor equals 1**. Any non-unity amplitude $A$ simply multiplies the unit signal, e.g., $A \cdot u(t)$, $A \cdot t \cdot u(t)$, etc.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **mechanical engineer testing a brand-new car suspension** before selling it. You wouldn't just drive it around randomly — you'd push it with **standardized forces** to see how it responds:

- A **sudden, sharp tap** with a hammer = **Impulse signal** (testing for natural frequency / shock response)
- A **sudden step load** (jumping onto the bumper) = **Step signal** (testing for damping, overshoot, settling)
- A **slowly increasing push** (gradually applying weight) = **Ramp signal** (testing tracking ability for rising commands)
- A **constantly accelerating push** (pressing harder and harder) = **Parabolic signal** (testing tracking of accelerating commands)
- A **rhythmic, oscillating push** (vibrating the car) = **Sinusoidal signal** (testing resonance and frequency response)

In control systems engineering, we do the **same thing mathematically** — we feed the system these "engineered pushes" and observe how the **output** behaves. Because every engineer in the world uses the same test signals, results across labs, papers, and industries are **directly comparable**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Mapping Table for Student Memory:**
>
> | Test Signal | Physical Real-World Analogy |
> |:---:|:---|
> | **Impulse** | A hammer strike / lightning bolt |
> | **Step** | Sudden switch ON / sudden load applied |
> | **Ramp** | Gradually opening a valve |
> | **Parabolic** | Constantly accelerating a vehicle |
> | **Sinusoidal** | Vibration from an unbalanced motor |

---

### 1.3 Why Standard Test Signals? (The Engineering "Why")

1. **Reproducibility** — Two engineers in different countries get the same input shape, hence comparable results.
2. **System Characterization** — From the response to a step, we directly read **rise time, peak time, overshoot, settling time, steady-state error**.
3. **Mathematical Tractability** — All five signals have **simple, closed-form Laplace transforms**, enabling algebraic (rather than differential) analysis.
4. **Superposition Testing** — Any arbitrary input $x(t)$ can be expressed as a superposition of impulse signals (via convolution), so impulse response alone characterizes an LTI system completely.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — Step by Step

#### **A. Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$**

**Definition:** An ideal impulse is a pulse of infinitesimal duration and infinite amplitude whose **area (strength) is unity**.

**Time-domain Mathematical Form:**

$$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty & t = 0 \\ 0 & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$$

**Why this matters:** The impulse response $h(t)$ of an LTI system **fully characterizes the system**. Given $h(t)$, the response to **any** input $x(t)$ is:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Property (Key Property):** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(t)\, \delta(t - a)\, dt = f(a)$

#### **B. Unit Step Signal $u(t)$**

**Definition:** A signal that switches from **0 to 1 instantaneously** at $t = 0$ and remains at 1 forever.

**Time-domain Mathematical Form:**

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Why this matters:** The step response is the **most commonly used** test in industry. From it, we extract:
- **Maximum overshoot** $M_p$
- **Rise time** $t_r$
- **Settling time** $t_s$
- **Steady-state error** $e_{ss}$

#### **C. Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$**

**Definition:** A signal whose value **increases linearly** with time, beginning from zero at $t = 0$.

**Time-domain Mathematical Form:**

$$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} t & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Why this matters:** Ramp response reveals the system's ability to track a **constantly changing** reference — used in **servo position control** (e.g., antenna tracking a moving satellite).

#### **D. Unit Parabolic Signal $p(t)$**

**Definition:** A signal whose value increases as the **square of time** (i.e., constant acceleration).

**Time-domain Mathematical Form:**

$$p(t) = \frac{t^2}{2}\, u(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{t^2}{2} & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is **mandatory** in the KTU syllabus. Without the $\tfrac{1}{2}$, the Laplace transform would be $\tfrac{2}{s^3}$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{s^3}$. Always include the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for "Unit Parabolic."

#### **E. Unit Sinusoidal Signal**

**Time-domain Mathematical Form:**

$$x(t) = \sin(\omega t)\, u(t)$$

where $\omega$ is the **angular frequency in rad/s** (a standard metric).

**Why this matters:** Sinusoidal input yields a steady-state sinusoidal output (in LTI systems) — and the **ratio of output to input amplitudes** as a function of frequency gives the **frequency response** $M(\omega)$ and **phase** $\phi(\omega)$.

---

### 2.2 The Master Family Tree — Relationships Between Signals

The five signals are **not independent** — they are linked by integration and differentiation. This is a **favorite KTU 2-mark and 7-mark topic**.

> **Moving DOWN the family tree = INTEGRATION**
> **Moving UP the family tree = DIFFERENTIATION**

$$\boxed{\delta(t) \;\xrightarrow{\;\int\;}\; u(t) \;\xrightarrow{\;\int\;}\; r(t) \;\xrightarrow{\;\int\;}\; p(t)}$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\delta(t) = \dot{\delta}(t), \quad \frac{d}{dt}u(t) = \delta(t), \quad \frac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t), \quad \frac{d}{dt}p(t) = r(t)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**D**ifferentiate **U**p, **I**ntegrate **D**own" (DUID). Each differentiation moves the signal up the tree; each integration moves it down.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Signal Name | Time-Domain $x(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ | Laplace Transform $X(s)$ | Slope/Shape |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | Spike (area = 1) |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | Constant 1 |
| 3 | Unit Ramp | $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^{2}}$ | Line, slope = 1 |
| 4 | Unit Parabolic | $p(t) = \dfrac{t^{2}}{2}\, u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^{3}}$ | Quadratic curve |
| 5 | Unit Sinusoidal | $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$ | Oscillating |

| # | Key Identity | Formula |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Impulse-Step | $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}u(t)$ |
| 2 | Step-Ramp | $u(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}r(t)$ |
| 3 | Ramp-Parabolic | $r(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}p(t)$ |
| 4 | Impulse response → Any response | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ |
| 5 | Heaviside expansion | $u(t-a) = $ shifted step |
| 6 | Laplace of $f(t)\, u(t)$ | $\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\, u(t)\} = F(s)$ |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The table above uses `\dfrac` and `\omega` — no bare vertical pipe characters are used inside any table cell, preserving Mermaid/Markdown safety.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Test Signal Used | Purpose |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Aircraft Autopilot** | Step + Ramp | Test pitch/yaw tracking |
| **Hard Disk Drive Servo** | Impulse | Measure head-positioning natural frequency |
| **Cruise Control** | Step + Ramp | Speed-command tracking |
| **Audio Amplifier** | Sinusoidal (swept $\omega$) | Plot Bode magnitude plot |
| **Robotic Arm Trajectory** | Parabolic | Track accelerating motion profiles |
| **Vibration Isolation** | Impulse + Sinusoidal | Identify resonant peaks |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Laplace Transform of Each Standard Test Signal

---

#### **Derivation 1: Laplace Transform of Unit Step Signal $u(t)$**

**Starting definition:**

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1 & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Applying the Laplace integral definition:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} u(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Substituting $u(t) = 1$ in the integration range:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Evaluating the integral:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Applying the limits:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{e^{-s \cdot \infty}}{-s} - \frac{e^{-s \cdot 0}}{-s} = \frac{0}{-s} - \frac{1}{-s} = \frac{1}{s}$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}}$$

> **Convergence condition:** Valid for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$.

---

#### **Derivation 2: Laplace Transform of Unit Ramp Signal $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$**

**Starting definition:** $r(t) = t$ for $t \geq 0$.

**Applying the Laplace definition:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Using integration by parts** with $u = t$, $dv = e^{-st}\, dt$ so that $du = dt$, $v = \dfrac{-e^{-st}}{s}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \left[ t \cdot \frac{-e^{-st}}{s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{-e^{-st}}{s}\, dt$$

**Evaluating the boundary term:** At $t = \infty$, $t \cdot e^{-st} \to 0$ for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. At $t = 0$, the term is $0$.

$$\text{Boundary term} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

**Evaluating the integral term:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^{2}}$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s^{2}}}$$

---

#### **Derivation 3: Laplace Transform of Unit Parabolic Signal $p(t) = \dfrac{t^{2}}{2} u(t)$**

**Applying the Laplace definition:**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} u(t)\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t^{2}}{2}\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Pulling the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ out:**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} u(t)\right\} = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Using the standard formula** $\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{n} e^{-st}\, dt = \dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ for $n = 2$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{2}\, e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{2!}{s^{3}} = \frac{2}{s^{3}}$$

**Substituting back:**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} u(t)\right\} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{s^{3}} = \frac{1}{s^{3}}$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} u(t)\right\} = \frac{1}{s^{3}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor is critical:** Without it, the Laplace transform would be $\frac{2}{s^{3}}$, breaking the elegant "powers of $s$" pattern. The KTU convention deliberately uses the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor to keep $X(s) = s^{-n}$ for the $n$-th member of the family.

---

#### **Derivation 4: Laplace Transform of Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$**

**Starting from the sifting property:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\, \delta(t)\, dt = f(0)$$

**Setting $f(t) = e^{-st}$:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, \delta(t)\, dt = e^{-s \cdot 0} = 1$$

**By the Laplace definition (one-sided, but impulse is at $t = 0$, captured in both limits):**

$$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0^{-}}^{0^{+}} \delta(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = e^{0} = 1$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1}$$

---

#### **Derivation 5: Laplace Transform of Unit Sinusoidal $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$**

**Using Euler's identity:**

$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$

**Substituting into the Laplace integral:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Splitting and using** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{at}\} = \dfrac{1}{s - a}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

**Combining the fractions over a common denominator:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)}$$

**Simplifying numerator and denominator:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}} = \frac{\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}$$

**Final Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\, u(t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^{2} + \omega^{2}}}$$

---

### 3.2 The "Division-by-s" Rule (High-Yield KTU Shortcut)

> [!TIP]
> **Power Rule of Laplace for Standard Test Signals:** In the $s$-domain, **integration in time equals division by $s$**.
>
> $$\frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s^{n-1}} = \frac{1}{s^{n}}$$

**Application to verify family tree:**

Starting from impulse $X_1(s) = 1$, dividing by $s$ gives step $X_2(s) = \tfrac{1}{s}$, dividing again gives ramp $X_3(s) = \tfrac{1}{s^2}$, and so on.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Standard Test Signal Family Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DOUBLE IMPULSE dot delta t"] -->|"integrate dt"| B["UNIT IMPULSE delta t"]
    B -->|"integrate dt"| C["UNIT STEP u(t)"]
    C -->|"integrate dt"| D["UNIT RAMP r(t) = t"]
    D -->|"integrate dt"| E["UNIT PARABOLIC p(t) = t^2 / 2"]
    E -->|"integrate dt"| F["UNIT CUBIC t^3 / 6"]

    A1["LAPLACE DOMAIN"] --- A
    A1A["s^2"] --- A
    A1B["s"] --- B
    A1C["1 / s"] --- C
    A1D["1 / s^2"] --- D
    A1E["1 / s^3"] --- E
    A1F["1 / s^4"] --- F

    style A fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c80,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2a7,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#e0d4f4,stroke:#609,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f4d4e8,stroke:#936,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each **integration** step in the time domain (downward arrow) corresponds to a **division by $s$** in the Laplace domain (right column).

---

### 4.2 System Response Testing Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT["STANDARD TEST SIGNAL x(t)"] -->|"Laplace X(s)"| SYS["LTI SYSTEM G(s) = Num s / Den s"]
    SYS -->|"Laplace Y(s) = G(s) X(s)"| OUT["SYSTEM OUTPUT y(t)"]
    OUT -->|"Inverse Laplace Y(s) to y(t)"| ANALYSIS["PERFORMANCE METRICS: Rise Time, Overshoot, Settling Time, Steady State Error"]
    INPUT2["Impulse delta t"] --> SYS
    INPUT3["Step u(t)"] --> SYS
    INPUT4["Ramp r(t)"] --> SYS
    INPUT5["Parabolic p(t)"] --> SYS
    INPUT6["Sinusoidal sin wt"] --> SYS

    style INPUT fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c80,stroke-width:2px
    style SYS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:3px
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2a7,stroke-width:2px
    style ANALYSIS fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style INPUT2 fill:#fff,stroke:#888
    style INPUT3 fill:#fff,stroke:#888
    style INPUT4 fill:#fff,stroke:#888
    style INPUT5 fill:#fff,stroke:#888
    style INPUT6 fill:#fff,stroke:#888
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Any of the five test signals can be fed into the **same LTI system** $G(s)$. The output is computed as $Y(s) = G(s) \cdot X(s)$, then inverse Laplace transformed to obtain $y(t)$, from which all **time-domain performance specifications** are extracted.

---

### 4.3 Time-Domain Shapes of the Five Standard Signals

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["TIME DOMAIN SHAPES t >= 0"] --> B["Impulse: vertical spike at t=0, area 1"]
    A --> C["Step: jumps to 1 at t=0, stays flat"]
    A --> D["Ramp: 45 degree line through origin"]
    A --> E["Parabolic: upward opening curve, starts flat"]
    A --> F["Sinusoidal: oscillating wave between -1 and 1"]

    style B fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#c80
    style D fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2a7
    style E fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c
    style F fill:#e0d4f4,stroke:#609
```

---
