> - **Topic:** Routh Hurwitz Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ROUTH HURWITZ CRITERION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion** is a **necessary and sufficient algebraic condition** that determines whether all roots of the characteristic polynomial of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system possess **negative real parts**, i.e., whether the closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable**, without actually computing the roots of the polynomial.

For a general characteristic equation of order *n*:

$$
P(s) = a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + a_2 s^{n-2} + \cdots + a_{n-1} s + a_n = 0
$$

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion states that the system is **stable if and only if**:
1. All coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ are **real, finite, and have the same sign** (typically all positive for standard form).
2. **All elements of the first column of the Routh array are strictly positive** (no sign change).

The number of sign changes in the first column of the Routh array equals the **number of roots lying in the right-half of the s-plane (RHP)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Seesaw of Stability"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a tall **tower of stacked blocks** representing the poles (roots) of the system. For the tower to stand (system to be stable), **every single block must tilt slightly to the left** (have a negative real part). The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is like a **balance inspector** who checks the tower's stability *without looking at each block individually* — it just measures the *overall tilt trend* by inspecting a structured table. If the inspector finds that the tower's "tilt reading" flips direction at any row (a **sign change**), that flip corresponds to one block that is *leaning the wrong way* (a pole in the RHP). The number of direction flips = number of unstable poles.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic equation** is derived by setting the denominator polynomial of the closed-loop transfer function equal to zero: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.
- **Stability boundary** in the s-plane is the **imaginary axis** ($\sigma = 0$).
- The criterion yields results in terms of **count of unstable poles** — a **discrete integer metric** (no units).

### 1.4 Why This Criterion Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> Routh-Hurwitz is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in KTU Module 3. Expect direct 3-mark definition questions and a guaranteed 7 or 14-mark problem involving construction of the Routh array, identification of stability, and determining the range of a system parameter $K$ for stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Necessary Condition (Preliminary Check)

Before constructing the Routh array, verify:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All coefficients of the characteristic equation must be real, non-zero, and of the same sign (usually positive).**
> - If **any coefficient is zero or negative**, the system is **unstable** (or conditionally stable) — *do not waste time constructing the full Routh array*.
> - If **any coefficient is missing**, the system **may be unstable** (presence of odd powers of *s* may indicate RHP roots).

### 2.2 Construction of the Routh Array — Step-by-Step Logic

For a polynomial of degree **n**, build a table with **n + 1 rows**:

**Step 1 — Top two rows are filled directly from coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^n &\;\;|\;\; a_0 \quad a_2 \quad a_4 \quad a_6 \quad \cdots \\
s^{n-1} &\;\;|\;\; a_1 \quad a_3 \quad a_5 \quad a_7 \quad \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute subsequent rows using the determinant formula:**

For the third row ($s^{n-2}$):

$$
b_1 = \frac{a_1 a_2 - a_0 a_3}{a_1}, \qquad b_2 = \frac{a_1 a_4 - a_0 a_5}{a_1}, \qquad b_3 = \frac{a_1 a_6 - a_0 a_7}{a_1}
$$

In general form, an element in any new row is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{New Element} = \frac{(\text{First element of row above}) \times (\text{Element two rows above, same column}) - (\text{First element two rows above}) \times (\text{Element one row above, same column})}{\text{First element of row above}}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Continue the pattern until the $s^0$ row is reached.**

**Step 4 — Examine the FIRST COLUMN of the array. Count sign changes.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Characteristic equation | $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$ |
| 2 | Closed-loop denominator | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ |
| 3 | Necessary condition | All $a_i > 0$ (no zero/negative coefficient) |
| 4 | Routh array $b_j$ formula | $b_j = \dfrac{(a_1)(a_{2j}) - (a_0)(a_{2j+1})}{a_1}$ |
| 5 | General row element | $R_{ij} = \dfrac{R_{(i-1),0} \cdot R_{(i-2),j+1} - R_{(i-2),0} \cdot R_{(i-1),j+1}}{R_{(i-1),0}}$ |
| 6 | Stability condition | All first-column elements $> 0$ |
| 7 | Number of RHP poles | Number of sign changes in first column |
| 8 | Marginal stability | A row becomes all zeros — auxiliary polynomial method applies |
| 9 | Special Case 1: Zero in first column | Replace zero with small positive $\epsilon$ and apply limit $\epsilon \to 0^+$ |
| 10 | Special Case 2: Row of zeros | Form auxiliary polynomial from row above, take its derivative, replace zero row |
| 11 | Range of $K$ | Set Routh array element (typically $s^1$ or $s^0$ row) equal to zero and solve for $K$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace control systems** — Pre-flight stability check of autopilot loops.
> - **Power system stabilizers (PSS)** — Ensuring grid-connected generators do not oscillate.
> - **Process control in chemical plants** — Determining the safe range of controller gain $K_c$ for a PID-controlled reactor.
> - **Op-amp circuit design** — Checking stability of feedback amplifier stages.

### 2.5 Special Cases (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

> [!WARNING]
> **Case A — Zero in the First Column (but not entire row):**
> Substitute a small positive number $\epsilon$ in place of zero and continue. Take the limit as $\epsilon \to 0^+$ to determine the sign.

> [!WARNING]
> **Case B — Entire Row of Zeros:**
> 1. Form the **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ from the row above the zero row (using even powers of *s*).
> 2. Differentiate: $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
> 3. Replace the zero row with the coefficients of $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$.
> 4. The roots of $A(s) = 0$ are **symmetric about the origin** and represent marginally stable poles ($j\omega$ axis or symmetric RHP/LHP pairs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example — Stability Test for a 4th Order System

**Problem:** Determine the stability of the system whose characteristic equation is:

$$
s^4 + 2 s^3 + 3 s^2 + 4 s + 5 = 0
$$

**Solution — Step 1: Verify the necessary condition.**

All coefficients are: $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ — all **real, non-zero, and positive**. ✔ The necessary condition is satisfied. Proceed to construct the Routh array.

**Step 2: Construct the Routh array.**

Top two rows are filled directly:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | 2 | 4 | 0 |

**Step 3: Compute the $s^2$ row.**

$$
b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(4)}{2} = \frac{6 - 4}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1
$$

$$
b_2 = \frac{(2)(5) - (1)(0)}{2} = \frac{10 - 0}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5
$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | 2 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 1 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 4: Compute the $s^1$ row.**

$$
c_1 = \frac{(1)(4) - (2)(5)}{1} = \frac{4 - 10}{1} = -6
$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | 2 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 1 | 5 | 0 |
| $s^1$ | −6 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 5: Compute the $s^0$ row.**

$$
d_1 = \frac{(-6)(5) - (1)(0)}{-6} = \frac{-30}{-6} = 5
$$

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| $s^3$ | 2 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 1 | 5 | 0 |
| $s^1$ | −6 | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 5 |   |   |

**Step 6: Examine the first column and count sign changes.**

First column: $1, \; 2, \; 1, \; -6, \; 5$

| Transition | Sign | Change? |
|------------|------|---------|
| $1 \to 2$ | $(+) \to (+)$ | No |
| $2 \to 1$ | $(+) \to (+)$ | No |
| $1 \to -6$ | $(+) \to (-)$ | **YES (1st sign change)** |
| $-6 \to 5$ | $(-) \to (+)$ | **YES (2nd sign change)** |

**Total sign changes = 2.**

**Step 7: Conclusion.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The system has **2 poles in the right-half plane (RHP)**. Therefore, the closed-loop system is **UNSTABLE**.

---

### 3.2 Second Worked Example — Finding the Range of $K$ for Stability

**Problem:** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback system is:

$$
G(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}
$$

Determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable.

**Step 1: Form the characteristic equation.**

$$
1 + G(s) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + \frac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)} = 0
$$

$$
s(s+1)(s+2) + K = 0
$$

Expanding $s(s+1)(s+2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s)(s+1)(s+2) &= s \cdot (s^2 + 3s + 2) \\
&= s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s
\end{aligned}
$$

So the characteristic equation is:

$$
s^3 + 3s^2 + 2s + K = 0
$$

**Step 2: Verify necessary condition.**

All coefficients $1, 3, 2, K$ must be positive, so we require $K > 0$.

**Step 3: Construct the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 2 |
| $s^2$ | 3 | K |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{3(2) - 1(K)}{3} = \dfrac{6-K}{3}$ | 0 |
| $s^0$ | K |   |

**Step 4: Apply the stability condition (first column > 0).**

- Row $s^3$: $1 > 0$ ✔
- Row $s^2$: $3 > 0$ ✔
- Row $s^1$: $\dfrac{6-K}{3} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 6$
- Row $s^0$: $K > 0$

**Step 5: Combine the inequalities.**

$$
\boxed{\;0 < K < 6\;}
$$

**Step 6: Verify the boundary cases.**

- At $K = 0$: characteristic equation becomes $s(s^2 + 3s + 2) = 0$, giving a pole at the **origin** — **marginally unstable**.
- At $K = 6$: $\dfrac{6-K}{3} = 0$ — a row becomes zero — **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation on $j\omega$-axis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The closed-loop system is **stable for $0 < K < 6$**.

---

### 3.3 Special Case — Entire Row of Zeros (Auxiliary Polynomial Method)

**Problem:** Determine stability of the system with characteristic equation:

$$
s^5 + 2s^4 + 24s^3 + 48s^2 - 25s - 50 = 0
$$

**Step 1: Notice the sign change in coefficients** (−25, −50 are negative). The system is **immediately unstable** — but let's still demonstrate the method.

**Step 2: Construct the Routh array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^5$ | 1 | 24 | −25 |
| $s^4$ | 2 | 48 | −50 |
| $s^3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3: Entire row of zeros detected at $s^3$.** Form auxiliary polynomial from $s^4$ row:

$$
A(s) = 2s^4 + 48s^2 - 50
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dA(s)}{ds} = 8s^3 + 96s
$$

**Step 4: Replace the zero row with coefficients of $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$:**

$$
8s^3 + 0 \cdot s^2 + 96s + 0
$$

New $s^3$ row: $8, \; 96, \; 0$

**Step 5: Continue the array.**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $s^5$ | 1 | 24 | −25 |
| $s^4$ | 2 | 48 | −50 |
| $s^3$ | 8 | 96 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{8(48) - 2(96)}{8} = \dfrac{384-192}{8} = 24$ | $\dfrac{8(-50) - 2(0)}{8} = -50$ |   |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{24(96) - 8(-50)}{24} = \dfrac{2304 + 400}{24} = 112.5$ |   |   |
| $s^0$ | −50 |   |   |

**First column:** $1, 2, 8, 24, 112.5, -50$ — sign changes: $112.5 \to -50$ (1 sign change) → **1 pole in RHP**, system is **unstable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Routh Array Construction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Characteristic Equation of degree n] --> B[Check Necessary Condition: All coefficients positive and non-zero]
    B -->|Fail| C[System is UNSTABLE - STOP]
    B -->|Pass| D[Write first two rows s^n and s to power n-1 from coefficients]
    D --> E[Compute s to power n-2 row using determinant formula]
    E --> F{Next row computation required?}
    F -->|Yes| E
    F -->|No| G[Reach s to power 0 row]
    G --> H{Any row of ZEROS detected?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Form Auxiliary Polynomial A of s from row above]
    I --> J[Compute dA by ds]
    J --> K[Replace zero row with dA by ds coefficients]
    K --> E
    H -->|No| L{Any ZERO in first column only?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Replace zero with small epsilon and apply limit]
    M --> E
    L -->|No| N[Examine FIRST COLUMN of array]
    N --> O[Count number of sign changes in first column]
    O --> P[Number of sign changes = Number of poles in RHP]
    P --> Q{All first column elements positive?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[System is STABLE]
    Q -->|No| S[System is UNSTABLE]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Stability Outcome (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Closed Loop Transfer Function T of s] --> B[Form Characteristic Equation: Denominator = 0]
    B --> C[Routh Array Construction]
    C --> D{First Column Analysis}
    D -->|All Positive| E[STABLE: No RHP poles]
    D -->|One Sign Change| F[UNSTABLE: 1 RHP pole]
    D -->|Two Sign Changes| G[UNSTABLE: 2 RHP poles]
    D -->|k Sign Changes| H[UNSTABLE: k RHP poles]
    D -->|Zero Element in First Column| I[MARGINAL: Purely imaginary poles possible]
```

### 4.3 Stability Region in s-Plane (Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complex s-plane] --> B[Imaginary Axis j omega - Stability Boundary]
    B --> C[Left Half Plane LHP - Stable Region negative real part]
    B --> D[Right Half Plane RHP - Unstable Region positive real part]
    D --> E[Routh Hurwitz identifies RHP poles by sign changes]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. What is the necessary condition for stability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a mathematical test used to determine the **absolute stability** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system by examining the coefficients of the characteristic equation $a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n = 0$.

**Necessary condition:** All coefficients of the characteristic equation must be **real, finite, non-zero, and of the same sign** (typically positive). If any coefficient is zero, negative, or missing, the system is **unstable** or only marginally stable.

**Sufficient condition:** When constructing the Routh array, **all elements in the first column must be strictly positive** (no sign changes). The number of sign changes in the first column equals the number of roots in the right-half plane (RHP).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "special case" in the Routh array? Explain briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Special cases in the Routh array construction arise when the standard algorithm cannot be directly continued. There are two main special cases:

1. **Zero in the first column (but not the entire row):** Replace the zero with a small positive value $\epsilon$ and continue. Apply the limit $\epsilon \to 0^+$ to determine the sign behavior.

2. **Entire row of zeros:** This indicates the presence of **symmetrical roots** about the origin (either on the $j\omega$-axis or as mirror pairs in LHP/RHP). The remedy is to form an **auxiliary polynomial** $A(s)$ from the row immediately above the zero row (containing only even powers of *s*), then differentiate it as $\dfrac{dA(s)}{ds}$ and replace the zero row with the coefficients of this derivative.

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Construct the Routh array for the system whose characteristic equation is:

$$
s^4 + 6s^3 + 11s^2 + 6s + K = 0
$$

Determine the range of $K$ for which the system is stable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system with characteristic equation $s^4 + 3s^3 + 2s^2 + s + 1 = 0$, determine stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. Identify the number of poles in the RHP, if any. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Step-by-step (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Verify necessary condition. All coefficients are positive **provided $K > 0$**.

**Step 2:** Construct the Routh array.

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 11 | K |
| $s^3$ | 6 | 6 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{6(11) - 1(6)}{6} = \dfrac{66-6}{6} = 10$ | $\dfrac{6(K) - 1(0)}{6} = K$ |   |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{10(6) - 6(K)}{10} = \dfrac{60-6K}{10}$ |   |   |
| $s^0$ | K |   |   |

**Step 3:** Apply stability condition (first column > 0).

- $1 > 0$ ✔
- $6 > 0$ ✔
- $10 > 0$ ✔
- $\dfrac{60-6K}{10} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 10$
- $K > 0$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$
\boxed{\;0 < K < 10\;}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-step (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Routh array for $s^4 + 3s^3 + 2s^2 + s + 1 = 0$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| $s^3$ | 3 | 1 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{3(2) - 1(1)}{3} = \dfrac{5}{3}$ | $\dfrac{3(1) - 1(0)}{3} = 1$ |   |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{(5/3)(1) - 3(1)}{5/3} = \dfrac{5/3 - 3}{5/3} = \dfrac{-4/3}{5/3} = -\dfrac{4}{5}$ |   |   |
| $s^0$ | 1 |   |   |

**Step 2:** First column: $1, 3, \dfrac{5}{3}, -\dfrac{4}{5}, 1$

Sign changes:
- $\dfrac{5}{3} \to -\dfrac{4}{5}$: $(+) \to (-)$ — **1st sign change**
- $-\dfrac{4}{5} \to 1$: $(-) \to (+)$ — **2nd sign change**

**Step 3:** Number of sign changes = 2 → **2 poles in RHP** → **System is UNSTABLE**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** The open-loop transfer function of a unity feedback control system is $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+4)(s+6)}$. Find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. Comment on the marginal stability case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine the stability of a system with characteristic equation $2s^4 + s^3 + 3s^2 + 5s + 10 = 0$. How many poles lie in the RHP? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Step-by-step (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Characteristic equation: $s(s+4)(s+6) + K = 0$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(s+4)(s+6) &= s(s^2 + 10s + 24) \\
&= s^3 + 10s^2 + 24s
\end{aligned}
$$

So: $s^3 + 10s^2 + 24s + K = 0$

**Step 2:** Routh array:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|-----|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 24 |
| $s^2$ | 10 | K |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{10(24) - 1(K)}{10} = \dfrac{240 - K}{10}$ |   |
| $s^0$ | K |   |

**Step 3:** Stability constraints:
- $K > 0$
- $240 - K > 0 \;\Rightarrow\; K < 240$

$$
\boxed{\;0 < K < 240\;}
$$

**Step 4:** Marginal stability at $K = 240$: The characteristic equation becomes $s^3 + 10s^2 + 24s + 240 = 0$. Since $s = -10$ is a root, factor: $(s+10)(s^2 + 24) = 0$, giving purely imaginary poles at $s = \pm j\sqrt{24} = \pm j 2\sqrt{6}$. The system exhibits **sustained oscillations**.

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-step (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Routh array for $2s^4 + s^3 + 3s^2 + 5s + 10 = 0$:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^4$ | 2 | 3 | 10 |
| $s^3$ | 1 | 5 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{1(3) - 2(5)}{1} = -7$ | $\dfrac{1(10) - 2(0)}{1} = 10$ |   |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{-7(5) - 1(10)}{-7} = \dfrac{-45}{-7} = \dfrac{45}{7}$ |   |   |
| $s^0$ | 10 |   |   |

**Step 2:** First column: $2, 1, -7, \dfrac{45}{7}, 10$

Sign changes:
- $1 \to -7$: $(+) \to (-)$ — **1st sign change**
- $-7 \to \dfrac{45}{7}$: $(-) \to (+)$ — **2nd sign change**

**Step 3:** Number of RHP poles = **2** → System is **UNSTABLE**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the necessary condition first** — losing 1 mark for skipping the coefficient positivity check.
> 2. **Sign error in the Routh determinant formula** — the formula is $\frac{(P)(Q) - (R)(S)}{P}$ where P is the element directly above and two rows above; students often swap the multiplications.
> 3. **Forgetting to handle the special case of a row of zeros** — when a row of zeros appears, simply writing "system is unstable" without using the auxiliary polynomial loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **For marginal stability problems (range of $K$)**, examiners expect you to **state the boundary case explicitly** and **comment on the nature of marginal stability** (sustained oscillation / pole at origin).
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the number of sign changes** in your final answer — always write "*Number of RHP poles = N*".

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The Routh-Hurwitz criterion is a **necessary and sufficient** algebraic test for stability of LTI systems.
- ✅ **Necessary condition:** all coefficients of the characteristic polynomial must be **real, non-zero, and have the same sign** (usually positive).
- ✅ **Routh array** has `n + 1` rows; top two rows are filled directly from the polynomial coefficients.
- ✅ Subsequent rows are computed using the **determinant-style cross-multiplication formula**:
$$\text{New Element} = \frac{(\text{Element above}) \times (\text{Element two rows above}) - (\text{Element above two rows}) \times (\text{Element above, next column})}{(\text{Element above})}$$
- ✅ **Stability** ⟺ all first-column elements are positive (no sign change).
- ✅ **Number of sign changes in first column = number of poles in the RHP** (unstable roots).
- ✅ **Special Case 1 (zero in first column only):** replace with $\epsilon \to 0^+$.
- ✅ **Special Case 2 (entire row of zeros):** form auxiliary polynomial, take its derivative, replace zero row.
- ✅ For **range of $K$** problems, set the critical Routh array element to zero and solve for $K$.
- ✅ **Marginal stability** occurs when a row becomes zero — the system oscillates at constant amplitude.
- ✅ For **unity negative feedback**, the characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Root Locus Techniques: Introduction, Properties and Its Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Root Locus Techniques: Introduction, Properties and Its Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Root Locus** is the graphical trajectory (path) traced in the complex s-plane by the closed-loop poles of a feedback control system as a specific system parameter — typically the open-loop gain $K$ — is varied from **$0$ to $+\infty$**, while all other system parameters remain constant. The technique was formalized by **Walter R. Evans** in **1948** and is formally known as **Evans' Root Locus Method**.

For a standard unity negative feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$, the characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

The root locus is the locus of all points $s$ in the s-plane that satisfy the **magnitude condition** and the **angle condition** simultaneously:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angle Condition (Primary Criterion):**
> $$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm 180^\circ (2q+1), \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$
>
> **Magnitude Condition (Secondary, used to find $K$):**
> $$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1$$

The **angle condition alone** determines the loci (the path), while the **magnitude condition** is used to determine the value of $K$ at any point on the locus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Root locus is the path traced by the closed-loop poles in the s-plane as the system gain $K$ is varied from $0$ to $\infty$. It provides a graphical method for analyzing the transient response and stability of a control system without solving the characteristic equation repeatedly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing on a **hillside at dusk** with a **flashlight** strapped to your head. As you slowly walk forward (this represents increasing gain $K$ from $0 \to \infty$), the **shadow of a tree on the ground** moves along a specific path. The **tree's position** represents the **open-loop pole**, the **light source (your head)** represents the **controller/gain**, and the **shadow's path** is the **root locus**.

Why is this useful?

- If you know the **path of the shadow**, you can predict **where the tree's image (the closed-loop pole) will be** at any future instant.
- In a control system, the **closed-loop poles** dictate the **stability, speed of response, damping, and oscillation** of the system.
- The root locus gives you a **complete map** of pole locations for *all possible gain values* in a single picture — a stunning **engineering efficiency**.

### Why Engineers Use Root Locus

1. **Design**: To choose a gain $K$ that gives desirable transient response.
2. **Stability Analysis**: To immediately see if poles enter the right-half plane (instability).
3. **Compensation**: To visualize the effect of adding controllers (poles/zeros) before actually building the system.
4. **Speed**: Far quicker than solving a 5th-order polynomial 100 times by hand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on *construction rules*, *asymptote calculations*, and *breakaway points*. Memorize the **9 standard construction rules** — they appear almost every semester.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Range/Value |
|---|---|---|
| Gain | $K$ | $0 \leq K \leq \infty$ |
| Number of poles | $n$ | Determined by $G(s)H(s)$ |
| Number of zeros | $m$ | Determined by $G(s)H(s)$ |
| Asymptote angles | $\phi_A$ | Multiples of $\frac{180^\circ}{n-m}$ |
| Centroid | $\sigma_A$ | Real-valued scalar |
| Angle of departure | $\phi_d$ | Measured at complex pole |
| Angle of arrival | $\phi_a$ | Measured at complex zero |
| Stability boundary | $s = j\omega$ | $K = K_{critical}$ from Routh array |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logic of Root Locus Construction

The construction of a root locus follows a **systematic, step-by-step logic** rooted in the angle and magnitude conditions. Below is the operational breakdown.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

1. **Form the open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$ in **factored pole-zero form**:
   $$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s-z_1)(s-z_2)\cdots(s-z_m)}{(s-p_1)(s-p_2)\cdots(s-p_n)}$$
   where $K > 0$ is the parameter of interest.

2. **Write the characteristic equation**:
   $$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \implies (s-p_1)(s-p_2)\cdots(s-p_n) + K(s-z_1)\cdots(s-z_m) = 0$$

3. **Identify the angle and magnitude conditions** (the dual pillars of the method).
   - **Angle condition** dictates which points $s$ lie on the locus.
   - **Magnitude condition** quantifies the value of $K$ at each point on the locus.

4. **Apply the 9 construction rules** to sketch the locus without plotting every single point.

5. **Refine critical regions** (near $j\omega$-axis, near breakaway, near complex poles) using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion and angle of departure/arrival formulas.

6. **Calibrate $K$ values** at important points (e.g., $j\omega$-crossing, breakaway) using the magnitude condition.

## 2.2 The 9 Construction Rules (KTU High-Yield)

| Rule # | Rule Name | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Insight |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Number of Branches** | Number of branches = $\max(n, m)$ | Each branch starts at a pole (or zero if $m > n$) |
| **2** | **Symmetry** | Locus is symmetric about the real axis | Complex poles appear in conjugate pairs |
| **3** | **Starting & Ending Points** | Starts ($K=0$): at open-loop **poles** $p_1 \ldots p_n$. Ends ($K=\infty$): at open-loop **zeros** $z_1 \ldots z_m$, or $\infty$ | If $n > m$, $(n-m)$ branches go to infinity |
| **4** | **Real Axis Segments** | A point on the real axis is on the locus iff the **total number of real poles and zeros to its right is odd** | Simple, quick test for sketching |
| **5** | **Asymptotes** | Angles: $\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1)180^\circ}{n-m}$, $q = 0, 1, \ldots, n-m-1$. Centroid: $\sigma_A = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n-m}$ | Branches at infinity converge to these lines |
| **6** | **Breakaway & Break-in Points** | $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$ at the breakaway/break-in point, where $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)}$ | Multiple roots of the characteristic equation |
| **7** | **$j\omega$-Axis Crossing** | Found using **Routh-Hurwitz**: the auxiliary row gives the crossing frequency $\omega_c$ | Determines **critical gain** $K_{max}$ for stability |
| **8** | **Angle of Departure** $\phi_d$ | $\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$ (sum of angles from zeros and other poles to the departing pole) | Tells the direction in which the locus leaves a complex pole |
| **9** | **Angle of Arrival** $\phi_a$ | $\phi_a = 180^\circ - \sum \phi_z + \sum \phi_p$ (sum of angles from poles and other zeros to the arriving zero) | Tells the direction in which the locus reaches a complex zero |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Examination** (Memorize These):

### Centroid Formula

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} z_j}{n - m}$$

### Asymptote Angle Formula

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{n-m}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-m-1$$

### Breakaway / Break-in Point Equation

Differentiate $K = -\dfrac{1}{G(s)H(s)}$ and set $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$:

$$\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{1}{G(s)H(s)}\right] = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{d}{ds}[G(s)H(s)] = 0 \text{ (when } G(s)H(s) \text{ is polynomial ratio)}$$

### Angle of Departure from Complex Pole $p_k$

$$\phi_d = 180^\circ + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \angle(p_k - z_j) - \sum_{\substack{i=1 \\ i \neq k}}^{n} \angle(p_k - p_i)$$

### Angle of Arrival at Complex Zero $z_k$

$$\phi_a = 180^\circ - \sum_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq k}}^{m} \angle(z_k - z_j) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \angle(z_k - p_i)$$

### Value of $K$ at Any Point on the Locus

$$K = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert s - p_i \vert}{\prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert s - z_j \vert}$$

### Critical Gain from Routh Array (for $j\omega$-crossing)

When the auxiliary row becomes zero, the auxiliary equation gives $\omega_c$; substitute into the characteristic equation to find $K_{max}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | How Root Locus Helps |
|---|---|
| **Servo Motor Positioning** | Choose $K$ for fast, non-oscillatory response |
| **Aircraft Autopilot Design** | Ensure stability margins across all flight regimes |
| **Cruise Control Systems** | Avoid overshoot while maintaining quick response |
| **Robotic Arm Control** | Place dominant poles for desired damping ratio $\zeta$ |
| **Power System Stabilizers** | Tune $K$ to dampen electromechanical oscillations |
| **DC Motor Speed Control** | Predict instability before physical implementation |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Angle and Magnitude Conditions

Consider the standard unity negative feedback system with characteristic equation:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \implies G(s)H(s) = -1$$

The right-hand side $-1$ can be represented in polar form as:

$$-1 = 1 \cdot e^{j(2q+1)\pi}, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

Substituting the factored form $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K \prod (s - z_j)}{\prod (s - p_i)}$ and equating **angles** and **magnitudes** on both sides:

### Angle Condition Derivation

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \angle(s - z_j) - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \angle(s - p_i) = (2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ$$

### Magnitude Condition Derivation

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = \frac{K \prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert s - z_j \vert}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert s - p_i \vert} = 1$$

Solving for $K$:

$$K = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert s - p_i \vert}{\prod_{j=1}^{m} \vert s - z_j \vert}$$

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example: Complete Root Locus Construction

**Given:** Open-loop transfer function

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

**Task:** Construct the complete root locus and find: (a) centroid & asymptotes, (b) breakaway points, (c) $j\omega$-axis crossing, (d) range of $K$ for stability.

### Step 1: Identify Poles and Zeros

- **Poles:** $p_1 = 0$, $p_2 = -2$, $p_3 = -4 \implies n = 3$
- **Zeros:** None $\implies m = 0$

### Step 2: Apply Rule 1 — Number of Branches

$$\text{Number of branches} = \max(n, m) = \max(3, 0) = 3$$

### Step 3: Apply Rule 4 — Real Axis Segments

The real axis is part of the locus wherever the number of real poles and zeros to the **right** of the test point is **odd**:

| Test Interval | Poles/Zeros to Right | Count | On Locus? |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| $(-\infty, -4)$ | $p_1, p_2, p_3$ | 3 (odd) | ✅ Yes |
| $(-4, -2)$ | $p_1, p_2$ | 2 (even) | ❌ No |
| $(-2, 0)$ | $p_1$ | 1 (odd) | ✅ Yes |
| $(0, +\infty)$ | None | 0 (even) | ❌ No |

**Conclusion:** Locus exists on $(-\infty, -4)$ and $(-2, 0)$.

### Step 4: Apply Rule 5 — Asymptotes

Since $n - m = 3 - 0 = 3$:

**Asymptote Angles** (with $q = 0, 1, 2$):

$$\phi_A = \frac{(2q+1) \cdot 180^\circ}{3}$$

- $q = 0$: $\phi_A = 60^\circ$
- $q = 1$: $\phi_A = 180^\circ$
- $q = 2$: $\phi_A = 300^\circ$ (or $-60^\circ$)

**Centroid:**

$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum p_i - \sum z_j}{n - m} = \frac{(0 + (-2) + (-4)) - 0}{3} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

### Step 5: Apply Rule 6 — Breakaway Points

The characteristic equation:

$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0 \implies s(s+2)(s+4) + K = 0$$

Expanding:

$$s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$$

Solve for $K$:

$$K = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)$$

Differentiate with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

$$3s^2 + 12s + 8 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm 4\sqrt{3}}{6} = -2 \pm \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}$$

So:

$$s_1 = -2 + 1.155 = -0.845 \quad (\text{in valid range } (-2, 0)) \;\;\checkmark$$
$$s_2 = -2 - 1.155 = -3.155 \quad (\text{on real axis segment } (-\infty, -4)? \;\;\text{No — invalid})$$

**Breakaway point:** $s = -0.845$ ✅

### Step 6: Apply Rule 7 — $j\omega$-Axis Crossing (Routh-Hurwitz)

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$

**Routh Array:**

| | | |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\frac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

**For marginal stability** (auxiliary row becomes zero): $\frac{48 - K}{6} = 0 \implies K = 48$

**Auxiliary equation** (from $s^2$ row): $6s^2 + K = 0 \implies 6s^2 + 48 = 0 \implies s^2 = -8 \implies s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$

**Critical gain:** $K_{max} = 48$ at $\omega_c = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ rad/s

### Step 7: Range of $K$ for Stability

From the Routh array, for stability we need all first-column entries positive:

- $K > 0$
- $\frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \implies K < 48$

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

### Step 8: Value of $K$ at Breakaway Point

$$K = -(s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s)\Big\vert_{s = -0.845}$$

$$s^3 = -0.603, \quad 6s^2 = 4.284, \quad 8s = -6.760$$

$$K = -(-0.603 + 4.284 - 6.760) = -( -3.079) = 3.079$$

**Check:** $K_{breakaway} \approx 3.08$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Calculation | Result | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $n - m$ | $3$ | Three asymptote directions |
| $\sigma_A$ | $-2$ | Centroid on real axis |
| Asymptote angles | $60^\circ, 180^\circ, 300^\circ$ | Direction of branches at infinity |
| Breakaway point | $s = -0.845$ | Locus leaves real axis here |
| $K$ at breakaway | $\approx 3.08$ | Threshold for complex pole formation |
| $j\omega$ crossing | $s = \pm j 2\sqrt{2}$ | Stability boundary |
| Critical $K$ | $K = 48$ | Maximum gain for stability |
| Stability range | $0 < K < 48$ | System stable only in this range |
