- The fisherman always knows the starting count ($P$) and the count of encirclements ($N$), so he predicts $Z$ using the simple rule: $N = Z - P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recognition Point for KTU Exams:** Bode plot gives *relative stability* (how stable), while Nyquist gives *absolute stability* (stable or not) and exact count of unstable closed-loop poles. Both operate on the **open-loop** transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ — the loop is *not* physically closed during the test.

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|------|--------|------|------------|
| Gain Crossover Frequency | $\omega_{gc}$ | **rad/s** | Frequency at which magnitude plot crosses **0 dB** |
| Phase Crossover Frequency | $\omega_{pc}$ | **rad/s** | Frequency at which phase plot crosses $-180^\circ$ |
| Gain Margin | $GM$ | **dB** | Negative of magnitude in dB at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| Phase Margin | $PM$ | **degrees** | $180^\circ$ plus phase angle at $\omega_{gc}$ |
| Number of RHP poles (OL) | $P$ | count | Poles of $G(s)H(s)$ in the right half-plane |
| Number of encirclements | $N$ | count | Clockwise negative, counter-clockwise positive |
| Unstable closed-loop poles | $Z$ | count | Zeros of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ in RHP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Bode Plot Stability

### 2.1.1 Asymptotic Magnitude Plot Construction Rules

For a transfer function expressed in **Bode (time-constant) form**:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{K \left(1 + j\omega T_a\right)\left(1 + j\omega T_b\right)^n \cdots}{\left(j\omega\right)^m \left(1 + j\omega T_1\right)\left(1 + j\omega T_2\right)^n \cdots}$$

| Term Type | Corner Frequency | Slope Contribution (dB/decade) | Phase Contribution (degrees) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Constant $K$ | — | $20 \log_{10} K$ flat | $0^\circ$ |
| $(j\omega)^{\pm m}$ | none | $\pm 20m$ dB/dec | $\pm 90 m^\circ$ |
| Simple zero $(1 + j\omega T)$ | $\omega = 1/T$ | $+20$ dB/dec **above** corner | $0^\circ \to +90^\circ$ |
| Simple pole $(1 + j\omega T)^{-1}$ | $\omega = 1/T$ | $-20$ dB/dec **above** corner | $0^\circ \to -90^\circ$ |
| Quadratic zero | $\omega = \omega_n$ | $+40$ dB/dec above corner | $0^\circ \to +180^\circ$ |
| Quadratic pole | $\omega = \omega_n$ | $-40$ dB/dec above corner | $0^\circ \to -180^\circ$ |

### 2.1.2 Exact Phase Expressions

$$\phi(\omega) = \sum \tan^{-1}(\omega T_{\text{zero}}) - \sum \tan^{-1}(\omega T_{\text{pole}}) - 90^\circ \cdot m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the logarithm?** A logarithm compresses the wide frequency range (0.001 to 1000 rad/s) into a manageable paper width, and the multiplicative poles/zeros become **additive slopes**, making hand-sketching trivial — this is why Bode plots dominate control engineering.

### 2.1.3 Stability Conditions on Bode Plot

A minimum-phase system is **stable** if and only if both:

$$\boxed{GM > 0 \text{ dB} \quad \text{AND} \quad PM > 0^\circ}$$

| Condition | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------------|
| $GM > 0$ dB, $PM > 0^\circ$ | **Stable** system |
| $GM = 0$ dB **or** $PM = 0^\circ$ | **Marginally stable** (sustained oscillation) |
| $GM < 0$ dB, $PM < 0^\circ$ | **Unstable** system |
| Multiple $\omega_{pc}$ | **Conditionally stable** system |

> [!WARNING]
> **Conditional Stability Trap (KTU frequently tested):** A system with multiple phase crossovers can have $GM > 0$ and $PM > 0$ but still be unstable because of the *intermediate* phase crossover. Always check all crossover frequencies, not just the dominant one.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of the Nyquist Criterion

### 2.2.1 Cauchy's Argument Principle (The Mathematical Root)

If a contour $\Gamma_s$ in the $s$-plane encloses $Z$ zeros and $P$ poles of $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$ (traversed clockwise), then the mapped contour $\Gamma_F$ in the $F$-plane encircles the origin $N$ times, where:

$$N = Z - P$$

For the closed-loop characteristic equation, $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, the *origin* of the $F$-plane corresponds to the *critical point* $G(s)H(s) = -1$ in the $GH$-plane. Therefore:

$$\boxed{N = Z - P \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z = N + P}$$

### 2.2.2 The Nyquist Contour in the $s$-Plane

The contour $\Gamma_s$ is traversed **clockwise** in the $s$-plane (this is the *engineer's* convention) covering:

1. The imaginary axis from $\omega = -j\infty$ to $\omega = +j\infty$ (the principal path)
2. A semicircle of **infinite radius** in the right half-plane
3. Detours (indentations) around any open-loop poles lying on the imaginary axis (e.g., integrators $1/s$)

### 2.2.3 Encirclement Counting Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Clockwise encirclements of the critical point $-1 + j0$ are **negative**; counter-clockwise encirclements are **positive**. The total $N$ is the *algebraic sum* of all encirclements.

### 2.2.4 Special Cases — Poles on the Imaginary Axis

When $G(s)H(s)$ has poles at $s = 0$ (integrators) or on the $j\omega$-axis, the Nyquist contour must be **indented** with infinitesimal semicircles *to the right* (into the right half-plane) of these poles, bypassing them. The detour typically spans $90^\circ$ of phase per pole order at the start of the plot.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Magnitude (dB) | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ | Vertical axis of Bode plot |
| 2 | Phase (degrees) | $\phi(\omega) = \angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ | In degrees |
| 3 | Gain Crossover | $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ i.e. **0 dB** | Solved graphically/analytically |
| 4 | Phase Crossover | $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$ |  |
| 5 | **Gain Margin** | $GM = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ dB | Positive for stable |
| 6 | **Phase Margin** | $PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | Positive for stable |
| 7 | Closed-loop bandwidth relation | $\omega_{BW} \approx 1.5 \cdot \omega_{gc}$ (approx.) | Useful for design |
| 8 | Nyquist encirclements | $N = Z - P$ | Clockwise $N$ is **negative** |
| 9 | Stability from Nyquist | $Z = N + P = 0$ for stability | $Z$ is number of unstable CL poles |
| 10 | Corner frequency | $\omega_c = 1/T$ | Marks slope/phase change |
| 11 | Slope per pole | $-20$ dB/dec | For simple real pole |
| 12 | Integrator phase | $-90^\circ$ | For each $1/s$ factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Gain and phase margins are the language of robust control design. Aerospace, automotive stability control, and analog amplifier design all specify minimum margins (e.g., $PM \geq 45^\circ$ and $GM \geq 6$ dB are industry-standard minimums).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gain Margin from Bode Plot

### Step 1 — Define the Magnitude at the Phase Crossover

The phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ is defined as the frequency at which the open-loop phase is exactly $-180^\circ$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180^\circ$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

The magnitude of the open-loop transfer function at this frequency is:

$$M_{pc} = \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

### Step 3 — Define Gain Margin Conceptually

Gain Margin represents "how much the gain $K$ can be multiplied before the system becomes marginally stable." For marginal stability, the magnitude must become **unity (1)** at $\omega_{pc}$:

$$K_{critical} \cdot \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert_{K=1} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $K_{critical}$

$$K_{critical} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

### Step 5 — Express in Decibels (Standard Engineering Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}(K_{critical}) \\[4pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}\right) \\[4pt]
&= -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert \;\; \text{dB}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Phase Margin from Bode Plot

### Step 1 — Define the Gain Crossover Frequency

The gain crossover $\omega_{gc}$ is where the magnitude curve crosses **0 dB** (i.e., $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$):

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20\log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 0 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Phase at $\omega_{gc}$

The phase of the open-loop system at this frequency is:

$$\phi_{gc} = \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

### Step 3 — Define the Distance to $-180^\circ$

The phase margin is the additional phase lag the system can tolerate before reaching the instability condition $\phi = -180^\circ$:

$$PM = -180^\circ - \phi_{gc}$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange for the Standard Convention

Since $\phi_{gc}$ is negative (lag) for typical systems:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PM &= -180^\circ - \phi_{gc} \\[4pt]
&= -180^\circ + (-\phi_{gc}) \\[4pt]
&= 180^\circ + \phi_{gc}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})}$$

---

## 3.3 Rigorous Derivation of the Nyquist Stability Criterion

### Step 1 — Define the Closed-Loop Characteristic Polynomial

The closed-loop system poles are roots of:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

Let $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$. For stability, **all zeros of $F(s)$ must lie in the left half-plane (LHP)**.

### Step 2 — Construct the Nyquist Contour

A closed contour $\Gamma_s$ in the $s$-plane is drawn such that the *entire right half-plane is enclosed*. This contour consists of:
- The $j\omega$-axis from $\omega = -\infty$ to $\omega = +\infty$ (path $\mathcal{C}_1$)
- An infinite semicircle in the right half-plane from $\omega = +\infty$ back to $\omega = -\infty$ (path $\mathcal{C}_2$)
- Small indentations around any imaginary-axis poles of $G(s)H(s)$ (path $\mathcal{C}_3$)

The traversal is **clockwise** (this is the KTU/IEEE convention; pure math convention is counter-clockwise).

### Step 3 — Apply Cauchy's Argument Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cauchy's Argument Principle:** If a function $F(s)$ is analytic on and inside a closed contour $\Gamma_s$ except at $P$ poles and $Z$ zeros, then the image $F(\Gamma_s)$ encircles the origin $N$ times, where:
> 
> $$N = Z - P$$
> 
> Counter-clockwise encirclements are **positive**, clockwise are **negative**.

### Step 4 — Shift the Origin to $-1 + j0$

The characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ is satisfied when $G(s)H(s) = -1$. Therefore, the origin of the $F(s)$-plane corresponds to the **point $-1 + j0$** in the $G(s)H(s)$-plane.

So the criterion becomes: **Count encirclements of $-1 + j0$ by $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ as $\omega$ travels from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.**

### Step 5 — Account for the Conjugate Symmetry

For a real-coefficient $G(s)H(s)$:

$$G(-j\omega)H(-j\omega) = \overline{G(j\omega)H(j\omega)}$$

The plot for $\omega \in (-\infty, 0)$ is the **mirror image** of the plot for $\omega \in (0, +\infty)$ across the real axis. The infinite semicircle in $\mathcal{C}_2$ maps to the origin of the $GH$-plane (assuming strictly proper $G(s)H(s)$) and contributes **zero** encirclements.

### Step 6 — State the Final Result

The total number of encirclements $N$ over $\omega \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ equals twice the encirclements counted on $\omega \in (0, +\infty)$:

$$\boxed{N = Z - P \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad Z = N + P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability requirement:** $Z = 0$ (no RHP closed-loop poles) requires $N = -P$. If the open-loop system is already stable ($P = 0$), the system is closed-loop stable if and only if $N = 0$ (no encirclements of $-1$).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Bode Plot Margins

**Problem:** For $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(1 + 0.1s)(1 + 0.01s)}$, find the gain and phase margins.

### Step 1 — Convert to Frequency Response

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega (1 + j0.1\omega)(1 + j0.01\omega)}$$

### Step 2 — Corner Frequencies

- $\omega_1 = 1/0.1 = 10$ rad/s
- $\omega_2 = 1/0.01 = 100$ rad/s

### Step 3 — Initial Slope (due to integrator $1/s$)

- Below $\omega = 10$: slope = $-20$ dB/dec, starting from $20 \log_{10}(10/\omega)$
- At $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s: magnitude = $20 \log_{10}(10/0.1) = 40$ dB

### Step 4 — Slope Changes

| Frequency range (rad/s) | Slope (dB/dec) |
|--------------------------|----------------|
| $0.1 \to 10$ | $-20$ |
| $10 \to 100$ | $-40$ |
| $100 \to \infty$ | $-60$ |

### Step 5 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 0 dB)

For the segment $0.1 \le \omega \le 10$ with slope $-20$ dB/dec starting at 40 dB at $\omega = 0.1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= 40 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{0.1}\right) \\[4pt]
20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{0.1}\right) &= 40 \\[4pt]
\log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{0.1}\right) &= 2 \\[4pt]
\omega_{gc} &= 0.1 \times 100 = 10 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180^\circ$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\omega) &= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega) \\[4pt]
-180^\circ &= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) - \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega_{pc}) \\[4pt]
90^\circ &= \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega_{pc}) + \tan^{-1}(0.01\omega_{pc})
\end{aligned}
$$

For high frequencies, both $\tan^{-1}$ terms approach $90^\circ$, so $\omega_{pc} \to \infty$. Using a trial approach:

At $\omega_{pc} = 50$ rad/s: $\tan^{-1}(5) + \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 78.7^\circ + 26.6^\circ = 105.3^\circ$ (too high)
At $\omega_{pc} = 20$ rad/s: $\tan^{-1}(2) + \tan^{-1}(0.2) = 63.4^\circ + 11.3^\circ = 74.7^\circ$ (too low)

Iterating: $\omega_{pc} \approx 33$ rad/s. (For KTU exams, this is usually provided in the figure.)

### Step 7 — Compute Gain Margin

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{dB} &= -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\omega_{pc} = 33$ rad/s and $G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{10}{\omega \sqrt{1+0.01\omega^2}\sqrt{1+0.0001\omega^2}}$:

$$GM_{dB} \approx +19.5 \text{ dB (stable)}$$

### Step 8 — Compute Phase Margin

At $\omega_{gc} = 10$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PM &= 180^\circ - 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}(0.1) \\[4pt]
&= 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 45^\circ - 5.71^\circ \\[4pt]
&= 39.3^\circ \text{ (stable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{System is stable with } GM \approx +19.5 \text{ dB and } PM \approx 39.3^\circ}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Bode Plot Stability Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Open Loop TF GsHs]) --> stepB[Express in Bode time-constant form]
    stepB --> stepC[Identify corner frequencies: omega_i = 1/T_i]
    stepC --> stepD[Plot magnitude: 20 log GH vs log omega]
    stepD --> stepE[Plot phase: angle GH vs log omega]
    stepE --> stepF{Magnitude crosses 0 dB?}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Mark omega_gc on phase plot]
    stepF -- No --> stepH[Check instability via phase only]
    stepG --> stepI[Compute PM = 180 + angle at omega_gc]
    stepE --> stepJ{Phase crosses -180 deg?}
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepK[Mark omega_pc on magnitude plot]
    stepJ -- No --> stepL[GM is infinite: very stable]
    stepK --> stepM[Compute GM = -20 log GH at omega_pc]
    stepI --> stepN{GM > 0 AND PM > 0?}
    stepM --> stepN
    stepN -- Yes --> stepO([System STABLE: relative margins quantify robustness])
    stepN -- No --> stepP([System UNSTABLE: design compensator])
    stepH --> stepP
    stepL --> stepO
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Nyquist Stability Criterion Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Open Loop TF GsHs]) --> stepB[Count P: poles of GH in RHP]
    stepB --> stepC{Poles on jw axis?}
    stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Indent contour with infinitesimal semicircles to the right]
    stepC -- No --> stepE[Use standard Nyquist contour: jw axis + infinite RHP semicircle]
    stepD --> stepF[Map contour to GH-plane: plot GH for omega in 0 to plus infinity]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Add mirror image for omega in minus infinity to 0]
    stepG --> stepH[Count N: encirclements of -1 + j0]
    stepH --> stepI[Apply: N = Z - P]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Z = N + P]
    stepJ --> stepK{Z = 0?}
    stepK -- Yes --> stepL([CLOSED-LOOP STABLE])
    stepK -- No --> stepM([UNSTABLE: Z unstable closed-loop poles])
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Mapping from $s$-Plane to $GH$-Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Source: s-plane Nyquist Contour]
        S1[omega: 0 to plus infinity on jw-axis]
        S2[Infinite RHP semicircle]
        S3[Indentations around s=0 poles]
    end
    subgraph B[Target: GH-plane Image]
        T1[GH for omega in 0 to plus infinity]
        T2[GH approaches origin as omega to infinity]
        T3[GH sweeps plus 90 deg per integrator at omega to 0]
        T4[Mirror image for negative omega]
    end
    subgraph C[Decision: Critical Point -1 + j0]
        C1[Count N: encirclements]
    end
    S1 --> T1
    S2 --> T2
    S3 --> T3
    T1 --> T4
    T1 --> C1
    T3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Apply Z = N + P]
```

## 4.4 Bode Plot Sketch — Generic Stable System (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Low omega: Slope -20 dB/dec, Phase approx -90 deg"] --> B["omega_gc: Mag crosses 0 dB, Phase approx -135 deg"]
    B --> C["Mid omega: Phase crosses -180 deg at omega_pc"]
    C --> D["High omega: Mag continues down, Phase approaches -270 deg"]
    B -.-> E["PM = 180 + angle at omega_gc approx 45 deg"]
    C -.-> F["GM = -20 log GH at omega_pc approx 15 dB"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define gain margin and phase margin. Explain their significance in assessing relative stability of a control system.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain $K$ can be multiplied before the closed-loop system reaches the verge of instability. It is measured in **dB** at the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase = $-180^\circ$):

$$GM_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

- **Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag that can be tolerated at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude = 1 or 0 dB) before the system becomes unstable:

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Significance:** A system is stable if and only if $GM > 0$ dB and $PM > 0^\circ$. Larger margins indicate greater *relative stability*, meaning the system can tolerate parameter variations before becoming unstable. Industry-standard minimums are $GM \geq 6$ dB and $PM \geq 30^\circ$ to $45^\circ$ for good transient response.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Nyquist stability criterion. What is the significance of the point $-1 + j0$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Nyquist Stability Criterion** states that the number of closed-loop poles $Z$ in the right half of the $s$-plane is given by:

$$Z = N + P$$

where $N$ is the number of *clockwise* encirclements of the critical point $-1 + j0$ by the Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ (with $\omega$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$), and $P$ is the number of open-loop poles in the right half-plane.

**Significance of $-1 + j0$:** The characteristic equation of the closed-loop system is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, which is equivalent to $G(s)H(s) = -1$. Therefore, the origin of the $F(s) = 1 + GH$ plane maps to the point $-1 + j0$ in the $GH$ plane. Cauchy's argument principle applied to $F(s)$ becomes a count of encirclements of $-1 + j0$ in the $GH$ plane. If $-1$ is *not* encircled (for stable open-loop systems), the closed-loop system is stable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For a unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{20(1 + 0.5s)}{s(1 + 2s)(1 + 0.1s)}$:**

**(a) Sketch the Bode plot. Determine the gain crossover frequency and phase crossover frequency. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Find the gain margin and phase margin. Comment on the stability of the system. (7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Convert to Bode Form**

The given TF is already in Bode form with:
- $K = 20$
- Type-1 system (one integrator at origin)
- Zero at $\omega = 1/0.5 = 2$ rad/s
- Pole at $\omega = 1/2 = 0.5$ rad/s
- Pole at $\omega = 1/0.1 = 10$ rad/s

**Step 2 — Magnitude Plot Slopes**

| Frequency Range (rad/s) | Slope (dB/dec) | Reason |
|--------------------------|----------------|--------|
| $0 < \omega < 0.5$ | $-20$ | Integrator only |
| $0.5 < \omega < 2$ | $-40$ | Add pole at 0.5 |
| $2 < \omega < 10$ | $-20$ | Add zero at 2 |
| $\omega > 10$ | $-40$ | Add pole at 10 |

**Step 3 — Magnitude at Reference Point**

At $\omega = 1$ rad/s (in the first region with slope $-20$ dB/dec, where magnitude $= 20 \log_{10} 20 = 26.02$ dB):

$$20 \log_{10} \left(\frac{20}{1}\right) = 26.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude at Each Corner**

- At $\omega = 0.5$: $26.02 - 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = 26.02 + 6.02 = 32.04$ dB
- At $\omega = 2$: $32.04 - 40 \log_{10}(2/0.5) = 32.04 - 24.08 = 7.96$ dB
- At $\omega = 10$: $7.96 - 20 \log_{10}(10/2) = 7.96 - 13.98 = -6.02$ dB

**Step 5 — Find $\omega_{gc}$ (magnitude = 0 dB)**

Since 0 dB lies between $\omega = 2$ (7.96 dB) and $\omega = 10$ (-6.02 dB) in the $-20$ dB/dec region:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= 7.96 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) &= 7.96 \\[4pt]
\log_{10}\left(\frac{\omega_{gc}}{2}\right) &= 0.398 \\[4pt]
\omega_{gc} &= 2 \times 10^{0.398} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Find $\omega_{pc}$ (phase = $-180^\circ$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(\omega) &= -90^\circ + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(2\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) \\[4pt]
-180^\circ &= -90^\circ + \tan^{-1}(0.5\omega) - \tan^{-1}(2\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $\omega = 3$ rad/s: $-90 + 56.3 - 80.5 - 16.7 = -130.9^\circ$ (not enough lag)
Try $\omega = 5$ rad/s: $-90 + 68.2 - 84.3 - 26.6 = -132.7^\circ$ (still not enough)
Try $\omega = 8$ rad/s: $-90 + 76.0 - 86.4 - 38.7 = -149.1^\circ$ (still not enough)
Try $\omega = 15$ rad/s: $-90 + 82.4 - 88.2 - 56.3 = -152.1^\circ$
Try $\omega = 50$ rad/s: $-90 + 87.7 - 89.8 - 78.7 = -170.8^\circ$ (close)
Try $\omega = 100$ rad/s: $-90 + 88.9 - 89.97 - 84.3 = -175.4^\circ$
Try $\omega = \infty$: $-90 + 90 - 90 - 90 = -180^\circ$

So $\omega_{pc} \to \infty$, but approaches 180° asymptotically. From the curves, at $\omega_{pc} \approx 200$ rad/s, $\phi \approx -179^\circ$.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Gain Margin**

Since $\omega_{pc} \approx \infty$, and at very high frequencies, the magnitude slope is $-40$ dB/dec, so $\vert GH \vert \to 0$:

$$GM_{dB} = \infty \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Phase Margin**

At $\omega_{gc} = 5$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PM &= 180^\circ + \phi(\omega_{gc}) \\[4pt]
&= 180^\circ + \left[-90^\circ + \tan^{-1}(2.5) - \tan^{-1}(10) - \tan^{-1}(0.5)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 180^\circ + \left[-90^\circ + 68.2^\circ - 84.3^\circ - 26.6^\circ\right] \\[4pt]
&= 180^\circ - 132.7^\circ \\[4pt]
&= 47.3^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stability Comment**

Since $PM = 47.3^\circ > 0$ and $GM = \infty$ dB, **the system is stable** with good relative stability. A phase margin between 30° and 60° is considered excellent for most engineering applications.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A unity feedback control system has $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$.**

**(a) Using the Nyquist stability criterion, find the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is stable. (7 Marks)**

**(b) If $K = 50$, determine the number of unstable closed-loop poles using the Nyquist plot description. (7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Open-Loop Poles**

Poles of $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$ are at $s = 0, -2, -5$. **None are in the right half-plane**, so $P = 0$.

**Step 2 — Stability Requirement**

For closed-loop stability, $Z = 0$, so $N = Z - P = 0$. The Nyquist plot must **not encircle** $-1 + j0$.

**Step 3 — Find the Phase Crossover Frequency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(j\omega)H(j\omega) &= \frac{K}{j\omega(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 5)} \\[4pt]
\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) &= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega}{5}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\omega_{pc}$, set phase = $-180^\circ$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-180^\circ &= -90^\circ - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right) \\[4pt]
90^\circ &= \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For high $\omega$, both $\tan^{-1}$ terms approach $90^\circ$. Using $\tan(A+B) = \tan(90^\circ) = \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2}\right) + \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}\right)\right] = \frac{\frac{\omega_{pc}}{2} + \frac{\omega_{pc}}{5}}{1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}^2}{10}} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator must equal zero:

$$1 - \frac{\omega_{pc}^2}{10} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert &= \frac{K}{\omega_{pc} \cdot \sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 4} \cdot \sqrt{\omega_{pc}^2 + 25}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{K}{3.162 \cdot \sqrt{10 + 4} \cdot \sqrt{10 + 25}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{K}{3.162 \cdot \sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{35}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{K}{3.162 \cdot 3.742 \cdot 5.916} = \frac{K}{70.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Stability Condition**

The critical point $-1$ is encircled when $\vert GH \vert > 1$ at $\omega_{pc}$. To avoid encirclement (and ensure stability with $P = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{K}{70.0} &< 1 \\[4pt]
K &< 70
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{0 < K < 70 \text{ for closed-loop stability}}$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — For $K = 50$, Compute Magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{10}$**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{50}{70.0} = 0.714 < 1$$

**Step 2 — Interpret Geometrically**

Since the magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ is less than 1, the Nyquist plot **does not encircle** the critical point $-1 + j0$. With $P = 0$:

$$N = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z = N + P = 0 + 0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

For $K = 50$, the closed-loop system is **stable** with **zero unstable poles**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Frequently Losing Marks):**
> 1. **Sign error in $N$:** Forgetting that clockwise encirclements are **negative**. KTU expects the IEEE convention.
> 2. **Mirror image omission:** Failing to consider $\omega \in (-\infty, 0)$ when counting $N$. Always state whether your count is for the full plot or just positive $\omega$ (in which case multiply by 2 if symmetric).
> 3. **Ignoring indentations:** For systems with poles at $s = 0$ (Type-1 or higher), the contour must be indented. The indentation contributes $90^\circ$ per pole order at the start of the plot — missing this throws off the entire $N$ count.
> 4. **Confusing $GM > 0$ dB with $GM$ as a positive number:** $GM = -5$ dB means the system is *unstable* by 5 dB. Always check the sign.
> 5. **Drawing phase plot from wrong formula:** Phase contribution of $(1 + j\omega T)^{-1}$ is $-\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$, not $+$. Each $j\omega$ in the denominator contributes $-90^\circ$, not $+90^\circ$.
> 6. **For Bode:** Linear extrapolation on a log axis is in **decades**, not equal spacing on the paper — a common 1-mark error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Bode Plot Quick-Reference Checklist

- [x] **Bode form first:** Always rewrite $G(s)H(s)$ in time-constant form $\dfrac{K \prod(1 + sT_{zi})}{\prod(s)^m \prod(1 + sT_{pi})}$ before sketching.
- [x] **Corner frequencies** are $\omega_i = 1/T_i$ — mark these clearly on the frequency axis.
- [x] **Slope per factor:** zero = +20 dB/dec, pole = -20 dB/dec, integrator = -20 dB/dec, differentiator = +20 dB/dec.
- [x] **Quadratic terms** contribute $\pm 40$ dB/dec slope change and $\pm 180^\circ$ phase change.
- [x] **$\omega_{gc}$:** Where magnitude = **0 dB** (i.e., $\vert GH \vert = 1$).
- [x] **$\omega_{pc}$:** Where phase = $-180^\circ$.
- [x] **$GM_{dB} = -20\log_{10}\vert GH \vert$ at $\omega_{pc}$** (must be **positive** for stability).
- [x] **$PM = 180^\circ + \angle GH$ at $\omega_{gc}$** (must be **positive** for stability).
- [x] **Industry design standard:** $PM \geq 45^\circ$, $GM \geq 6$ dB.

### 🎯 Nyquist Quick-Reference Checklist

- [x] **Master formula:** $N = Z - P$ — never write $N = P - Z$ (this is the most common KTU mistake).
- [x] **Clockwise = negative** for $N$; **counter-clockwise = positive** for $N$.
- [x] **$P$ = open-loop RHP poles** (count carefully, including repeated poles).
- [x] **Stability requires $Z = 0$**, so $N = -P$.
- [x] **For stable open-loop** ($P = 0$): $N = 0$ means stable (no encirclements).
- [x] **Always indent** the contour around imaginary-axis poles of $GH$.
- [x] **Conjugate symmetry:** mirror image for $\omega < 0$ is automatic for real-coefficient $GH$.
- [x] **The point $-1 + j0$** is the critical reference — count its encirclements.
- [x] **At $\omega = 0$** for Type-1 systems, the plot starts at $\infty$ along the $-90^\circ$ direction.
- [x] **At $\omega = \infty$** for strictly proper systems, the plot terminates at origin.

### 🎯 Universal KTU 2024 Exam Tips

- [x] **Always show units** (dB for magnitude, degrees for phase, rad/s for frequency).
- [x] **Final stability statement** is mandatory: "Since $GM > 0$ dB and $PM > 0^\circ$ (or $Z = 0$), the system is **stable**."
- [x] **Mention relative stability** when Bode is used: "The system has *good* relative stability" or "marginal stability."
- [x] **Sketch the plots neatly** with labeled axes and crossover points — KTU awards 2–3 marks for the sketch alone.
- [x] **Be precise with the sign of $N$** in Nyquist problems; many students lose 2 marks for one sign error.
- [x] **For multi-part questions**, calculate intermediate values symbolically and substitute only at the end to minimize arithmetic errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P, PI and PID Controllers: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** P, PI and PID Controllers: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In control systems engineering, a **Controller** is a device or software algorithm that automatically regulates the behavior of a dynamic system by comparing its actual output (controlled variable) with a desired reference input (setpoint) and generating a corrective actuating signal based on the error.

The three fundamental continuous (analog) controller actions studied universally in **KTU 2024 Scheme Control Systems** are:

- **P Controller (Proportional Controller):** Produces a control output proportional to the instantaneous error signal.
- **PI Controller (Proportional-Integral Controller):** Combines proportional action with integral action to eliminate steady-state error.
- **PID Controller (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Controller):** Adds derivative action to the PI structure to anticipate and dampen future error trends.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on understanding the *qualitative effect* of each controller mode (P, PI, PD, PID) on **steady-state error ($e_{ss}$), transient response (rise time, peak overshoot, settling time), and stability**. Students must master both the transfer function form and the time-domain performance implications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control of a Car"

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h**:

- **P Controller (Proportional):** This is like a driver who presses the accelerator **harder when the speed drops far below 80, and eases off as the speed gets closer**. If you are at 60, you press hard. If you are at 78, you press lightly. The catch? The driver may *never quite reach 80* because as the speed approaches the target, the gas pedal input becomes too small to overcome friction. The car settles at, say, 79 km/h — this is **steady-state error**.

- **PI Controller (Proportional + Integral):** Now imagine the driver has a memory. If the car has been running at 79 km/h for a long time, the driver says, *"I've been 1 km/h short for a while, so I will keep pressing a little extra until I hit exactly 80."* The **Integral term accumulates past errors** and forces the system to reach the exact setpoint over time, eliminating the steady-state error.

- **PID Controller (Proportional + Integral + Derivative):** Now add a sixth sense — the driver can also **anticipate**. If the car is about to go downhill (a future disturbance is coming), the driver eases off the accelerator *before* the speed actually overshoots. The **Derivative term reacts to the rate of change of error**, providing predictive damping that reduces overshoot and improves transient response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners often frame conceptual questions around real-world analogies. The "cruise control" or "room temperature AC controller" analogy is a classic high-scoring response for introducing PID controllers in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 1.3 Standard Control Architecture

In a **unity feedback closed-loop control system**, the controller is placed in the **forward path** between the error detector and the plant. The standard block diagram components are:

- **Reference Input (R(s))**: The desired output (e.g., setpoint temperature, target speed).
- **Error Signal (E(s))**: $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$.
- **Controller ($G_c(s)$)**: The P, PI, or PID compensator under study.
- **Plant ($G(s)$)**: The physical system being controlled.
- **Feedback Element (H(s))**: Typically unity ($H(s) = 1$) in introductory KTU problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology:** The term **"Controller Action"** refers strictly to the mathematical relationship between the error signal $e(t)$ and the controller output $u(t)$. The choice of action dictates the controller's transfer function $G_c(s) = \dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 P Controller — The Proportional Action

### Operational Concept
The **Proportional Controller** generates an output that is directly proportional to the instantaneous error signal. Mathematically, the time-domain relationship is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t)$$

where $K_p$ is the **Proportional Gain** (a dimensionless tuning constant or expressed in controller-specific units).

### Laplace Domain Transfer Function

Applying the Laplace Transform:

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) \implies G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p$$

### Effect on System Performance
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** **Decreases** as $K_p$ increases (faster response).
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** **Reduces** but **never reaches zero** for Type-0 systems.
- **Peak Overshoot ($M_p$):** **Increases** with higher $K_p$.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Generally **increases** for very high $K_p$.
- **Stability:** The system can become **unstable** if $K_p$ is set too high.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to assume P-control eliminates steady-state error. It does **not** — it only **reduces** it. To *eliminate* $e_{ss}$, integral action is required.

## 2.2 PI Controller — The Proportional-Integral Action

### Operational Concept
The **PI Controller** adds an integral term that continuously accumulates the past error values. Its time-domain equation is:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where $K_i$ is the **Integral Gain** (units: 1/time, or expressed as $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ where $T_i$ is the **integral time constant**).

### Laplace Domain Transfer Function

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) \implies G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

Equivalently, in terms of reset time $T_i$:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} \right)$$

### Effect on System Performance
- **Eliminates Steady-State Error:** The integral action increases the system **Type number by 1**, making $e_{ss} = 0$ for step inputs.
- **Slower Transient Response:** Integral action introduces a pole at the origin, which can slow down the response.
- **Increased Overshoot:** The integrator can cause the response to overshoot the setpoint significantly.
- **Reduces Steady-State Disturbance Rejection Lag.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Pole at Origin?** The $\dfrac{1}{s}$ term in the PI controller places a pole at $s = 0$ in the open-loop transfer function. This extra integrator boosts the system Type, which (by the Final Value Theorem for step inputs) forces the steady-state error to zero.

## 2.3 PID Controller — The Proportional-Integral-Derivative Action

### Operational Concept
The **PID Controller** combines all three control actions, producing an output based on the present error (P), the accumulation of past errors (I), and the rate of change of current error (D):

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $K_d$ is the **Derivative Gain** (units: time).

### Laplace Domain Transfer Function

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

Equivalently, in standard form with time constants $T_i$ (integral time) and $T_d$ (derivative time):

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i \, s} + T_d \, s \right)$$

### Effect on System Performance
- **Eliminates Steady-State Error:** Inherits this property from the I-action.
- **Improves Damping:** The D-action acts like a "predictive brake," reducing overshoot.
- **Faster Response:** D-action responds to the *trend* of the error, not just its magnitude.
- **Amplifies Noise:** A major drawback — the derivative of high-frequency measurement noise can destabilize the controller.
- **Three Tuning Knobs ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$):** Provides flexibility but makes tuning more complex (e.g., Ziegler-Nichols method).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** PID controllers are the **workhorse of industrial automation**, governing over 95% of all practical control loops in process industries (chemical plants, refineries, HVAC systems, robotics, automotive cruise control, and aerospace autopilots).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Controller Type** | **Transfer Function $G_c(s)$** | **Time-Domain Equation $u(t)$** | **Key Effect on $e_{ss}$** | **Key Effect on Overshoot** | **Number of Tuning Parameters** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $K_p$ | $K_p \, e(t)$ | Reduces (does not eliminate) | Increases with $K_p$ | 1 |
| **PI** | $K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} = \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | $K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt$ | **Eliminates** (for step input) | Can be high; slower transient | 2 |
| **PD** | $K_p + K_d s$ | $K_p e(t) + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | Does not eliminate | **Reduces** (adds damping) | 2 |
| **PID** | $K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | $K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | **Eliminates** | Reduced (D-action damps it) | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on PD Controller:** Although not the focus of this introductory note, the PD controller is included for completeness. It is a **two-parameter controller** that improves transient response (damping) but does **not** eliminate steady-state error — hence the need to combine it with the I-action to form the full PID.

## 2.5 Pole-Zero Perspective — Why These Controllers Behave This Way

Each controller introduces specific poles and zeros in the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$:

- **P Controller:** No new poles or zeros (just gain adjustment).
- **PI Controller:** Adds **one pole at $s = 0$** (integrator) and **one zero at $s = -\dfrac{K_i}{K_p}$**. The zero partially cancels the slow dynamics introduced by the pole.
- **PID Controller:** Adds **one pole at $s = 0$** and **two zeros** whose locations depend on the relative magnitudes of $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters for KTU:** Many Part B (14-mark) problems ask students to design a PID controller by pole-zero placement or to sketch the **root locus** of the compensated system. Understanding which pole/zero each mode contributes is essential.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: P Controller Output for a Unit Step Input

### Problem Setup
A unity feedback system has a plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+2)}$ and a P controller $G_c(s) = K_p$. Determine the closed-loop transfer function, the time response to a unit step input, and the steady-state error.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Form the open-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G(s) = K_p \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+2)} = \frac{K_p}{s(s+2)}$$

This step combines the controller transfer function and the plant transfer function in the forward path.

**Step 2: Form the closed-loop transfer function (with unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$).**

The standard closed-loop formula is:

$$T(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s) H(s)} = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

Substituting:

$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K_p}{s(s+2)}}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{s(s+2)}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the expression.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(s+2)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p}{s(s+2) + K_p} = \frac{K_p}{s^2 + 2s + K_p}$$

This is the standard second-order form, which can be rewritten as:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$

where $\omega_n^2 = K_p$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = 2$.

**Step 4: Identify natural frequency and damping ratio.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{1}{\omega_n} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_p}}$$

This reveals a critical insight: as $K_p$ increases, the natural frequency grows but the damping ratio **decreases**, leading to more oscillatory (underdamped) behavior.

**Step 5: Compute the steady-state error for a unit step input.**

The error transfer function is:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{s(s+2)}} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

By the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_p}{s(s+2)}} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+2)}{s(s+2) + K_p} = \frac{0}{K_p} = 0$$

**Result:** The steady-state error for a **step input** is **zero** because the open-loop system is **Type 1** (it has an integrator in the plant itself). This is a **plant-driven** result, not a controller-driven one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle KTU Insight:** When a Type-1 plant is controlled by a P controller, the steady-state error for a step input is already zero by virtue of the plant's own integrator. The P controller does *not* add a pole at the origin. The integral term in a PI controller becomes essential when the plant is **Type 0** (no inherent integrator).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PI Controller Steady-State Error for a Ramp Input

### Problem Setup
A unity feedback system has a Type-0 plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+3}$ and a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$. Determine the steady-state error for a unit ramp input $r(t) = t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Form the open-loop transfer function.**

$$G_{OL}(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{s+3}$$

Combine the PI controller into a single fraction:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s(s+3)}$$

This shows the PI controller effectively increases the system Type from 0 to 1.

**Step 2: Apply the Final Value Theorem for a ramp input.**

For $r(t) = t$, we have $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$.

The error signal in the s-domain is:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{s^2}}{1 + \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s(s+3)}}$$

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{s^2} \cdot \frac{s(s+3)}{s(s+3) + K_p s + K_i} = \frac{s+3}{s \left[ s^2 + 3s + K_p s + K_i \right]}$$

$$E(s) = \frac{s+3}{s \left[ s^2 + (3 + K_p)s + K_i \right]}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the limit.**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{s+3}{s \left[ s^2 + (3 + K_p)s + K_i \right]}$$

Cancel the common factor of $s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s+3}{s^2 + (3 + K_p)s + K_i}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{0 + 3}{0 + 0 + K_i} = \frac{3}{K_i}$$

**Result:** The steady-state error for a unit ramp input with a PI controller is $\dfrac{3}{K_i}$, which is **finite but nonzero**. To make this zero, you would need a Type-2 system (two integrators in the loop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Insight:** This derivation showcases the **integral action's role in eliminating step-input error**, but its *inability* to eliminate ramp-input error for a single-integrator loop. Examiners love questions that ask: *"With a PI controller, for which input types is the steady-state error zero?"* The answer: **step inputs only**, when the plant itself has no integrator.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Time Response Comparison

The following table presents a side-by-side comparison of the three controllers' effects on key time-domain specifications. The reference is a generic second-order system with plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + 2s + 1}$ and unit step input.

| **Specification** | **P Controller ($K_p$ only)** | **PI Controller** | **PID Controller** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady-State Error (step)** | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p \cdot G(0)}$ | **Zero** (for Type-0 plant, due to I-action) | **Zero** |
| **Rise Time ($t_r$)** | Decreases as $K_p$ ↑ | Slightly increases | Generally decreases |
| **Peak Overshoot ($M_p$)** | Increases as $K_p$ ↑ | Can be high | Reduced (D-action damps) |
| **Settling Time ($t_s$)** | May increase if $K_p$ too high | May increase | Usually improves |
| **Stability Margin** | Reduces as $K_p$ ↑ | Can be tighter | Best when well-tuned |
| **Noise Sensitivity** | Low | Low | **High** (D amplifies noise) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Simplest | Moderate | Highest (3 parameters) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Closed-Loop Control System with P/PI/PID Controller

The figure below illustrates the canonical closed-loop architecture with a controller $G_c(s)$ in the forward path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rnode[Reference Input R(s)] --> SumNode{Summing Junction}
    FbNode[Feedback Signal C(s)] -->|negative feedback| SumNode
    SumNode -->|Error E(s)| CtrlNode[Controller Gc s]
    CtrlNode -->|Control U s| PlantNode[Plant G s]
    PlantNode -->|Output C s| OutNode[System Output]
    OutNode --> FbNode
    OutNode --> OutSink[Measured Output]

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000

    class Rnode,OutSink input
    class CtrlNode ctrl
    class PlantNode plant
    class OutNode output
    class SumNode sum
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **summing junction** computes the error $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$.
- The **controller block** $G_c(s)$ represents the P, PI, or PID action.
- The **plant block** $G(s)$ is the physical system being controlled.
- The **feedback path** returns the output to be compared with the reference.

## 4.2 Detailed Architecture — PID Controller Internal Structure

The internal structure of a **PID controller** showing the three parallel branches (P, I, D) is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ErrorIn[Error E s] --> Pbranch[Proportional Kp]
    ErrorIn --> Ibranch[Integral Ki over s]
    ErrorIn --> Dbranch[Derivative Kd times s]
    Pbranch --> SumPID{Summing Junction}
    Ibranch --> SumPID
    Dbranch --> SumPID
    SumPID --> Uout[Control Signal U s]

    classDef errorNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef branchNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef sumNode fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000

    class ErrorIn errorNode
    class Pbranch,Ibranch,Dbranch branchNode
    class SumPID sumNode
    class Uout outNode
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The error $E(s)$ is fed into **three parallel branches** simultaneously.
- Each branch applies its respective gain: $K_p$, $\dfrac{K_i}{s}$, or $K_d \, s$.
- The three branch outputs are summed to produce the total control signal $U(s)$.

## 4.3 Functional Flow — How a PID Controller Processes a Step Disturbance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeDomain[Time Domain Processing]
        A[Error e t arrives] --> B{P Action}
        A --> C{I Action}
        A --> D{D Action}
        B --> E[Output uP equals Kp times e t]
        C --> F[Output uI equals Ki times integral of e tau d tau]
        D --> G[Output uD equals Kd times de t over dt]
    end
    E --> H[Sum all three outputs]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Final u t sent to plant]

    classDef inputNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef actionNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000

    class A inputNode
    class B,C,D actionNode
    class E,F,G outputNode
    class H,I outputNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to draw the PID block diagram in an exam, students often forget to label the summing junction with the negative sign for feedback. Always explicitly indicate the **negative feedback polarity** at the summing junction — a missing minus sign costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: Define a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller. Write its transfer function and explain the role of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PID controller** is a feedback controller that computes its control signal $u(t)$ as a weighted sum of three terms based on the error $e(t)$:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Role of each term (1 mark each):**
- **Proportional ($K_p$):** Produces an output proportional to the present error. Speeds up response but cannot eliminate steady-state error.
- **Integral ($K_i$):** Accumulates past errors. Eliminates steady-state error by driving the system to the exact setpoint.
- **Derivative ($K_d$):** Reacts to the rate of change of error. Provides predictive damping, reduces overshoot, and improves transient stability.

---

### Question 2: Comparison of P and PI Controllers

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Compare a P controller and a PI controller in terms of their effect on steady-state error and transient response. Mention one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Criterion** | **P Controller** | **PI Controller** |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-State Error** | Reduces but does not eliminate | Eliminates (for step input) |
| **Transient Response** | Faster rise time, may overshoot | Slower, more overshoot possible |
| **Disadvantage** | Cannot remove $e_{ss}$ entirely; may cause steady-state offset | Slower response; integral windup |

**One disadvantage of each (1 mark):**
- **P Controller Disadvantage:** A pure P controller leaves a **persistent steady-state error** (offset) for step inputs in Type-0 systems.
- **PI Controller Disadvantage:** The integral action can cause **integral windup** during actuator saturation, leading to large overshoots and oscillations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an **internal choice** between two alternative questions (A or B). Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - July 2024, Module 3]`**

**A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a closed-loop control system employing a PID controller. Derive the transfer function of the PID controller and discuss the effect of each term on the system performance. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** For a unity feedback system with plant $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$ and a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$:
- (i) Find the steady-state error for a unit step input. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Find the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(4 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to A(a):**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the diagrams in Section 4 of these notes. The standard unity feedback architecture has the controller in the forward path.

**Derivation of Transfer Function (4 marks):**

Starting from the time-domain equation:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Take the Laplace transform of each term:

$$\mathcal{L}\{K_p \, e(t)\} = K_p \, E(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau\right\} = \frac{K_i}{s} E(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right\} = K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

Summing in the Laplace domain:

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

Factoring out $E(s)$:

$$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s\right) E(s)$$

Therefore, the PID transfer function is:

$$\boxed{G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}}$$

**Effect of each term (3 marks):**
- **P term:** Decreases rise time, reduces (but does not eliminate) $e_{ss}$.
- **I term:** Eliminates $e_{ss}$ for step inputs, but may increase overshoot and settling time.
- **D term:** Increases damping, reduces overshoot, improves transient stability. Sensitive to noise.

---

**Solution to A(b):**

**(i) Steady-state error for unit step input (3 marks):**

The open-loop transfer function is:

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \, G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{s(s+4)}$$

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s^2(s+4)}$$

For a unit step input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} = \frac{1/s}{1 + \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s^2(s+4)}}$$

By the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s^2(s+4)}{s^2(s+4) + K_p s + K_i}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{0}{K_i} = 0$$

**Result:** $e_{ss} = 0$ (zero steady-state error for step input). This is guaranteed because the loop has **two integrators** (one from the plant, one from the PI controller), making the system Type-2 effectively for step input handling — though for the **step** input, even Type-1 suffices.

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit ramp input (4 marks):**

For a unit ramp input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$.

$$E(s) = \frac{1/s^2}{1 + \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s^2(s+4)}} = \frac{s^2(s+4)}{s^2 \left[ s^2(s+4) + K_p s + K_i \right]}$$

Wait — correcting the algebra:

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{s^2} \cdot \frac{s^2(s+4)}{s^2(s+4) + K_p s + K_i} = \frac{s+4}{s^2(s+4) + K_p s + K_i}$$

By the Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s(s+4)}{s^2(s+4) + K_p s + K_i}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{0}{K_i} = 0$$

**Result:** $e_{ss} = 0$ for the ramp input as well! This is because the open-loop system with the PI controller effectively has **Type-2** behavior (two integrators: one from plant, one from PI). A Type-2 system has zero steady-state error for both step and ramp inputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using the static error constant:** Velocity error constant $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_{OL}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s^2(s+4)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s(s+4)} = \dfrac{K_i}{0} = \infty$. Since $K_v = \infty$, we have $e_{ss,\text{ramp}} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = 0$. ✓

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`**

**B (a)** Compare the P, PI, and PID controllers in terms of:
- (i) Transfer function and time-domain equation **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Effect on steady-state error, rise time, and overshoot **(4 marks)**

**B (b)** A unity feedback system has plant $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{(s+1)(s+5)}$ and uses a P controller $G_c(s) = K_p$. Determine:
- (i) The range of $K_p$ for system stability using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) The steady-state error for a unit step input when $K_p = 10$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to B(a):**

**(i) Transfer function and time-domain equation (3 marks):**

| **Controller** | **Transfer Function $G_c(s)$** | **Time-Domain Equation $u(t)$** |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | $K_p$ | $K_p \, e(t)$ |
| **PI** | $K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ | $K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$ |
| **PID** | $K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | $K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ |

**(ii) Effect on performance (4 marks):**

- **P Controller:** Reduces $e_{ss}$ but cannot eliminate it. Decreases rise time. Increases overshoot as $K_p$ increases. Can destabilize the system.
- **PI Controller:** Eliminates $e_{ss}$ for step inputs. May increase rise time and overshoot. Slower transient response.
- **PID Controller:** Eliminates $e_{ss}$. D-action reduces overshoot and improves damping. Best transient performance, but sensitive to noise.

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**(i) Range of $K_p$ for stability (4 marks):**

The open-loop transfer function is:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{10 \, K_p}{(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{10 \, K_p}{s^2 + 6s + 5}$$

The characteristic equation of the closed-loop system is:

$$1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0$$

$$s^2 + 6s + 5 + 10 \, K_p = 0$$

**Applying Routh-Hurwitz Criterion** for a second-order polynomial $as^2 + bs + c = 0$:

For stability, we require $a > 0$, $b > 0$, and $c > 0$.

- $a = 1 > 0$ ✓
- $b = 6 > 0$ ✓
- $c = 5 + 10 \, K_p > 0 \implies K_p > -\dfrac{1}{2}$

Since $K_p$ is a gain (must be non-negative for a practical P controller), the **range of stability is**:

$$\boxed{K_p > -\frac{1}{2}, \text{ or in practical terms, } K_p \geq 0}$$

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit step input with $K_p = 10$ (3 marks):**

The system is **Type 0** (no integrator in the open loop). The position error constant is:

$$K_p^{pos} = \lim_{s \to 0} G_{OL}(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10 \cdot 10}{(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{100}{1 \cdot 5} = 20$$

The steady-state error for a unit step input is:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p^{pos}} = \frac{1}{1 + 20} = \frac{1}{21} \approx 0.0476$$

**Result:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{21} \approx 4.76\%$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in feedback:** When drawing the closed-loop block diagram, always mark the summing junction with a "−" sign for the feedback path. An unmarked summing junction loses **1 mark**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the steady-state error formula:** Use $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ for **Type 0** systems with step input — **not** $e_{ss} = 1/K_p$ (which is for Type 1 with ramp input). Mixing these up is a common error.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Routh array construction:** In Routh-Hurwitz problems, students often state stability conditions without showing the Routh array table. The full array earns **full marks**; a bare condition earns **partial credit at best**.
>
> 4. **Miscounting integrator terms:** When determining system Type, count the number of poles at the origin in $G_c(s)G(s)H(s)$ **before** forming the closed loop. The PI controller contributes **one** integrator pole.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in PID transfer function derivation:** The numerator of $G_c(s)$ for a PID controller is $K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i$ (in that order) — not $K_p s^2 + K_i s + K_d$. Order matters!
>
> 6. **Neglecting noise sensitivity of D-action:** When asked about PID disadvantages, always mention that the derivative term **amplifies high-frequency noise** — a frequently omitted but important point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **P Controller:** $G_c(s) = K_p$; reduces but does not eliminate $e_{ss}$; introduces no new poles/zeros.
- **PI Controller:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_p s + K_i}{s}$; **eliminates $e_{ss}$ for step input**; adds one pole at origin and one zero; risk of integral windup.
- **PID Controller:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$; eliminates $e_{ss}$, improves transient damping; **sensitive to measurement noise**.
- **Three Tuning Knobs:** $K_p$ (present), $K_i$ (past), $K_d$ (future).
- **Key Performance Trade-off:** Increasing $K_p$ speeds up response but increases overshoot and may destabilize.
- **Steady-State Error Hierarchy:**
  - P controller on Type-0 plant → finite non-zero $e_{ss}$ for step.
  - PI controller on Type-0 plant → **zero** $e_{ss}$ for step.
  - P controller on Type-1 plant → zero $e_{ss}$ for step.
- **Industrial Dominance:** PID controllers govern **>95% of all industrial control loops** — they are the de facto standard.
- **Noise Sensitivity:** D-action amplifies noise → practical implementations use a **filtered derivative** $G_d(s) = \dfrac{K_d s}{1 + \tau_f s}$ to avoid this.
- **Tuning Methods:** Ziegler-Nichols (ultimate gain + ultimate period) is the most common heuristic for initial PID tuning in industry.
- **Exam Pattern (KTU 2024):** Expect 3-mark conceptual questions on P/PI/PID comparison, and 14-mark analytical questions involving $e_{ss}$ calculations with various input types and stability analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering any PID-related question, structure your response as: **(1) State the transfer function**, **(2) Identify the poles and zeros added**, **(3) Apply the Final Value Theorem or static error constants to find $e_{ss}$**, and **(4) Conclude with the qualitative effect on transient response**. This four-step structure aligns with the KTU Board examiner's marking scheme and guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Compensators: Need for Compensators, Lag and Lead Compensators Using Bode Plots (Only Design Steps)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Compensators: Need for Compensators, Lag and Lead Compensators Using Bode Plots (Only Design Steps)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Compensator?

A **compensator** is an additional control element (usually an electrical network) inserted into the forward path of a feedback control system to modify its open-loop transfer function so that the **closed-loop performance** meets the desired specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A compensator is a controller that modifies the frequency response of a system to achieve the desired steady-state accuracy and transient response without altering the basic plant dynamics in an undesirable way.*

Mathematically, when a compensator $G_c(s)$ is cascaded with the plant $G(s)$, the new open-loop transfer function becomes:

$$G_{new}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G(s)$$

## 1.2 Need for Compensators

Even though the basic plant is fixed (cannot be modified physically), its performance is often inadequate. A compensator becomes necessary due to the following practical reasons:

| S.No | Limitation of Uncompensated System | How Compensator Solves It |
|------|-------------------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Poor steady-state accuracy (high $K_p$ not possible) | Lag compensator increases low-frequency gain |
| 2 | Poor transient response (low damping, high overshoot) | Lead compensator increases phase margin |
| 3 | Low bandwidth (slow response) | Lead compensator extends bandwidth |
| 4 | Instability or marginal stability | Lead/phase-lag improves PM and GM |
| 5 | Sensitivity to parameter variations | Compensator shapes the response robustly |

> [!NOTE]
> **Compensation Rule of Thumb:**
> - Need **steady-state accuracy** $\rightarrow$ Use **LAG compensator**
> - Need **transient improvement** (faster, less overshoot) $\rightarrow$ Use **LEAD compensator**
> - Need **both** $\rightarrow$ Use **LAG-LEAD compensator**

## 1.3 Types of Compensators

The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on **frequency-domain (Bode plot) design** of the following:

1. **Lead Compensator** – Adds positive phase to improve transient response.
2. **Lag Compensator** – Adds attenuation at high frequency to boost low-frequency gain.
3. **Lag-Lead Compensator** – Combines both for simultaneous improvement.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Car Steering System:**
> Imagine driving a car (the **plant**). If the steering is too loose (poor transient response), you add a **"tightening mechanism" (LEAD compensator)** that responds faster to your input. If the car drifts on a slope (poor steady-state accuracy), you add a **"slow corrective tilt" (LAG compensator)** that accumulates small corrections over time to keep it on track. A **LAG-LEAD compensator** does both — like a power-steering system with auto-correction.

## 1.5 The Fundamental Trade-off in Design

In any compensator design, two conflicting requirements must be balanced:

- **Steady-state accuracy** (low-frequency behavior, dictated by error constants $K_p, K_v, K_a$)
- **Transient response** (high-frequency behavior, dictated by phase margin $\phi_m$ and gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$)

> [!WARNING]
> Increasing the gain $K$ to improve steady-state accuracy **decreases phase margin** and may destabilize the system. This is the central problem that compensators solve — they adjust the **shape** of the frequency response, not just the gain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compensator Transfer Functions (Standard Forms)

### A. Lead Compensator

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \alpha \tau s}, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

- $\tau > 0$ : Time constant of the compensator
- $\alpha$ : Lead ratio (attenuation factor)
- $K_c$ : Gain constant (often set to 1 in Bode design)

In frequency domain ($s = j\omega$):

$$G_c(j\omega) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega\tau}{1 + j\alpha\omega\tau}$$

**Pole-Zero Relationship:** Zero is at $z_c = -1/\tau$, Pole is at $p_c = -1/(\alpha\tau)$.
Since $\alpha < 1$, the **zero is to the right of the pole** in the LHP → leads to **phase lead**.

### B. Lag Compensator

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \beta \tau s}, \quad \beta > 1$$

In frequency domain:

$$G_c(j\omega) = K_c \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega\tau}{1 + j\beta\omega\tau}$$

**Pole-Zero Relationship:** Zero at $z_c = -1/\tau$, Pole at $p_c = -1/(\beta\tau)$.
Since $\beta > 1$, the **pole is closer to origin** (right of zero in s-plane closer than zero) → leads to **phase lag** but allows gain boost at low frequency.

### C. Lag-Lead Compensator

$$G_c(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{(1 + \tau_1 s)(1 + \tau_2 s)}{(1 + \beta \tau_1 s)(1 + \tau_2 s / \beta)}$$

where $\tau_1 > \tau_2$ and $\beta > 1$. First section lags, second section leads.

## 2.2 Effect on Bode Plot — Visual Summary

| Parameter | Lead Compensator | Lag Compensator |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Pole-Zero placement | Zero before pole (in frequency) | Pole before zero (in frequency) |
| Phase contribution | **Positive** (phase lead) | **Negative** (phase lag) |
| Magnitude at high $\omega$ | **Increases** slope by $+20$ dB/dec | **Decreases** slope by $-20$ dB/dec |
| $\omega_z$ (zero freq) | $\omega_z = 1/\tau$ | $\omega_z = 1/\tau$ |
| $\omega_p$ (pole freq) | $\omega_p = 1/(\alpha\tau) > \omega_z$ | $\omega_p = 1/(\beta\tau) < \omega_z$ |
| Max phase $\phi_m$ | $\sin\phi_m = (1-\alpha)/(1+\alpha)$ | Small negative |
| Frequency at $\phi_m$ | $\omega_m = 1/(\tau\sqrt{\alpha})$ | $\omega_m = 1/(\tau\sqrt{\beta})$ |
| Effect on $K_v$ | Slight increase | **Major increase** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **only memorization needed** for KTU 2024 exam on this topic. Master these before attempting any design problem.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $\sin\phi_m = \dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}$ | Max phase lead angle | Lead Design |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1-\sin\phi_m}{1+\sin\phi_m}$ | Lead ratio from $\phi_m$ | Lead Design |
| 3 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{1}{\tau\sqrt{\alpha}}$ | Frequency of max phase | Lead Design |
| 4 | $20\log\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha}}$ | Magnitude boost at $\omega_m$ (dB) | Lead Design |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\omega_m\sqrt{\alpha}}$ | Time constant | Lead Design |
| 6 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \alpha\tau s}$ | Lead compensator form | Lead |
| 7 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{1 + \tau s}{1 + \beta\tau s}$ | Lag compensator form | Lag |
| 8 | $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)G_c(s)$ | Velocity error constant | Lag Design |
| 9 | $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)G_c(s)$ | Acceleration error constant | Lag Design |
| 10 | $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)G_c(s)$ | Position error constant | Lag Design |
| 11 | $\text{Gain to be added} = 20\log\dfrac{K_{new}}{K_{old}}$ | dB increase at low freq | Lag Design |
| 12 | $\omega_z = 1/\tau$, $\omega_p = 1/(\beta\tau)$ | Corner frequencies | Lag |
| 13 | $\omega_{gc,new} = \omega_{gc,required}$ | New gain crossover | Both |
| 14 | $PM_{required} = \phi_{specified} + 5°$ to $12°$ | Safety margin | Lead Design |
| 15 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ (unit step, type 0) | Steady-state error | Lag context |

## 2.4 Physical Realization (PD/PI Equivalence)

| Compensator | Continuous Form | Realizable Form | Acts Like |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Lead | $\dfrac{1+\tau s}{1+\alpha\tau s}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{s+1/\tau}{s+1/(\alpha\tau)}$ | **PD controller + first-order filter** |
| Lag | $\dfrac{1+\tau s}{1+\beta\tau s}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{s+1/\tau}{s+1/(\beta\tau)}$ | **PI controller + first-order filter** |

> [!NOTE]
> In **frequency-domain Bode design**, we use the ideal form $G_c(s) = \frac{1+\tau s}{1+\alpha\tau s}$ (with unity DC gain) because Bode plot design is based on **phase** and **magnitude shape**, not absolute gain. The system gain $K$ is adjusted separately.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lead compensator** $\equiv$ **PD controller** — used in **servo motors, robotics, hard disk drives, missile guidance** where **fast, well-damped response** is critical.
- **Lag compensator** $\equiv$ **PI controller** — used in **process control (chemical plants, temperature control, cruise control)** where **eliminating steady-state error** matters more than speed.
- **Lag-Lead** — used in **aerospace, autopilot, satellite tracking** where **both accuracy and speed** are required.
