stability check] TInf --> STABLE{All eigenvalues
have negative real part?} STABLE --> STABLEYES[Yes: x tends to 0] STABLE --> STABLENO[No: unbounded growth] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block Diagram Interpretation:** The pipeline shows that $\Phi(t)$ operates on the *initial state* in parallel with the *forced-response convolution kernel* $\Phi(t-\tau)B$. The feedback arrow from $x(t)$ to $\Phi(t)$ indicates that the *same* transition matrix governs all future evolution — it is a **time-invariant operator**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the State Transition Matrix. List any four of its properties.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The State Transition Matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t}$ is the matrix that maps the initial state $\mathbf{x}(0)$ to the state at time $t$ for the unforced system $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$, i.e., $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi}(t)\mathbf{x}(0)$. **Four properties:** 1. $\mathbf{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$ 2. $\dfrac{d}{dt}\mathbf{\Phi}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}(t)$ 3. $\mathbf{\Phi}(t_1)\mathbf{\Phi}(t_2) = \mathbf{\Phi}(t_1+t_2)$ 4. $\mathbf{\Phi}^{-1}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi}(-t)$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Write the expression for the complete solution of the state equation $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$. Identify the role of $\Phi(t)$ in each term.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$ \mathbf{x}(t) = \underbrace{\mathbf{\Phi}(t)\,\mathbf{x}(0)}_{\text{zero-input (free) response}} + \underbrace{\int_{0}^{t} \mathbf{\Phi}(t-\tau)\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau}_{\text{zero-state (forced) response}} $$ - The **first term** represents how the *initial condition* propagates forward in time via $\Phi(t)$. - The **second term** represents how *past inputs* contribute to the present state through the convolution with $\Phi(t-\tau)B$. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** Compute the State Transition Matrix $\mathbf{\Phi}(t)$ for the system whose system matrix is: $$ \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} $$ using the **Laplace Transform method**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Verify the four fundamental properties of the State Transition Matrix obtained in part (a). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Step-by-step computation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Form the resolvent: $$ s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 2 & s+3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2:** Determinant: $$ \det(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}) = s(s+3) + 2 = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2) $$ **Step 3:** Adjugate and inverse: $$ (s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} \begin{bmatrix} s+3 & 1 \\ -2 & s \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 4:** Partial fractions and inverse Laplace: $$ \mathbf{\Phi}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ **(b) Verification of properties (7 marks):** **Property 1 — $\mathbf{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$:** $$ \mathbf{\Phi}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 2-1 & 1-1 \\ -2+2 & -1+2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{I} \ \checkmark $$ **Property 2 — $\dot{\mathbf{\Phi}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}(t)$:** Differentiate $\Phi(t)$: $$ \dot{\mathbf{\Phi}}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \\ 2e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ Compute $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}(t)$: $$ \mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-t} - e^{-2t} & e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \\ -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} & -e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ Row 1: $[(-2e^{-t}+2e^{-2t}),\ (-e^{-t}+2e^{-2t})]$ Row 2: $[-2(2e^{-t}-e^{-2t}) - 3(-2e^{-t}+2e^{-2t}),\ -2(e^{-t}-e^{-2t}) - 3(-e^{-t}+2e^{-2t})] = [2e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t},\ e^{-t} - 4e^{-2t}]$ These match $\dot{\mathbf{\Phi}}(t)$ ✓ **Property 3 — $\mathbf{\Phi}^{-1}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi}(-t)$:** Substitute $-t$ in $\Phi(t)$ and check that the product with $\Phi(t)$ gives $I$ (granted by structure of exponentials). **Property 4 — $\mathbf{\Phi}(t_1+t_2) = \mathbf{\Phi}(t_1)\mathbf{\Phi}(t_2)$:** Follows from $e^{\mathbf{A}(t_1+t_2)} = e^{\mathbf{A}t_1}e^{\mathbf{A}t_2}$. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** For the system matrix: $$ \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$ compute the State Transition Matrix using the **Cayley–Hamilton theorem**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the initial state is $\mathbf{x}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, find $\mathbf{x}(t)$ for the unforced system. Also comment on stability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Cayley–Hamilton method (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Characteristic equation:** $$ \det(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}) = (s-1)(s-2) = s^2 - 3s + 2 = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Cayley–Hamilton says:** $$ \mathbf{A}^2 - 3\mathbf{A} + 2\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{0} \implies \mathbf{A}^2 = 3\mathbf{A} - 2\mathbf{I} $$ **Step 3 — Express $e^{\mathbf{A}t}$ as polynomial:** $$ e^{\mathbf{A}t} = \alpha_0(t)\mathbf{I} + \alpha_1(t)\mathbf{A} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute eigenvalues $\lambda_1=1$, $\lambda_2=2$:** $$ e^{t} = \alpha_0(t) + \alpha_1(t) $$ $$ e^{2t} = \alpha_0(t) + 2\alpha_1(t) $$ **Step 5 — Solve linear system:** Subtracting: $\alpha_1(t) = e^{2t} - e^{t}$ Substituting: $\alpha_0(t) = 2e^{t} - e^{2t}$ **Step 6 — Final answer:** $$ \mathbf{\Phi}(t) = (2e^{t} - e^{2t})\mathbf{I} + (e^{2t} - e^{t})\mathbf{A} $$ $$ = \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{t} - e^{2t} & 0 \\ 0 & 2e^{t} - e^{2t} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} e^{2t} - e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & 2e^{2t} - 2e^{t} \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \mathbf{\Phi}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ **(b) State evolution and stability (7 marks):** $$ \mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi}(t)\,\mathbf{x}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{2t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} e^{t} \\ e^{2t} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Stability comment:** Eigenvalues of $A$ are $\lambda_1 = 1$ and $\lambda_2 = 2$, both **positive real**. Since the eigenvalues have positive real parts, $e^{t} \to \infty$ and $e^{2t} \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$. Therefore, the system is **unstable** (unbounded growth). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify $\Phi(0) = I$** — Examiners award 1 mark specifically for this check. Always substitute $t=0$. > 2. **Sign errors in partial fractions** — Be very careful when computing $A$ and $B$ coefficients. Always cross-check by recombining. > 3. **Mixing up $\Phi(t-\tau)$ vs $\Phi(\tau-t)$** — The forced response integral uses $\Phi(t-\tau)$, *not* $\Phi(\tau-t)$. This is a 1-mark killer. > 4. **Not simplifying $\mathbf{A}^n$ for $n \geq 3$ in Cayley–Hamilton** — Always reduce higher powers of $A$ using the characteristic equation. > 5. **Stability comment must mention eigenvalues** — Saying "unstable" alone is not enough; cite that the eigenvalues have positive real parts. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** $\mathbf{\Phi}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t}$ is the matrix exponential that maps $\mathbf{x}(0) \to \mathbf{x}(t)$ in the unforced system. - **Core ODE:** $\dot{\mathbf{\Phi}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{\Phi}(t)$ with $\mathbf{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$. - **Laplace Recipe:** $\mathbf{\Phi}(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1}\}$ — the *fastest* exam method. - **Cayley–Hamilton Recipe:** Replace $e^{\mathbf{A}t}$ with $\alpha_0(t)\mathbf{I} + \cdots + \alpha_{n-1}(t)\mathbf{A}^{n-1}$, then solve using eigenvalues. - **Properties to memorise:** $\Phi(0)=I$, $\Phi(t_1)\Phi(t_2)=\Phi(t_1+t_2)$, $\Phi^{-1}(t)=\Phi(-t)$, $\dot{\Phi}=A\Phi$. - **Complete state solution:** $\mathbf{x}(t) = \mathbf{\Phi}(t)\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^t \mathbf{\Phi}(t-\tau)\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau$. - **Stability link:** If all eigenvalues of $A$ have **negative real parts**, then $\Phi(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ — system is asymptotically stable. - **Forced response integral argument order is $(t-\tau)$, not $(\tau-t)$.** - **Diagonal form shortcut:** If $A = P\Lambda P^{-1}$, then $e^{\mathbf{A}t} = P e^{\Lambda t} P^{-1}$ — useful when eigenvalues are distinct. - **KTU favourite marks:** Verification of $\Phi(0)=I$ is awarded **1 dedicated mark** in almost every board question.

topics.title: Controllability and Observability -Kalman's Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT503 - CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Course Code:** PCECT503 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Controllability and Observability -Kalman's Test > - **Prepared as per:** KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned, OBE Framework) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) ### Controllability A linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space system described by $$ \dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t) $$ is said to be **completely state controllable** (or simply **controllable**) if for any initial state $x(0) = x_0$ and any desired final state $x(t_f) = x_f$, there exists an admissible control input $u(t)$ defined on a finite interval $0 \le t \le t_f$ that transfers the state from $x_0$ to $x_f$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kalman's Controllability Test (KTU Board Definition):** > The system is completely state controllable **if and only if** the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C}_M$ has full rank $n$, where $n$ is the order of the system. > $$ > \mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2 B & \cdots & A^{n-1} B \end{bmatrix}_{n \times n} > $$ > The necessary and sufficient condition is: > $$ > \text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n > $$ ### Observability A system is said to be **completely observable** if for any unknown initial state $x(0) = x_0$, there exists a finite time $t_f$ such that the knowledge of the input $u(t)$ and the output $y(t)$ over the interval $0 \le t \le t_f$ is sufficient to uniquely determine $x_0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kalman's Observability Test (KTU Board Definition):** > The system is completely observable **if and only if** the observability matrix $\mathcal{O}_M$ has full rank $n$. > $$ > \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}_{n \times n} > $$ > The necessary and sufficient condition is: > $$ > \text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n > $$ --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) ### The "Car on a Hill" Analogy for Controllability > [!NOTE] > **Imagine driving a manual-transmission car on a foggy mountain road.** > - The *state* is the position and velocity of your car. > - The *control input* is the steering wheel, accelerator, and brake. > - The system is **controllable** if you can drive the car from *any* starting point (top of hill, valley, hairpin bend) to *any* desired destination within a finite time, using only your available controls. > - If one of your wheels is jammed (input is missing), you might be unable to turn left — making the system **not completely controllable**. ### The "Security Camera" Analogy for Observability > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a building with rooms and CCTV cameras.** > - The *state* is the contents of each room. > - The *output* is what the cameras record. > - The system is **observable** if by looking at all the camera footage over some time, the security guard can reconstruct exactly what was happening in every room. > - If a "dead" room (one that no camera can see, and no influence reaches a seen room) exists, the system is **not observable**. ### The Duality Insight Kalman himself showed that **observability of $(A, C)$ is mathematically the dual problem of controllability of $(A^T, C^T)$**. Hence the test for observability mirrors that for controllability, just transposed. --- ## 1.3 Significance for KTU 2024 Examinations - **Marks Weightage:** This topic carries **12–14 marks** typically in Module 4. - **Board Pattern:** Students are usually asked to **construct** the controllability/observability matrix and **check rank** using determinant for 2nd/3rd order systems. - **Critical Pitfall:** Many students confuse controllability and observability matrices — the order of multiplications and the transposed structure are frequently interchanged in scripts. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ### Why is $n$ sub-blocks sufficient? Kalman proved that if $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) < n$, then no higher power $A^k B$ (for $k \ge n$) can increase the rank. This is due to the **Cayley–Hamilton Theorem**, which states that $A^n$ can be expressed as a linear combination of lower powers of $A$ ($I, A, A^2, \ldots, A^{n-1}$). Therefore, all reachable directions from the input are spanned by at most $n$ matrices: $B, AB, \ldots, A^{n-1}B$. ### Structural Steps of Kalman's Test **For Controllability:** 1. Identify the system matrices $A$ ($n \times n$) and $B$ ($n \times p$). 2. Sequentially compute $B$, then $AB$, then $A^2B$, ..., up to $A^{n-1}B$. 3. Stack these as columns to form the $n \times (np)$ matrix $\mathcal{C}_M = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^2B \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1}B]$. 4. Compute $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M)$. 5. **Conclusion:** If $\text{rank} = n$, the system is completely state controllable. If $\text{rank} < n$, the system is **uncontrollable** (rank deficiency = number of uncontrollable states). **For Observability:** 1. Identify the system matrices $A$ ($n \times n$) and $C$ ($q \times n$). 2. Sequentially compute $C$, then $CA$, then $CA^2$, ..., up to $CA^{n-1}$. 3. Stack these as rows to form the $(nq) \times n$ matrix $\mathcal{O}_M = [C^T \;\; (CA)^T \;\; (CA^2)^T \;\; \cdots \;\; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$. 4. Compute $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M)$. 5. **Conclusion:** If $\text{rank} = n$, the system is completely observable. If $\text{rank} < n$, the system is **unobservable**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Independence of Similarity Transformations:** > The controllability and observability properties are *invariant* under a non-singular similarity transformation $\bar{A} = T^{-1} A T$, $\bar{B} = T^{-1} B$, $\bar{C} = C T$. The rank of the controllability/observability matrix remains unchanged. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes / Units | |---|---------|--------------------|---------------| | 1 | Controllability Matrix | $\mathcal{C}_M = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^2B \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1}B]$ | $n \times np$ matrix | | 2 | Controllability Condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = n$ | Equivalent to $\det(\mathcal{C}_M) \neq 0$ for square matrix | | 3 | Observability Matrix | $\mathcal{O}_M = [C^T \;\; (CA)^T \;\; \cdots \;\; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ | $nq \times n$ matrix | | 4 | Observability Condition | $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = n$ | Equivalent to $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) \neq 0$ for square matrix | | 5 | Duality Relation | $(A, C)$ observable $\iff$ $(A^T, C^T)$ controllable | Structural duality | | 6 | POPOV-Belevitch-Hautus (PBH) — Alternative | $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \;\; B] = n$ for all eigenvalues $\lambda$ | Equivalent to Kalman's test | | 7 | Transfer Function Relation | No pole-zero cancellation in $\dfrac{C \cdot \text{adj}(sI-A) \cdot B}{\det(sI-A)}$ | If cancellation exists $\Rightarrow$ loss of controllability/observability | | 8 | Number of Uncontrollable States | $n - \text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M)$ | Measure of controllability loss | | 9 | Number of Unobservable States | $n - \text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M)$ | Measure of observability loss | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (Aircraft Autopilot Design):** Before designing a controller, engineers must verify that the *state variables* (pitch, roll, yaw rates) are *controllable* through the available actuators (ailerons, elevator, rudder). - **Power Systems:** A state representing rotor angle may be unobservable from terminal voltage measurements alone — Kalman test reveals this and dictates whether additional sensors are required. - **Robotics:** Joint torques must be able to drive all joint positions/velocities (controllability); encoders must allow full pose reconstruction (observability). - **Process Control (KTU Industrial Context):** In distillation columns, inferential control depends on estimating unmeasured compositions (temperatures) from secondary measurements (reflux, reboiler duty) — observability is paramount. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete KTU-Style Solved Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]** > For the LTI system described below, check controllability and observability using Kalman's test: > $$ > \dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x > $$ ### Step 1 — Identify the System Matrices $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ Here $n = 3$, so we need matrices up to $A^2 B$ and $CA^2$. --- ### Step 2 — Test for Controllability (Kalman's Test) #### 2a. Compute $AB$ $$ AB = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** Row-wise dot product of $A$ with $B$ — first row: $(0)(0)+(1)(0)+(0)(1)=0$, second row: $0+0+1=1$, third row: $-6(0)-11(0)-6(1)=-6$. #### 2b. Compute $A^2 B = A(AB)$ $$ A^2 B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ -6 \end{bmatrix} $$ Performing row-wise multiplication: $$ A^2 B = \begin{bmatrix} (0)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(-6) \\ (0)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(-6) \\ (-6)(0) + (-11)(1) + (-6)(-6) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -6 \\ -11 + 36 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -6 \\ 25 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** Row 3 calculation: $-11(1) = -11$ and $-6(-6) = +36$, so total $= -11+36 = 25$. #### 2c. Form the Controllability Matrix $$ \mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \\ 1 & -6 & 25 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### 2d. Compute $\det(\mathcal{C}_M)$ Expanding along the first column (only one non-zero entry at row 3): $$ \det(\mathcal{C}_M) = (1) \cdot (-1)^{3+1} \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & -6 \end{vmatrix} = 1 \cdot (0 \cdot -6 - 1 \cdot 1) = -1 $$ #### 2e. Controllability Conclusion $$ \det(\mathcal{C}_M) = -1 \neq 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = 3 = n $$ $$ \boxed{\text{The system is COMPLETELY STATE CONTROLLABLE.}} $$ --- ### Step 3 — Test for Observability (Kalman's Test) #### 3a. Compute $CA$ $$ CA = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** This picks out the first row of $A$, since $C = [1 \; 0 \; 0]$. #### 3b. Compute $CA^2 = (CA)A$ $$ CA^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Logic:** Row-wise — $(0)(0)+(1)(0)+(0)(-6)=0$, $(0)(1)+(1)(0)+(0)(-11)=0$, $(0)(0)+(1)(1)+(0)(-6)=1$. #### 3c. Form the Observability Matrix $$ \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_3 $$ #### 3d. Compute $\det(\mathcal{O}_M)$ $$ \det(\mathcal{O}_M) = \det(I_3) = 1 \neq 0 $$ #### 3e. Observability Conclusion $$ \det(\mathcal{O}_M) = 1 \neq 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{rank}(\mathcal{O}_M) = 3 = n $$ $$ \boxed{\text{The system is COMPLETELY OBSERVABLE.}} $$ --- ## 3.2 A Counter-Example (When Kalman's Test Fails) > [!NOTE] > **Consider the system:** > $$ > A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} > $$ **Controllability Check:** $$ AB = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}_M) = 1 - 2 = -1 \neq 0 $$ **Conclusion: Controllable.** **Observability Check:** $$ CA = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{rank} = 1 < 2 $$ **Conclusion: NOT completely observable** — determinant is 0 because rows are linearly dependent. > [!IMPORTANT] > This demonstrates that a system can be **controllable but not observable** (or vice versa). The two are *independent* properties. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kalman's Test ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: State-Space Model
A, B, C, D matrices] --> B{Identify system order n} B --> C[Controllability Path] B --> D[Observability Path] C --> C1[Compute B] C1 --> C2[Compute A times B] C2 --> C3[Compute A squared times B] C3 --> C4[Continue up to A power n-1 times B] C4 --> C5[Stack as columns: C_M] C5 --> C6[Compute rank or determinant] C6 --> C7{rank = n?} C7 -->|Yes| C8[COMPLETELY CONTROLLABLE] C7 -->|No| C9[Uncontrollable
Deficiency = n - rank] D --> D1[Compute C] D1 --> D2[Compute C times A] D2 --> D3[Compute C times A squared] D3 --> D4[Continue up to C times A power n-1] D4 --> D5[Stack as rows: O_M] D5 --> D6[Compute rank or determinant] D6 --> D7{rank = n?} D7 -->|Yes| D8[COMPLETELY OBSERVABLE] D7 -->|No| D9[Unobservable
Deficiency = n - rank] C8 --> E[Final Verdict: System Properties] C9 --> E D8 --> E D9 --> E E --> F1[Controllable + Observable
= Stabilizable & Detectable] E --> F2[Uncontrollable but Observable
= Cannot design full state feedback] E --> F3[Controllable but Unobservable
= Cannot design Luenberger observer] E --> F4[Neither Controllable nor Observable
= System has hidden dynamics] ``` ## 4.2 State-Space Model Block Diagram (Reference Context) ```mermaid flowchart LR U["u(t)
Control Input"] --> SUM[("+")] B["B Matrix
n x p"] --> SUM SUM --> X["x dot(t)
State Derivative"] X --> INT["Integrator
1 over s"] INT --> XSTATE["x(t)
State Vector"] XSTATE --> A["A Matrix
n x n"] A --> SUM XSTATE --> C["C Matrix
q x n"] C --> Y["y(t)
Measured Output"] XSTATE --> OUT["x(t)
Internal States"] U2["u(t)"] --> D["D Matrix
q x p"] D --> SUM2[("+")] SUM2 --> Y ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree Summary of Kalman's Test Outcomes ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Apply Kalman's Test
to System] --> S1{Rank of C_M = n?} S1 -->|No| S2[Uncontrollable] S1 -->|Yes| S3[Controllable] S3 --> S4{Rank of O_M = n?} S4 -->|No| S5[Unobservable] S4 -->|Yes| S6[Fully Controllable and Observable] S2 --> S7[Check Observability] S5 --> S8[Check Controllability] S7 --> S4 S8 --> S1 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define state controllability. State the condition for complete state controllability using Kalman's test.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - *Definition (2 marks):* A system is said to be completely state controllable if it is possible to transfer the system from any initial state $x(t_0)$ to any desired state $x(t_f)$ in a finite time interval by a suitable control vector $u(t)$. - *Kalman's Condition (1 mark):* The system is completely state controllable if and only if the controllability matrix $$ \mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix} $$ has rank $n$ (full rank), where $n$ is the order of the system. --- ### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Differentiate between controllability and observability. State the duality relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - *Controllability (1 mark):* Deals with the ability of the input $u(t)$ to drive the system from any initial state to any desired state. It involves the pair $(A, B)$. - *Observability (1 mark):* Deals with the ability to reconstruct the initial state $x(0)$ from the knowledge of the output $y(t)$ and input $u(t)$ over a finite interval. It involves the pair $(A, C)$. - *Duality (1 mark):* The system $(A, B, C)$ is completely observable if and only if its dual system $(A^T, C^T, B^T)$ is completely controllable. The observability matrix $\mathcal{O}_M$ is the transpose of the controllability matrix of the dual system. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern ### Question B-A. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** For the system: $$ \dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} x $$ Check controllability and observability using Kalman's test. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of controllability and observability in modern control system design. What is meant by pole-zero cancellation in transfer function and its link with controllability/observability? --- #### Solution to B-A (a) **Step 1 — Identify Matrices** $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Here $n = 2$, so we need $B$, $AB$, $C$, and $CA$. **Step 2 — Controllability** Compute $AB$: $$ AB = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 + 1 \\ 0 - 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Form $\mathcal{C}_M$: $$ \mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Compute determinant: $$ \det(\mathcal{C}_M) = (1)(-2) - (0)(1) = -2 \neq 0 $$ **Conclusion:** $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}_M) = 2 = n$ → **System is completely controllable.** **Step 3 — Observability** Compute $CA$: $$ CA = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Form $\mathcal{O}_M$: $$ \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Compute determinant: $$ \det(\mathcal{O}_M) = (1)(-1) - (1)(-1) = -1 + 1 = 0 $$ **Conclusion:** $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) = 0$, $\text{rank} = 1 < 2$ → **System is NOT completely observable.** --- #### Solution to B-A (b) **Significance of Controllability (3 Marks):** 1. **Prerequisite for Pole Placement:** A system must be completely controllable to arbitrarily assign all closed-loop poles using state feedback $u = -Kx$. If uncontrollable, some poles are *frozen* and cannot be moved. 2. **Optimal Control Validity:** The Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) design requires controllability of $(A, B)$ to guarantee a finite, meaningful cost. 3. **Stabilizability Requirement:** Even if a system is not fully controllable, stability of the *uncontrollable* modes is sufficient for stabilizability (weaker condition). **Significance of Observability (2 Marks):** 1. **Observer Design:** A Luenberger observer $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$ requires $(A, C)$ to be observable to place observer poles arbitrarily. 2. **State Estimation:** Kalman filter convergence depends on detectability (all unobservable modes stable). **Pole-Zero Cancellation & Linkage (2 Marks):** When the transfer function $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$ is derived from a state-space model, the characteristic polynomial $\det(sI - A)$ may share common factors with the numerator polynomial $C \cdot \text{adj}(sI - A) \cdot B$. Such **pole-zero cancellation** in the transfer function indicates that the system is either: - Not completely state controllable, OR - Not completely observable, OR - Both. This is because the cancelled poles correspond to modes hidden from either the input path (loss of controllability) or the output path (loss of observability). Hence, **transfer function representation can never reveal all internal dynamics of a system** — the state-space model is superior in exposing controllability/observability properties. --- ### Question B-B. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** For the system: $$ \dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -11 & -6 \end{bmatrix} x + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} x $$ Determine whether the system is completely controllable and completely observable using Kalman's test. **(b) [7 Marks]** If a system has $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, verify the duality principle by showing that observability of $(A, C)$ is equivalent to controllability of $(A^T, C^T)$. --- #### Solution to B-B (a) > [!NOTE] > This is the same example solved in **Section 3.1** above. Students are expected to reproduce: > - Controllability matrix: $\mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \\ 1 & -6 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$ with $\det = -1 \neq 0$ → **Controllable**. > - Observability matrix: $\mathcal{O}_M = I_3$ with $\det = 1 \neq 0$ → **Observable**. **Full answer (for reference):** Controllability: - $B = [0, 0, 1]^T$, $AB = [0, 1, -6]^T$, $A^2B = [1, -6, 25]^T$ - $\mathcal{C}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & -6 \\ 1 & -6 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$, $\det(\mathcal{C}_M) = -1 \neq 0$ - **System is completely controllable.** Observability: - $C = [1, 0, 0]$, $CA = [0, 1, 0]$, $CA^2 = [0, 0, 1]$ - $\mathcal{O}_M = I_3$, $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) = 1 \neq 0$ - **System is completely observable.** --- #### Solution to B-B (b) **Step 1 — Test Observability of $(A, C)$** Given: $$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ Compute $CA$: $$ CA = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Form observability matrix: $$ \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2 $$ $\det(\mathcal{O}_M) = 1 \neq 0$ → **System $(A, C)$ is completely observable.** **Step 2 — Test Controllability of Dual System $(A^T, C^T)$** $$ A^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ (This is equivalent to: $A_d = A^T$, $B_d = C^T$.) Compute $A^T C^T$: $$ A^T C^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 1 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Form controllability matrix of the dual: $$ \mathcal{C}_M^{dual} = \begin{bmatrix} C^T & A^T C^T \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_2 $$ $\det(\mathcal{C}_M^{dual}) = 1 \neq 0$ → **Dual system is completely controllable.** **Step 3 — Verify Duality** We have: $$ \mathcal{O}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = (\mathcal{C}_M^{dual})^T $$ Both matrices are identical, and both have full rank 2. **Hence, observability of $(A, C)$ is verified to be equivalent to controllability of $(A^T, C^T)$ — Kalman's duality theorem is confirmed.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Transposition Error in Observability Matrix:** Students often write $\mathcal{O}_M = [C \;\; CA \;\; CA^2]^T$ but then accidentally form a column matrix. The observability matrix is the *transpose* of the stacked rows — i.e., it is built as rows $C, CA, CA^2, \ldots$ stacked vertically. Just remember: **observability is built by stacking $C^k$ as ROWS, not columns.** > 2. **Forgetting the Power $A^{n-1}$:** For a 3rd-order system, students sometimes stop at $A^1B$ or $CA$. The correct termination is at $n-1$, which is $A^2B$ and $CA^2$ for $n=3$. > 3. **Confusing $C^TA^T$ with $CA$:** When applying duality, students transpose the *entire product* as $(CA)^T = A^TC^T$, not just $C^T A^T$ in that order (though for square transpose products, the order reverses). Be careful with the algebra. > 4. **Skipping the Determinant Step:** Just stating "the matrix has full rank" without computing $\det \neq 0$ may lose 1 mark. Always show the determinant calculation. > 5. **Mixing Up the $D$ Matrix:** For SISO systems, $D$ does not affect controllability or observability — it only adds a direct feed-through term. Do not include it in the matrices. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - [ ] **Controllability** relates to the input $u(t)$ driving the state; **Observability** relates to the output $y(t)$ revealing the state. - [ ] **Controllability matrix** has $n$ block columns: $\mathcal{C}_M = [B, AB, A^2B, \ldots, A^{n-1}B]$. - [ ] **Observability matrix** has $n$ block rows: $\mathcal{O}_M = [C^T, (CA)^T, (CA^2)^T, \ldots, (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$. - [ ] Both matrices are **square $(n \times n)$** only when $p = q = 1$ (SISO); for MIMO, use the rank test. - [ ] Full rank = $n$ implies complete controllability/observability. - [ ] Rank deficiency indicates number of uncontrollable/unobservable states. - [ ] **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem** guarantees that $A^n$ and higher are linear combinations of $A^0, A^1, \ldots, A^{n-1}$ — hence $n$ sub-blocks suffice. - [ ] **Duality:** $(A, C)$ observable $\iff$ $(A^T, C^T)$ controllable. - [ ] **Pole-zero cancellation in transfer function** $\Rightarrow$ loss of controllability OR observability (or both). - [ ] **Invariance:** Controllability and observability are invariant under non-singular similarity transformations. - [ ] **PBH Test (Alternative):** $\text{rank}[\lambda I - A \;\; B] = n$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda$ of $A$ (controllability); similarly for observability. - [ ] **Stabilizability:** If uncontrollable modes are stable → stabilizable. - [ ] **Detectability:** If unobservable modes are stable → detectable. - [ ] For **2nd-order systems**: matrix is $2 \times 2$ — use simple $ad - bc$ for determinant. - [ ] For **3rd-order systems**: matrix is $3 \times 3$ — expand along the row/column with most zeros. - [ ] Always **state the conclusion clearly**: "Since $\det \neq 0$, the system is completely state controllable (observable)." - [ ] Kalman Test is the **most commonly asked** 14-mark question in Module 4 of KTU Control Systems (PCECT503) under the 2024 Scheme.
